• Close Monitoring: Persons in quarantine are closely monitored to ensure that those who become ill are efficiently separated from the well.
• Early Access to Interventions: Those in quarantine should be among the first to receive disease-prevention interventions.

Key Outcome: Adherence to these principles should prevent an increase in risk for acquiring disease while in quarantine.
quarantine,disease risk,monitoring,interventions,pandemic
Understanding Community Quarantine During a Pandemic
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Overview: Community-wide quarantine is one of several actions that may be considered during an influenza pandemic. It is not the only measure, and less drastic options may suffice depending on the situation.

Key Considerations:
1. **Active...
Overview: Community-wide quarantine is one of several actions that may be considered during an influenza pandemic. It is not the only measure, and less drastic options may suffice depending on the situation.

Key Considerations:
1. Active Monitoring: When most cases are imported or have clear epidemiologic linkages, active monitoring without activity restrictions may be adequate.
2. Voluntary Quarantine: If the outbreak indicates a need for stronger measures, jurisdictions can adopt voluntary quarantine and reserve compulsory measures for extreme situations.
3. Social Distancing Measures: When large numbers of cases with no identifiable links occur, community-level interventions such as snow days (school closures) may be preferred over full quarantine.
4. Quarantine Use: Wider use of quarantine is generally reserved for when all other control measures are believed to be ineffective.

Decision-Making Process:
• Officials must continuously assess the outbreak and evaluate the effectiveness of existing control measures.
• Decisions should be made based on limited information initially, with adjustments as more data becomes available.
quarantine,pandemic,community,public health,epidemiology
Understanding the Definition of Quarantine
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Definition: The term “quarantine” is often narrowly defined as legally mandated separation of well persons exposed to a communicable disease from those who are not.

Broader Clinical/Programmatic Use: In practice, quarantine may include all...
Definition: The term “quarantine” is often narrowly defined as legally mandated separation of well persons exposed to a communicable disease from those who are not.

Broader Clinical/Programmatic Use: In practice, quarantine may include all interventions—both mandatory and voluntary—that restrict the activities of persons exposed to a communicable disease.

Key Points:
• Voluntary Measures: Even when restrictions are voluntary, individuals under monitoring with activity limitations are considered to be in quarantine.
• Jurisdictional Variations: The precise legal definition may vary by jurisdiction but is not always necessary for effective implementation.
quarantine,definition,voluntary,mandatory,pandemic

Tornado Risk Assessment (12) -

Understanding Tornado Hazard Subtypes and Their Impact on Risk Calculations
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
The tornado hazard model is complex and involves subtype distinctions that are processed separately before being combined into the overall tornado hazard. These subtypes are based on the severity of the tornado event. As a result of this sub-hazard...
The tornado hazard model is complex and involves subtype distinctions that are processed separately before being combined into the overall tornado hazard. These subtypes are based on the severity of the tornado event. As a result of this sub-hazard approach, the published frequency and Historic Loss Ratio values are surrogates back calculated from the final Expected Annual Loss values. This is one of the few hazards in this analysis that has Census tract-level Historic Loss Ratios.
tornado hazard, subtype distinctions, historic loss ratio, expected annual loss, census tract-level data
Understanding Tornado Hazards and Their Impact on Risk Assessment
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Overview: This procedure outlines how tornado hazards are modeled and assessed within the National Risk Index 2025.

### Key Points:
• A tornado is defined as a narrow, violently rotating column of air that extends from the base of a...
Overview: This procedure outlines how tornado hazards are modeled and assessed within the National Risk Index 2025.

### Key Points:
• A tornado is defined as a narrow, violently rotating column of air that extends from the base of a thunderstorm to the ground. It becomes visible only if it forms a condensation funnel made up of water droplets, dust, and debris.
• The tornado hazard model involves subtype distinctions, which are processed separately before being combined into an overall tornado hazard assessment.
• These subtypes are based on the severity of the tornado event.

### Data Sources:
• Severe Weather Database Files: Provided by the Storm Prediction Center, these files contain historical records of tornado events. They are used to establish three subtypes, calculate the historical occurrence of each subtype, and determine frequency and exposure components.
• SHELDUS (Storm Hazard and Exposure Loss Data for the United States): This dataset is used for historical losses, which help build the Historic Loss Ratio.

### Risk Calculation:
• The tornado hazard model calculates risk across three consequence types:
1. Building: Assessing damage to structures.
2. Population: Evaluating impact on human life and safety.
3. Agriculture: Measuring effects on crops, livestock, and farmland.

### Additional Considerations:
• For a detailed summary of the Hazard Data Characteristics, refer to Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison in the National Risk Index Technical Documentation.
tornado, risk assessment, hazard modeling, severe weather database, SHELDUS, building damage, population impact, agricultural loss
Calculate Annualized Frequency of Tornado Sub-Type Events in a Fishnet Grid Cell
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the annualized frequency of tornado sub-type events within a fishnet grid cell, follow these steps:

1. Identify the number of distinct tornado path polygons that intersect a 49-by-49 km fishnet grid cell.
2. Apply a **tornado...
To calculate the annualized frequency of tornado sub-type events within a fishnet grid cell, follow these steps:

1. Identify the number of distinct tornado path polygons that intersect a 49-by-49 km fishnet grid cell.
2. Apply a tornado sub-type scaling factor to adjust for specific tornado categories (e.g., F0/1 or F-2/3/4/5).
3. Divide the result by the period of record for the tornado sub-type:
- 38.0 years for F0/1 tornadoes
- 74.0 years for F-2/3/4/5 tornadoes
4. This gives you the annualized frequency (in events per year) for that grid cell.

Notes:
• Ensure the scaling factor is appropriate for the sub-type of tornado being analyzed.
• Use accurate historical data to determine the number of intersecting tornado path polygons.
annualized frequency,tornado sub-type,fishnet grid cell,scaling factor,period of record
Adjust for Statistical Anomalies in Tornado Frequency Rates
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To adjust for statistical anomalies in Tornado sub-type annualized frequency rates, follow these steps:

1. Identify if a Census tract or county has experienced more high-magnitude Tornado events than low-magnitude events.
2. If...
To adjust for statistical anomalies in Tornado sub-type annualized frequency rates, follow these steps:

1. Identify if a Census tract or county has experienced more high-magnitude Tornado events than low-magnitude events.
2. If the lower magnitude sub-type annualized frequency rate is less than the next highest magnitude sub-type annualized frequency rate, scale it up to match the higher rate. For example:
- If an EF-scale 2 and 3 annualized frequency rate for a county is $1\times10^{-6}$ and its EF-scale 4 and 5 has a higher annualized frequency rate of $5\times10^{-6}$, the EF-scale 2 and 3 rate is set to $5\times10^{-6}$.
3. If there is a gap in sub-types, insert the missing sub-type and assign it the annualized frequency rate of the higher magnitude sub-type.

These adjustments ensure that, as the sub-type magnitude increases, the annualized frequency rate is less than or equal to the previous sub-type rate.
statistical anomalies,tornado risk adjustment,natural disaster risk assessment
Calculate Annualized Frequency for Census Tract or County Using Average Damage Area
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the annualized frequency for a Census tract or county, follow these steps:

1. Determine the Tornado sub-type annualized frequency rate (in annualized frequency per square kilometer).
2. Calculate the total area of the...
To calculate the annualized frequency for a Census tract or county, follow these steps:

1. Determine the Tornado sub-type annualized frequency rate (in annualized frequency per square kilometer).
2. Calculate the total area of the Census tract or county (in square kilometers).
3. If the Census tract or county area is less than the average damage area for the Tornado sub-type, use the average damage area instead of the total area.
4. Use the following formula:

$$
\text{Annualized frequency} = \text{Tornado sub-type annualized frequency rate} \times
\begin{cases}
\text{Total area of the Census tract or county}, & \text{if it is greater than or equal to the average damage area}\\
\text{Average damage area for the Tornado sub-type}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This ensures that exposure assumptions are consistent with annualized frequency calculations.
average damage area,annualized frequency calculation,natural disaster risk assessment
Calculate National Scaling Factor for Tornado Sub-Types
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure to Calculate National Scaling Factor for Tornado Sub-Types:

1. Determine Event Count:
- Identify the count of distinct tornado events of a specific sub-type that have occurred in the United States.
- Example: For EF-Scale 0...
Procedure to Calculate National Scaling Factor for Tornado Sub-Types:

1. Determine Event Count:
- Identify the count of distinct tornado events of a specific sub-type that have occurred in the United States.
- Example: For EF-Scale 0 and 1, the event count is 38,524.

2. Calculate Fishnet Intersect Count:
- Determine the summed total count of all 80-km buffered tornado-fishnet grid cell intersections for a specific sub-type in the United States.
- Example: For EF-Scale 0 and 1, the intersect count is 622,232.

3. Compute National Scaling Factor:
- Divide the event count by the fishnet intersect count to calculate the national scaling factor for each tornado sub-type.
- Example: For EF-Scale 0 and 1, the scaling factor is 0.06191 (calculated as 38,524 / 622,232).

4. Apply Minimum Scaling Factor:
- For fishnet grid cells that do not intersect a historical tornado path, apply a minimum scaling factor of 1/932, which is approximately 0.00107.
- This applies to grid cells that intersect one or more United States counties but have no historical tornado events.

5. Annualized Frequency Calculation:
- Multiply the national scaling factor by the event count for each grid cell sub-type and divide by the period of record (e.g., number of years in the dataset) to calculate a grid cell sub-type annualized frequency.

6. Adjust for Grid Cell Area:
- Divide the annualized frequency by the representative area associated with the grid cell to produce a sub-type annualized frequency rate per square kilometer.
- The fishnet grid cells are defined as 49-by-49-km, which is 2,401 square kilometers in size.

7. Account for Land Area Variations:
- Adjust the area associated with the fishnet grid cell to account for variations such as coastal areas (significant water coverage) or border regions (significant foreign land coverage).
- Define the area as the average of the fishnet grid size and the actual United States land area covered by the grid cell.

Example Table:
|_Tornado Sub-Type_|_National Tornado_<br>_Event Count_|_National Fishnet_<br>_Intersect Count_|_National Scaling Factor_|
|---|---|---|---|
|EF-Scale 0 and 1|38,524|622,232|0.06191|
|EF-Scale 2 and 3|12,352|213,015|0.05799|
|EF-Scale 4 and 5|646|13,189|0.04898|
|No historical Tornado events|1|932|0.00107|
national scaling factor, tornado sub-type, fishnet grid cell, event count, intersect count, annualized frequency, land area adjustment
Calculate Sub-Type Expected Annual Loss for Tornadoes
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the sub-type Expected Annual Loss for tornadoes, follow these steps:

1. Multiply the annualized frequency values for each Tornado sub-type by their respective sub-type exposure.
2. Multiply the result from step 1 by the...
To calculate the sub-type Expected Annual Loss for tornadoes, follow these steps:

1. Multiply the annualized frequency values for each Tornado sub-type by their respective sub-type exposure.
2. Multiply the result from step 1 by the Historic Loss Ratio for that sub-type.
3. The final value obtained is the sub-type Expected Annual Loss.

> Note: Annualized frequency values are calculated as the sum of all sub-type frequencies at the Census tract and county levels, as described in Equation 122.

For areas where the frequency of all sub-types is zero:
• Use the Minimum Annual Frequency value provided in the dataset.
tornado, expected annual loss, risk index, frequency aggregation, natural disaster
Calculate Tornado Historic Loss Ratio for a Location
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Determine Hazard Exposure: Identify the percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to tornado occurrences.

2. Use SHELDUS Data: Utilize data from SHELDUS (Storm Hazard and Event Loss Database) at...
Procedure:

1. Determine Hazard Exposure: Identify the percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to tornado occurrences.

2. Use SHELDUS Data: Utilize data from SHELDUS (Storm Hazard and Event Loss Database) at the county level, which provides historical loss records for tornado events from 1996 through 2019.

3. Match Tornado Events: Match SHELDUS loss records to specific tornado events using unique identifiers and additional information such as event timestamps, EF-scale, length and width of the tornado path, begin and end coordinates, and full tornado event loss data from the National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) Storm Events Database.

4. Apply Fuzzy Logic Matching: Use a fuzzy logic approach to map historical tornado paths to their county-specific loss in SHELDUS using fields held in common between the spatial source data and NCEI data, such as event timestamps, EF-scale, physical dimensions, and geographic coordinates.

5. Iterate Matching Process: Repeat the matching process with less stringent requirements for matching if necessary to ensure all tornado events are accurately matched.

6. Manual Inspection of High-Impact Tornadoes: For tornadoes with an EF-scale of 4 or 5, manually inspect records to ensure that loss is appropriately matched to the correct path due to their rarity and potential impact on multiple counties.

7. Compare Loss Data: Compare aggregated total loss from SHELDUS (from multiple county loss records attributed to the same tornado event) with the loss reported for the tornado path in the source data (from the Storm Prediction Center).

8. Calculate Loss Ratios per Basis: For each matched tornado event occurring in a county, calculate the loss ratio per basis using the formula:

$$\text{Loss Ratio per Basis} = \frac{\text{SHELDUS-recorded loss}}{\text{consequence value exposed to the Tornado path}}$$

9. Aggregate Data: Use the calculated loss ratios and aggregated data to determine the overall consequence-specific historic loss ratios for tornadoes.

10. Apply to Expected Annual Loss Calculations: Use sub-type specific historic loss ratio values in tornado expected annual loss calculations.
tornado,historic loss ratio,SHELDUS,NCEI,EF-scale,fuzzy logic matching,loss data,expected annual loss
Determine Tornado Hazard Characteristics for Risk Assessment
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify hazard type: Confirm the hazard is a tornado.
2. Define consequence types: Include population, building, and agriculture impacts.
3. Source annualized frequency data:
- Use NOAA's SPC Severe Weather...
Procedure:

1. Identify hazard type: Confirm the hazard is a tornado.
2. Define consequence types: Include population, building, and agriculture impacts.
3. Source annualized frequency data:
- Use NOAA's SPC Severe Weather Database.
4. Set period of record for annualized frequency: 1986-2023.
5. Establish hazard occurrence basis: Use distinct events as the basis for tornado occurrences.
6. Determine exposure extent data source:
- Average impact area per magnitude sub-type:
• 0.8 km² for EF-Scale 0 and 1
• 13 km² for EF-Scale 2 and 3
• 79 km² for EF-Scale 4 and 5.
7. Define exposure type: Apply average density to the average area of hazard impact per magnitude sub-type.
8. Source historic loss ratio data: Use SHELDUS (Storm Data and Insurance Information System).
9. Set period of record for historic loss ratio: 1996-2019.
10. Apply loss allocation date expansion: Yes, with a maximum of 1 day.
11. Set loss aggregation: No.
12. Implement zero-loss record padding:
• Yes (for EF-Scale 0 and 1)
• Yes (for EF-Scale 2 and 3)
• No (for EF-Scale 4 and 5).
13. Set period of record for zero-loss record padding: 1986-2023.
14. Configure Bayesian settings for agriculture:
- County: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No
- 196KM Fishnet: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No
- National Risk Index Historic Loss Ratio Regions: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No
- National: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No.
15. Configure Bayesian settings for building:
- County: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No
- 196KM Fishnet: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No
- National Risk Index Historic Loss Ratio Regions: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No
- National: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No.
16. Configure Bayesian settings for population:
- County: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No
- 196KM Fishnet: Filter: N/A; Set Usage Threshold: N/A; SHELDUS back testing applied: No; Smoothing applied: No.
tornado,hazard characteristics,risk assessment,NOAA,SPC,EF-Scale,SHELDUS,Bayesian settings
Interpret Expected Annual Loss Score for a Community
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To interpret the Expected Annual Loss Score for a community:

1. Understand that the score represents the community’s national percentile ranking relative to all communities at the same level (e.g., Census tract or county).
2. A higher score...
To interpret the Expected Annual Loss Score for a community:

1. Understand that the score represents the community’s national percentile ranking relative to all communities at the same level (e.g., Census tract or county).
2. A higher score indicates a higher risk of tornado-related losses compared to other communities.
3. Use this score in conjunction with Expected Annual Loss values and loss rates for informed decision-making regarding risk mitigation, insurance, and resource allocation.

This score is computed based on the data provided in the US National Risk Index 2025 and is used to compare communities at a national level.
expected annual loss score,tornado risk assessment,natural disaster
Interpret Total Expected Annual Loss by County for Tornado Occurrences
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To interpret the Total Expected Annual Loss by county for tornado occurrences:

1. Refer to Figure 101: Total Expected Annual Loss by County to Tornado from the US National Risk Index 2025.
2. Analyze the data presented in the figure,...
To interpret the Total Expected Annual Loss by county for tornado occurrences:

1. Refer to Figure 101: Total Expected Annual Loss by County to Tornado from the US National Risk Index 2025.
2. Analyze the data presented in the figure, which combines building value, population equivalence, and agriculture value losses into a single metric.
3. Use this information to understand the overall financial impact of tornado occurrences on each county.
4. Compare counties based on their total Expected Annual Loss values for risk assessment and planning purposes.

This figure provides a comprehensive view of tornado-related risks across different geographic regions.
total expected annual loss by county,tornado risk assessment,natural disaster
Sum Expected Annual Loss Values for Tornado Sub-Types and Consequence Types
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the total Expected Annual Loss values at the Census tract or county level, follow these steps:

1. Calculate the Expected Annual Loss (EAL) for each Tornado sub-type using the appropriate formula based on whether you...
To calculate the total Expected Annual Loss values at the Census tract or county level, follow these steps:

1. Calculate the Expected Annual Loss (EAL) for each Tornado sub-type using the appropriate formula based on whether you are calculating for a Census tract or county.
2. For each consequence type (e.g., building, population equivalence, agriculture), calculate the EAL separately.
3. Sum all the EAL values for each Tornado sub-type and consequence type to obtain the total Expected Annual Loss at the specified geographic level (Census tract or county).

This process is outlined in Equation 125, which aggregates individual EAL values across all relevant Tornado sub-types and consequence types.
expected annual loss,tornado sub-type,census tract,county,consequence type,equation 125

Poisonous Plants (11) -

Avoid Ingesting Poison Hemlock Parts
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Never ingest any part of poison hemlock, including the roots, stems, or leaves.

• The roots contain chambers full of a deadly sap containing the convulsant cicutoxin.
• Ingestion can lead to grand mal seizures followed by death even from...
Never ingest any part of poison hemlock, including the roots, stems, or leaves.

• The roots contain chambers full of a deadly sap containing the convulsant cicutoxin.
• Ingestion can lead to grand mal seizures followed by death even from tiny amounts.
• Take appropriate steps to avoid accidental ingestion, such as wearing gloves when handling the plant and thoroughly cleaning your hands afterward.
poison hemlock,avoiding ingestion,toxins,cicutoxin
Identify Canada Moonseed and Avoid Ingestion
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Canada Moonseed is often mistaken for grape vines but is highly toxic.

• Identification tips:
- Leaves have 3-7 angles or lobes, with the smooth stalk attached just in from the heart-shaped base (unlike Grape leaves, which are attached at...
Canada Moonseed is often mistaken for grape vines but is highly toxic.

• Identification tips:
- Leaves have 3-7 angles or lobes, with the smooth stalk attached just in from the heart-shaped base (unlike Grape leaves, which are attached at the edge of the leaf).
- No curly tendrils like those found on Grape vines.
• Toxins: Alkaloids such as berberine, menispine, menispermine, and dauricine.
• Symptoms of poisoning: Paralysis, convulsions, and death if treatment is not sought immediately.
• Avoid ingestion of berries or roots, especially in survival contexts where they may be mistaken for wild grapes.
canada moonseed,identification,toxins,avoiding ingestion
Identify Daphne and Avoid Ingestion
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Daphne (spurge laurel) is highly toxic with a rich content of the toxin mezerein.

• Appearance: Evergreen shrub with waxy, attractive foliage and fragrant blooms.
• Toxins: Mezerein found in leaves and red or yellow fruits.
• **Symptoms...
Daphne (spurge laurel) is highly toxic with a rich content of the toxin mezerein.

• Appearance: Evergreen shrub with waxy, attractive foliage and fragrant blooms.
• Toxins: Mezerein found in leaves and red or yellow fruits.
• Symptoms of poisoning: Nausea, violent vomiting, internal bleeding, coma, and death.
• Avoid ingestion of any part of the plant, especially the leaves and fruits.
daphne,toxins,mezerein,identification
Identify English Yew by Appearance and Toxins
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
English Yew (Taxus baccata) is one of the deadliest trees on the planet.

• Appearance: Evergreen with a majestic and lush appearance, common in forests of Europe.
• Toxin: Taxine, a cardiac depressant that acts rapidly and has no...
English Yew (Taxus baccata) is one of the deadliest trees on the planet.

• Appearance: Evergreen with a majestic and lush appearance, common in forests of Europe.
• Toxin: Taxine, a cardiac depressant that acts rapidly and has no antidote.
• Symptoms of poisoning: Rapid heart failure, no known antidote, and death can occur quickly after ingestion.
• Avoid all parts of the plant, including berries, leaves, and bark.
english yew,toxins,taxine,identification
Identify Narcissus (Daffodil) and Avoid Ingestion
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Narcissus (daffodil) is highly poisonous, especially its bulbs.

• Appearance: Flowers are yellow or white with a distinctive shape.
• Toxins: Found in the bulbs, not in the flower or stems.
• Symptoms of poisoning: Nausea, vomiting,...
Narcissus (daffodil) is highly poisonous, especially its bulbs.

• Appearance: Flowers are yellow or white with a distinctive shape.
• Toxins: Found in the bulbs, not in the flower or stems.
• Symptoms of poisoning: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and in severe cases, cardiac arrest.
• Avoid ingestion of bulbs, especially poet’s narcissus which is more toxic than daffodil.
narcissus,daffodil,toxins,bulbs,identification
Identify Strychnine Tree and Its Toxins
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Strychnine tree is highly toxic due to its seeds containing lethal levels of strychnine and brucine.

• Symptoms of poisoning: Painful vomiting, facial contortions, convulsions, and death.
• Avoid ingestion of any part of the plant,...
Strychnine tree is highly toxic due to its seeds containing lethal levels of strychnine and brucine.

• Symptoms of poisoning: Painful vomiting, facial contortions, convulsions, and death.
• Avoid ingestion of any part of the plant, especially the fruit seeds.
• Identification tips: The tree is often found in tropical regions; its fruit has a distinctive appearance with toxic seeds.
strychnine tree,toxins,strychnine,brucine,identification
Identify and Avoid Baneberry (White and Red Varieties)
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Baneberry is a shrub with small, acrid-tasting berries that are highly poisonous.
2. Avoid contact: Do not touch or ingest any part of the plant, including leaves or berries.
3. **Identify key...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Baneberry is a shrub with small, acrid-tasting berries that are highly poisonous.
2. Avoid contact: Do not touch or ingest any part of the plant, including leaves or berries.
3. **Identify key features:
- White Baneberry: Berries have large, conspicuous dots on thick stalks.
- Red Baneberry: Berries have small dots on thin stalks.
4. Be aware of toxicity: As few as 5-6 berries can make you seriously ill; more can cause death.

Warning: The berries are extremely poisonous and should be avoided at all costs. Alternate common name: Doll's Eyes.
baneberry, white baneberry, red baneberry, poisonous plants, toxicity, identification
Identify and Avoid Poison Sumac Plants
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Poison sumac is a small tree or shrub that grows in wet areas such as marshes, swamps, and standing water.
2. Avoid contact: Do not touch or ingest any part of the plant, including leaves, stems, or...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Poison sumac is a small tree or shrub that grows in wet areas such as marshes, swamps, and standing water.
2. Avoid contact: Do not touch or ingest any part of the plant, including leaves, stems, or berries.
3. Be aware of toxicity: Contact with poison sumac causes skin irritation similar to poison ivy. The plant has 7-13 pointed leaflets on a compound leaf and produces small white berries in clusters.

Warning: Poison sumac is highly toxic upon contact and can cause severe allergic reactions. Avoid areas where it grows.
poison sumac, poisonous plants, toxicity, identification
Identify and Avoid Rhododendron and Azalea Plants
Critical Source: Poisonous Plants
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Rhododendron is a tree-like evergreen shrub with large, brilliant blooms. It is common in the Pacific Northwest and is Washington's state flower.
2. Avoid contact: Do not touch or ingest any part of...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Rhododendron is a tree-like evergreen shrub with large, brilliant blooms. It is common in the Pacific Northwest and is Washington's state flower.
2. Avoid contact: Do not touch or ingest any part of the plant, including leaves, stems, flowers, or berries.
3. Be aware of toxicity: The plant contains andromedatoxin, which can cause severe pain, lethargy, depression, vomiting, nausea, progressive paralysis, coma, and death.
4. Watch for azalea toxicity: Azaleas are closely related to rhododendrons and are also poisonous; avoid them as well.

Warning: All parts of the plant are deadly. Ingestion can lead to severe illness or death.
rhododendron, azalea, poisonous plants, toxicity, identification
Identify Poison Hemlock by Appearance
High Priority Source: Poisonous Plants
Look for the following characteristics to identify poison hemlock:

• Height: 3-6 feet tall.
• Flowers: Small white flowers in a flat or rounded umbel (an umbrella-shaped cluster) 2-5" wide. Individual flowers about 1/8" wide, have 5...
Look for the following characteristics to identify poison hemlock:

• Height: 3-6 feet tall.
• Flowers: Small white flowers in a flat or rounded umbel (an umbrella-shaped cluster) 2-5" wide. Individual flowers about 1/8" wide, have 5 petals and 5 stamens.
• Leaves: Alternate compound leaves with lance-shaped leaflets, pointed, with numerous teeth. Sometimes doubly compound or lobed. Sometimes tinged with red. Up to about 4" long, 1.5" wide.
• Stem: Branching, smooth & stout, often mottled or solid purple. Hollow. Lower part of stem chambered. Roots have fat tuberlike branches.
• Habitat: Wet open areas such as marshes, along shores, and sometimes open swamps.
poison hemlock,identification,appearance,characteristics
Identify Poison Ivy
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Poison ivy is typically found in clusters of three leaves, though it can have more.
2. Look for characteristics: The leaves are usually shiny and may vary in color from green to red depending on the...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the plant: Poison ivy is typically found in clusters of three leaves, though it can have more.
2. Look for characteristics: The leaves are usually shiny and may vary in color from green to red depending on the season.
3. Avoid contact: Do not touch the plant or any parts of it, including the stems and roots.
4. Watch for symptoms: If exposed, a rash may develop within 24-72 hours, characterized by redness, itching, and blisters.

Warning: Even small amounts of urushiol (the oil in poison ivy) can cause severe reactions.
poison ivy, identification, rash, urushiol

Chemical Biological Radiological (11) -

Exit Procedures During Chemical Attack
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. If an attack is imminent, occurring, or has occurred:
- Occupants leaving the shelter should put on MOPP gear.
2. Ensure that the undressing area is well ventilated, and remove contaminated overgarments from the hot...
Procedure:

1. If an attack is imminent, occurring, or has occurred:
- Occupants leaving the shelter should put on MOPP gear.
2. Ensure that the undressing area is well ventilated, and remove contaminated overgarments from the hot line area to avoid vapor buildup.
3. When exiting the shelter:
- Enter the air lock first.
- If contamination may be present or suspected outside, or if higher authority prescribes continuous wearing of masks, mask immediately.
- If required, don MOPP gear using prescribed procedures.
4. Avoid contaminating MOPP gear during temporary exits to reduce reentry time and risk.
5. In shelters with multiple occupants, rotate individuals assigned outside duties if possible to minimize exposure.
exit procedures, MOPP gear, chemical attack, contamination avoidance
Respond to Chemical Agent Detection in Shelter
Critical Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. If the chemical agent detector indicates presence of a chemical agent:
- Immediately mask all occupants.
- Check shelter pressure, door, and power systems to ensure they are operating properly.
- Change filters...
Procedure:

1. If the chemical agent detector indicates presence of a chemical agent:
- Immediately mask all occupants.
- Check shelter pressure, door, and power systems to ensure they are operating properly.
- Change filters according to SOP and appropriate operational maintenance manual.
2. Ensure that occupants work in appropriate MOPP gear until the airflow purges the agent from the air.
3. Use a detector to confirm that no agent is present before discontinuing use of MOPP gear.
4. If contamination occurs inside the shelter:
- Wipe off any unabsorbed liquid agent from equipment using a wet rag if moisture will not harm the equipment.
- Warm the interior by turning off the air conditioner to enhance evaporation and desorption of the agent from materials.
- Use selective forced air from a heat source to aid in contamination removal.
5. Decontamination should be initiated shortly after agent is deposited for maximum effectiveness.
chemical attack, MOPP gear, decontamination, shelter procedures, chemical agent detector
Avoid Contamination by Knowing Contaminated Areas
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Identify contaminated areas using maps or intelligence reports.
2. Avoid flying over or landing in these areas if possible.
3. If landing is unavoidable, choose a location that minimizes splash contamination (e.g., flat, dry...
Procedure:

1. Identify contaminated areas using maps or intelligence reports.
2. Avoid flying over or landing in these areas if possible.
3. If landing is unavoidable, choose a location that minimizes splash contamination (e.g., flat, dry surfaces).
4. Limit the number of aircraft operating in contaminated zones to reduce overall exposure.
5. Use already contaminated aircraft for operations in contaminated areas where feasible.
contamination avoidance,decontamination,aircraft safety,nbc hazards
Contain Contamination on Aircraft Exterior and Use Engine Heat for Interior Decon
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. If air and ground crews are careful in a contaminated environment, contamination can be contained to the exterior of the aircraft.
2. Increased airflow over the aircraft’s skin increases evaporation rates, reducing some...
Procedure:

1. If air and ground crews are careful in a contaminated environment, contamination can be contained to the exterior of the aircraft.
2. Increased airflow over the aircraft’s skin increases evaporation rates, reducing some hazards.
3. Thickened agents may remain hazardous even after prolonged flights.
4. If the interior is contaminated, fly with doors open to reduce hazard.
5. Use engine heat to decontaminate the interior of the engine compartment.
6. Consider using immediate-, operational-, or thorough-decon methods for more active decontamination.
engine heat,interior decon,nbc hazards
Implement Measures to Limit Contamination Spread in Aircraft
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Use covers (engine covers, flyaway gear, hatches) when the aircraft is not in use.
2. Provide overhead cover for parked aircraft if possible.
3. Conduct inspections visually without touching or shaking items; contaminated crews...
Procedure:

1. Use covers (engine covers, flyaway gear, hatches) when the aircraft is not in use.
2. Provide overhead cover for parked aircraft if possible.
3. Conduct inspections visually without touching or shaking items; contaminated crews should perform this task.
4. Limit contamination transfer by using plastic lining in troop compartments when transporting contaminated personnel or casualties.
5. Install a plastic curtain between the troop compartment and flight compartment, secured with tape or Velcro.
6. Use the aircraft’s heater with the curtain to create overpressure in the pilot’s compartment, limiting vapor entry.
plastic lining,overpressure,decontamination measures,nbc hazards
Interior Monitoring Procedures for Chemical Agents
High Priority Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Each shelter or van must have a chemical agent detector kit placed at the downstream end of the airflow, usually near the door.
2. Monitor continuously for chemical agents using the detector kit and/or detector paper.
3. If an...
Procedure:

1. Each shelter or van must have a chemical agent detector kit placed at the downstream end of the airflow, usually near the door.
2. Monitor continuously for chemical agents using the detector kit and/or detector paper.
3. If an alert is issued:
- Shelter guards should monitor for agent presence before an attack begins.
- After a chemical attack ceases, periodically monitor outside air to determine if aerosol or liquid agent is still present.
4. Use a detector kit to monitor the air periodically for agent vapor.
monitoring procedures, chemical agent detection, shelter monitoring, detector kit
Operate in MOPP Gear When Aircraft is Contaminated
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. If an aircraft becomes contaminated, the aircrew must fly in MOPP gear.
2. Be aware that wearing MOPP gear degrades performance over time and may affect mission readiness.
3. Ensure all personnel are trained on proper use of MOPP...
Procedure:

1. If an aircraft becomes contaminated, the aircrew must fly in MOPP gear.
2. Be aware that wearing MOPP gear degrades performance over time and may affect mission readiness.
3. Ensure all personnel are trained on proper use of MOPP gear to minimize degradation of performance.
mopp gear,aircraft contamination,nbc hazards
Perform Aircraft Decontamination Using Appropriate Methods
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Use the immediate-, operational-, or thorough-decon method as appropriate for the level of contamination.
2. Conduct aircrew spot decon and aircraft washdown by unit personnel.
3. For more complex decontamination, a chemical decon...
Procedure:

1. Use the immediate-, operational-, or thorough-decon method as appropriate for the level of contamination.
2. Conduct aircrew spot decon and aircraft washdown by unit personnel.
3. For more complex decontamination, a chemical decon unit should perform DAD (Disinfection, Decontamination, and Deactivation) procedures.
4. Follow modifications to standard methods as needed for aircraft-specific requirements.
aircraft decon,dad,nbc hazards
Use Approved Decontaminants on Aircraft
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Only use approved decontaminants such as soap and water, kerosene, JP8, or diesel fuels.
2. Avoid using caustic decontaminants like DS2, STB, bleaches, or sodium hypochlorite.
3. Use JP8 to remove some agents from the aircraft’s...
Procedure:

1. Only use approved decontaminants such as soap and water, kerosene, JP8, or diesel fuels.
2. Avoid using caustic decontaminants like DS2, STB, bleaches, or sodium hypochlorite.
3. Use JP8 to remove some agents from the aircraft’s skin and components; however, it does not neutralize agents.
4. When using a cloth soaked in JP8, avoid wiping internal components near the exhaust.
5. If water is available, use it to rinse off JP8 after application.
6. Avoid high-pressure water or extreme hot air on delicate parts of the aircraft.
approved decontaminants,nbc hazards,aircraft cleaning
Use M9 Detector Paper for Contamination Detection
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Apply M9 detector paper to the landing gear of the aircraft.
2. Place another piece of M9 detector paper on the windscreen where aircrew can see it.
3. FARP personnel must check M9 detector paper before servicing the...
Procedure:

1. Apply M9 detector paper to the landing gear of the aircraft.
2. Place another piece of M9 detector paper on the windscreen where aircrew can see it.
3. FARP personnel must check M9 detector paper before servicing the aircraft.

CAUTION: M9 detector paper may show false positives if exposed to petroleum products.
m9 detector paper,contamination detection,nbc hazards
Use Sodium Carbonate for G Nerve Agent Decontamination
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure:

1. Apply sodium carbonate to surfaces contaminated with G nerve agents.
2. Sodium carbonate is not effective against V nerve or blister agents.
3. Be aware that the chemical reaction between sodium carbonate and V nerve agents is...
Procedure:

1. Apply sodium carbonate to surfaces contaminated with G nerve agents.
2. Sodium carbonate is not effective against V nerve or blister agents.
3. Be aware that the chemical reaction between sodium carbonate and V nerve agents is extremely slow and produces a highly toxic product.
4. Use caution when handling sodium carbonate, as it can be corrosive and potentially damage aircraft surfaces.
sodium carbonate,g nerve agent,nbc hazards

Fire Safety (10) -

Check Closed Doors for Heat Before Opening During a Fire
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: When escaping through a closed door during a fire:

• Use the back of your hand to feel the top of the door, doorknob, and the crack between the door and door frame.
• Do not use the palm of your hand or fingers...
Action: When escaping through a closed door during a fire:

• Use the back of your hand to feel the top of the door, doorknob, and the crack between the door and door frame.
• Do not use the palm of your hand or fingers to test for heat, as this can cause burns that impair escape (e.g., inability to climb ladders or crawl).

Decision Making:
• If the door is hot, do not open it. Escape through a window instead. If unable to escape, hang a white or light-colored sheet outside the window to signal firefighters.
• If the door is cool, open it slowly and ensure that fire and/or smoke are not blocking your escape route.
• If the escape route is blocked, shut the door immediately and use an alternate escape route (e.g., a window).
• If clear, leave immediately through the door and close it behind you. Be prepared to crawl, as smoke and heat rise, and the air is clearer and cooler near the floor.
check door heat, fire escape, closed door safety, fire safety procedure
Close Doors Behind You During Fire Escape to Delay Fire Spread
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: As you escape a fire:

• Close doors behind you as you leave.

Purpose: This action helps delay the spread of fire and smoke, increasing your safety and potentially giving firefighters more time to respond.
close doors during fire, fire safety, door closure procedure
Crawl Low Under Smoke During a Fire Escape
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: When escaping through smoke:

• Crawl low under the smoke to your exit.

Important Notes:
• Heavy smoke and poisonous gases collect first along the ceiling.
• Crawl close to the floor where the air is clearer and cooler.
crawl under smoke, fire escape technique, smoke inhalation prevention
Escaping from a House on Fire
Critical Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Creep or crawl with your face near the ground to avoid smoke inhalation.
2. Near the floor, the air is pure and can be safely breathed even if the room is full of smoke.
3. The best escape method from upper windows is using a...
Procedure:
1. Creep or crawl with your face near the ground to avoid smoke inhalation.
2. Near the floor, the air is pure and can be safely breathed even if the room is full of smoke.
3. The best escape method from upper windows is using a knotted rope.
4. If a leap is unavoidable, throw a bed out first or have beds placed by those outside to break the fall.
fire_escape,survival,house_fire
Evacuate Immediately if Heat or Smoke is Detected in a Damaged Building
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: If you detect heat or smoke when entering a damaged building:

• Evacuate immediately and do not reenter the building.
• Do not attempt to retrieve personal belongings unless it is safe to do so.
evacuate damaged building, smoke detection, fire safety protocol
Stay Out of the Building Once You Are Safely Escaped from a Fire
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: After escaping a fire:

• Do not reenter the building under any circumstances.
• Call 9-1-1 immediately to report the fire and your location.
do not reenter fire building, call 911, fire safety protocol
Stop, Drop, and Roll to Extinguish Clothing Fire
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: If your clothes catch on fire:

• Stop immediately.
• Drop to the ground.
• Roll over and over until the flames are extinguished.

Important Notes:
• Running increases the intensity of the fire due to increased oxygen...
Action: If your clothes catch on fire:

• Stop immediately.
• Drop to the ground.
• Roll over and over until the flames are extinguished.

Important Notes:
• Running increases the intensity of the fire due to increased oxygen exposure.
• Do not attempt to remove burning clothing while standing; this can spread the fire further.
stop drop roll, clothing fire, fire safety, emergency procedure
Bringing Horses out of a Stable on Fire
Critical Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Throw the harness or saddles to which the horses are accustomed over their backs.
2. This will encourage them to exit the stable as tractably as usual.
horse,stable_fire,safety
Do Not Open Safe or Strong Box After a Fire Until It Has Cooled
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: If you have a safe or strong box:

• Do not open it immediately after a fire, as it can retain intense heat for several hours.
• Opening the box before it has cooled could result in burns or injury.
safe box fire safety, do not open safe after fire, heat retention in safes
Ask Someone to Watch Your Property if You Must Leave a Damaged Building
Standard Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: If you must leave your home because a building inspector says the building is unsafe:

• Ask someone you trust to watch the property during your absence.
• This helps protect your belongings and ensures that no unauthorized...
Action: If you must leave your home because a building inspector says the building is unsafe:

• Ask someone you trust to watch the property during your absence.
• This helps protect your belongings and ensures that no unauthorized individuals access the premises.
property watch after fire, building inspector, safe property management

Tsunami Preparedness (10) -

Tsunami Warning and Evacuation Procedures in British Columbia
Critical Source: All Hazard Preparedness Workbook
Procedure:

1. In British Columbia, there is a Tsunami Warning and Alerting Plan in place to warn coastal residents.
2. If you live in a tsunami zone and feel a large earthquake, go inland or to higher ground immediately.
tsunami warning, evacuation, British Columbia, higher ground
Prepare for a Tsunami Risk
Critical Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
Procedure:

1. If you live near or visit a coastal area, learn about the tsunami risk in your region.
2. Check if your community has maps with evacuation zones and routes that show areas disproportionately affected by tsunamis.
3. **Sign...
Procedure:

1. If you live near or visit a coastal area, learn about the tsunami risk in your region.
2. Check if your community has maps with evacuation zones and routes that show areas disproportionately affected by tsunamis.
3. Sign up for local tsunami alerts and follow the guidance provided by authorities.
4. Learn the signs of a potential tsunami, including:
- An earthquake
- A loud roar from the ocean
- Unusual ocean behavior, such as:
- A sudden rise or wall of water
- Sudden draining showing the ocean floor
5. Know and practice community evacuation plans.
6. Map out your routes from home and work to safe shelters located:
- 100 feet or more above sea level, or
- At least one mile inland.
tsunami, coastal area, evacuation plan, tsunami alert, ocean behavior
Tsunami Awareness and Preparedness for Coastal Residents
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Identify tsunami risks: If you live in a coastal area, especially near large bodies of water, be aware that tsunamis can occur due to earthquakes.
2. Monitor seismic activity: Stay informed about earthquake events that...
Procedure:

1. Identify tsunami risks: If you live in a coastal area, especially near large bodies of water, be aware that tsunamis can occur due to earthquakes.
2. Monitor seismic activity: Stay informed about earthquake events that could trigger tsunamis, particularly along the Pacific Ring of Fire.
3. Understand warning signs: Be alert for sudden changes in sea level (either a rapid withdrawal or an unusual rise), which may indicate an approaching tsunami.
4. Evacuate immediately if warned: If you receive a tsunami warning, move to higher ground as quickly as possible and avoid low-lying areas.
5. Prepare an emergency kit: Include items such as water, food, flashlights, batteries, first aid supplies, and important documents in case of displacement.
6. Designate a safe meeting place: Choose a location that is above sea level where family members can regroup during or after a tsunami event.
7. Practice evacuation drills: Regularly rehearse your escape plan with all household members to ensure everyone knows what to do in an emergency.
tsunami,coastal safety,evacuation drill,sea level warning,emergency kit
Understanding Tsunamis and Their Dangers
Critical Source: All Hazard Preparedness Workbook
Procedure:

1. A tsunami is a Japanese word meaning *harbour wave*.
2. These huge waves are caused during an earthquake when plates along the ocean floor push up against each other, generating a series of ocean waves that race outward in all...
Procedure:

1. A tsunami is a Japanese word meaning *harbour wave*.
2. These huge waves are caused during an earthquake when plates along the ocean floor push up against each other, generating a series of ocean waves that race outward in all directions.
3. A typical wave height for Pacific tsunamis is between six to nine metres at the shoreline.
4. Tsunamis act quite differently from tidal waves and have nothing to do with tides or the weather.
5. Although they move through the open ocean at intense speeds, they are scarcely noticeable, even when passing under a boat.
6. Their danger lies in the shallower water near shore where the height of the waves increases as the tsunami runs into river and coastal channels.
tsunami, wave height, ocean floor, danger zones
Determine Local Evacuation Routes for Tsunamis
High Priority Source: All Hazard Preparedness Workbook
If you live in a tsunami zone, take the following actions:

• Identify and familiarize yourself with local evacuation routes that lead to higher ground.

• Ensure these routes are accessible and well-known by all household members.

• Check with...
If you live in a tsunami zone, take the following actions:

• Identify and familiarize yourself with local evacuation routes that lead to higher ground.

• Ensure these routes are accessible and well-known by all household members.

• Check with your community’s local emergency coordinator to determine how warnings for an impending tsunami will be issued (e.g., phone, door-to-door, or siren).
evacuation routes, tsunami warning, higher ground
Identify Tsunami Risk Areas in Your Province
High Priority Source: All Hazard Preparedness Workbook
To prepare for a tsunami, follow these steps:

• Review the outline map provided in your province’s emergency preparedness materials to identify coastal areas shaded as being at risk of experiencing damaging tsunamis generated by either close or...
To prepare for a tsunami, follow these steps:

• Review the outline map provided in your province’s emergency preparedness materials to identify coastal areas shaded as being at risk of experiencing damaging tsunamis generated by either close or distant coast events.

• Use this information to understand whether you live in an area that could be affected by a tsunami.
tsunami, risk areas, map, coastal regions
Access Historical Tsunami Runup Data from NOAA Database
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Visit the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) website.
2. Navigate to the National Geophysical Data Center or the World Data Service Global Historical Tsunami Database.
3. Locate the dataset titled...
Procedure:

1. Visit the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) website.
2. Navigate to the National Geophysical Data Center or the World Data Service Global Historical Tsunami Database.
3. Locate the dataset titled 'Global Historical Tsunami Database'.
4. Download the data in CSV format, as described in Table 77 of the National Risk Index Technical Documentation (2025).

Details:
• The dataset includes historical tsunami runup points with spatiotemporal information such as geographic coordinates, observation dates, and event validity.
• Each runup point is assigned a unique identifier (Tsunami Runup ID) and each tsunami event also has a unique identifier (Tsunami Event ID).
• The data spans from January 1, 1800, to December 31, 2021.
• There may be fewer records for Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands due to limited gauge coverage in those regions.
tsunami data, NOAA database, historical runup points, CSV format, tsunami event ID
Access Tsunami Loss Quantification Data from FEMA Study (P-2426)
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Visit the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) website.
2. Locate and access the study titled 'Estimated Average Annualized Tsunami Losses for the United States (P-2426)'.
3. Review the data provided in this study,...
Procedure:

1. Visit the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) website.
2. Locate and access the study titled 'Estimated Average Annualized Tsunami Losses for the United States (P-2426)'.
3. Review the data provided in this study, which includes:
- Economic loss estimates to buildings (structural, non-structural, contents, and inventory replacement values).
- Residential population losses from modeled earthquake-generated tsunami scenarios.
4. Use the Hazus 6.1 software, if available, for detailed analysis of tsunami risk in regions such as the West Coast (California, Oregon, Washington), Alaska, Hawai‘i, and U.S. Pacific Territories (American Samoa, Guam, Northern Mariana Islands), as well as Caribbean Territories (Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands).

Details:
• This study was conducted in collaboration with the National Tsunami Hazard Mitigation Program (NTMMP), NOAA, and the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS).
• The data does not include impacts from tsunamis generated by landslides, volcanic eruptions, glacial calving, near-earth objects, or weather events.
• Tsunami risk for states along the East Coast, Gulf Coast, and Great Lakes is not included in this study due to software limitations.
FEMA tsunami loss data, Hazus 6.1 software, economic loss quantification, P-2426 study
Understand Exclusions in Tsunami Risk Assessment from FEMA Study (P-2426)
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Review the documentation or metadata provided with the FEMA P-2426 study.
2. Identify the following exclusions:
- **Tsunamis generated by landslides, volcanic eruptions, glacial calving, near-earth objects, and weather...
Procedure:

1. Review the documentation or metadata provided with the FEMA P-2426 study.
2. Identify the following exclusions:
- Tsunamis generated by landslides, volcanic eruptions, glacial calving, near-earth objects, and weather events are not included in this assessment.
3. Note that the study focuses on earthquake-induced tsunami risk only, which is based on hazard data from specific regions:
- West Coast (California, Oregon, Washington)
- Alaska
- Hawai‘i
- U.S. Pacific Territories (American Samoa, Guam, Northern Mariana Islands)
- Caribbean Territories (Puerto Rico, U.S. Virgin Islands)
4. Recognize that the study does not include tsunami risk assessments for states along the East Coast, Gulf Coast, and Great Lakes due to software limitations in Hazus 6.1.

Details:
• This exclusion is important when interpreting the results of the study or using it for risk modeling.
• Users should be aware that this data does not provide a complete picture of all potential tsunami risks across the United States.
FEMA study exclusions, non-earthquake tsunamis, Hazus software limitations, risk assessment gaps
Understand Limitations of Historical Tsunami Data from the National Risk Index 2025
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Review documentation or metadata associated with the Global Historical Tsunami Database.
2. Identify known limitations, such as:
- Fewer gauges in Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands, leading to potential...
Procedure:

1. Review documentation or metadata associated with the Global Historical Tsunami Database.
2. Identify known limitations, such as:
- Fewer gauges in Puerto Rico and U.S. Virgin Islands, leading to potential underrepresentation of tsunami events in those regions.
3. Note that data is based on historical records dating back to 1800, but may not include all possible runup points due to incomplete or missing historical documentation.
4. Recognize that the dataset does not account for tsunamis caused by:
- Landslides
- Volcanic eruptions
- Glacial calving
- Near-earth objects
- Weather events
5. Understand that this data is used to calculate a period of record for historical losses, which is 222.15 years (from 1800 to 2021), and should not be confused with the probabilistic modeling period (e.g., “10,000-year event”).

Details:
• The dataset includes information on tsunami runup points, but may lack details on magnitude or damage for some events.
• This data is used as a basis for risk assessments and modeling, but should be interpreted with awareness of its limitations.
tsunami data limitations, historical underrepresentation, probabilistic modeling, NOAA dataset

Coastal Flooding (10) -

Respond to Coastal Flooding Events
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Objective: Respond effectively to coastal flooding events.

Procedure:
1. Monitor coastal flood warnings issued by the government and local authorities.
2. Evacuate immediately if advised, especially during the holiday season when more...
Objective: Respond effectively to coastal flooding events.

Procedure:
1. Monitor coastal flood warnings issued by the government and local authorities.
2. Evacuate immediately if advised, especially during the holiday season when more people may be affected.
3. Follow public warning systems and inform yourself of evacuation routes and shelter locations.
4. Ensure that vulnerable individuals (e.g., elderly, disabled) receive assistance during evacuation.
5. Prepare for potential disruptions to essential services such as:
- Roads and railways
- Hospitals, schools, care homes, and emergency services
6. Be aware that fatalities and casualties may occur, including those caused indirectly by flooding (e.g., due to lack of access to medical care).
7. Stay informed through official channels for updates on the situation.

Notes:
• Coastal flooding is caused by high tides, low pressure weather systems, and storm surges.
• Rising sea levels due to climate change will increase the risk of coastal flooding in the future.
coastal flooding,evacuation,response plan,natural disaster
Calculate Annualized Frequency for NOAA High Tide Flooding Subtypes
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the annualized frequency for National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) High Tide Flooding subtypes:

1. Identify the intersection of each NOAA High Tide Flooding subtype layer with the **Census block developed area...
To calculate the annualized frequency for National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) High Tide Flooding subtypes:

1. Identify the intersection of each NOAA High Tide Flooding subtype layer with the Census block developed area polygons.
2. For each subtype, calculate area-weighted frequencies using the midpoint probability classification categories specified by NOAA.
3. Use the following midpoint probabilities for each category:

• Category 1 (>10 events per year): 15
• Category 2 (5–10 events per year): 7.5
• Category 3 (1–5 events per year): 3
• Category 4 (99%–20% annual change): 0.6
• Category 5 (20%–10% annual chance): 0.15
• Category 6 (10%–5% annual chance): 0.075
• Category 7 (5%–2% annual chance): 0.035
• Category 8 (<2% annual chance): 0.015

4. Adjust frequencies for areas protected by levees, which are clipped out before computing the sub-type area-weighted frequencies.
NOAA,High Tide Flooding,midpoint probability,Census block,frequency calculation
Calculate Annualized Frequency for Special Flood Hazard Area 0.2% Annual Chance
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the annualized frequency for Special Flood Hazard Area 0.2% annual chance:

1. Identify the Census block level intersection with the Special Flood Hazard Area 0.2% annual chance floodplain delta layer.
2. Apply a constant value of...
To calculate the annualized frequency for Special Flood Hazard Area 0.2% annual chance:

1. Identify the Census block level intersection with the Special Flood Hazard Area 0.2% annual chance floodplain delta layer.
2. Apply a constant value of 0.002 as the annualized frequency to this floodplain sub-type.
3. This applies specifically to areas within the 500-year event floodplain.

> Note: For the Great Lakes Region, areas outside the Special Flood Hazard Area 100 and 500-year flood that intersect the Federal Emergency Management Agency Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment Great Lakes layer receive a minimum annualized frequency of 0.0001.
annualized frequency,Special Flood Hazard Area,0.2% annual chance,floodplain delta layer,Census block
Calculate Coastal Flooding Sub-Type Building Exposure
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the estimated building value exposed to the Coastal Flooding sub-type in a specific Census block:

1. Determine the intersected developed area of the Coastal Flooding sub-type with the Census block (in square kilometers). This is...
To calculate the estimated building value exposed to the Coastal Flooding sub-type in a specific Census block:

1. Determine the intersected developed area of the Coastal Flooding sub-type with the Census block (in square kilometers). This is denoted as $A_{block}$.
2. Calculate the developed area building value density of the Census block (in dollars per square kilometer). This is denoted as $V_{block}$.
3. Multiply $A_{block}$ by $V_{block}$ to obtain the estimated building value exposed to the Coastal Flooding sub-type in a specific Census block, denoted as $B_{block}$. The formula is:

$$ B_{block} = A_{block} imes V_{block} $$

This calculation is performed for each of the eight layers so that exposure values for each subtype of flood zone and consequence type are calculated.
coastal flooding, building exposure, census block, developed area, value calculation
Calculate Exposure Value for Buildings and Population in Coastal Flooding Zones
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure for Calculating Exposure Value for Buildings and Population:

1. Multiply Intersected Areas by Density Values:
- For each coastal flooding sub-type:
- Multiply the intersected area (in square kilometers) by the developed...
Procedure for Calculating Exposure Value for Buildings and Population:

1. Multiply Intersected Areas by Density Values:
- For each coastal flooding sub-type:
- Multiply the intersected area (in square kilometers) by the developed area building value density of the Census block.
- Multiply the same intersected area by the developed area population density of the Census block.

2. Use Hazus 6.0 Data for Density Calculations:
- Developed area building and population value densities are calculated as follows:
- Divide the total exposure values (from Hazus 6.0) by the developed land area in square kilometers.

3. Express Population Equivalence Exposure in Dollars:
- Use the Value of Statistical Life to convert population equivalence exposure into monetary terms (in dollars).

Equation Reference:
• See Equation 29 for the mathematical model used to calculate exposure values.
exposure calculation, building value density, population density, Hazus 6.0, Value of Statistical Life
Identify Coastal Flooding Exposure Areas
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure for Identifying Coastal Flooding Exposure Areas:

1. Intersect Event Sub-Type Layers with Census Block Developed Area Polygons:
- Use the following layers:
- National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Minor,...
Procedure for Identifying Coastal Flooding Exposure Areas:

1. Intersect Event Sub-Type Layers with Census Block Developed Area Polygons:
- Use the following layers:
- National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Minor, Moderate, and Major High Tide Flooding.
- Special Flood Hazard Area 100-year flood area.
- Special Flood Hazard Area 500-year flood area.
- Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment Great Lakes flood area.
- Intersect each layer independently with Census block developed area polygons within the processing database.

2. Refine Special Flood Hazard Areas:
- For the Special Flood Hazard Area 100-year and 500-year flood areas:
- Erase any developed area intersects that fall within the NOAA Minor, Moderate, or Major High Tide Flooding area.

3. Overlay Great Lakes Flood Area with Census Block Polygons:
- Overlay the FEMA Geospatial Flood Risk Assessment Great Lakes flood area with Census block developed area polygons.
- Identify any developed area polygon that intersects the Great Lakes susceptible zone as exposed to the Great Lakes flooding hazard-subtype.

4. Generate Exposure Tables:
- The resulting tables should include:
- Layer polygon’s unique identifier.
- Census block number.
- Developed area of intersection (in square kilometers).

Example Table Format (Table 22):
|_Coastal Floo_d_Zone_<br>_100yr ID_|_Census Block_|_Area Developed km2_|
|---|---|---|
|391|150030099021008|0.001979101|
|445|150030098012011|0|
|2112|480079501002007|1.59E-06|
coastal flooding, exposure areas, NOAA, FEMA, Census block, flood hazard zones
Prepare for Coastal Flooding Using Advanced Forecasting
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Monitor flood forecasts from the Flood Forecasting Centre (FFC).
2. Expect 5-7 days of flood forecasts showing medium and then high risk of coastal flooding.
3. Be prepared for Severe Flood Warnings, which will be...
Procedure:

1. Monitor flood forecasts from the Flood Forecasting Centre (FFC).
2. Expect 5-7 days of flood forecasts showing medium and then high risk of coastal flooding.
3. Be prepared for Severe Flood Warnings, which will be issued a minimum of 24 hours in advance by the Environment Agency (EA).

Details:
• The FFC provides at least a 3-day lead time for coastal events.
• Use this information to prepare your household, business, or community for potential flooding.
• Ensure that you have access to emergency supplies and evacuation plans in place.
coastal flooding,flood forecasting,environment agency,severe flood warnings
Use NOAA HTF Probability Data for Coastal Flooding Risk Assessment
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:
1. Access the High Tide Flooding (HTF) Probability dataset from [NOAA Office for Coastal Management](https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/sealevelrise/noaa-nos-techrpt01-global-regional-SLR-scenarios-US.pdf).
2. Analyze three...
Procedure:
1. Access the High Tide Flooding (HTF) Probability dataset from [NOAA Office for Coastal Management](https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/hazards/sealevelrise/noaa-nos-techrpt01-global-regional-SLR-scenarios-US.pdf).
2. Analyze three severity levels of flooding events:
- Minor (1–2-year floods)
- Moderate (10–50 year floods)
- Major (100-year floods)
3. Use GeoTIFF raster layers to assess probability values for each location, which range from 0 to 100.
Notes:
• This dataset is crucial for estimating the likelihood of high tide flooding events in coastal regions.
• Ensure that your GIS software supports GeoTIFF format.
NOAA,HTF,flood probability,coastal flooding
Utilize CoreLogic Special Flood Hazard Area Layer for Data Supplementation
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:
1. Access the CoreLogic digitized floodplain boundaries dataset.
2. Use this data to supplement FEMA’s National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL) in areas where only paper Flood Insurance Rate Maps exist.
3. This dataset has been compiled by...
Procedure:
1. Access the CoreLogic digitized floodplain boundaries dataset.
2. Use this data to supplement FEMA’s National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL) in areas where only paper Flood Insurance Rate Maps exist.
3. This dataset has been compiled by digitizing existing paper maps and legacy products from FEMA.
4. FEMA licenses this data for use in regions with digital data-coverage gaps.
Notes:
• CoreLogic’s data is critical for filling gaps in floodplain mapping where digital coverage is lacking.
• Ensure that the dataset is up to date and compatible with your GIS tools.
CoreLogic,flood hazard layer,coastal flooding,data supplementation
Utilize Local and National Response Resources for Coastal Flooding
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Local response teams will have access to operational resources such as:
- Temporary flood barriers
- Mobile pumps
- Logistical support for deployment
2. Flood rescue teams (over 100 specialist teams) are on...
Procedure:

1. Local response teams will have access to operational resources such as:
- Temporary flood barriers
- Mobile pumps
- Logistical support for deployment
2. Flood rescue teams (over 100 specialist teams) are on standby and can be deployed across the country.
3. National mutual aid and military assistance may also be used to support local response efforts.
4. The Environment Agency (EA) will lead any national coordination, in collaboration with the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.

Details:
• Local authorities work as part of a multi-agency team coordinated through the Local Resilience Forum (LRF).
• These teams are responsible for flood preparedness, warning systems, operating defences, evacuation planning, and accommodation requirements.
flood response,flood rescue teams,military assistance,national coordination

Landslide Risk Assessment (10) -

Calculate Exposure Value for Landslide Hazard Modeling
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Sum the intersected areas of the Landslide-susceptibility polygons for each Census block.
2. Multiply this sum by the average weighted grid percentage of the Census block to determine a base exposure value.
3....
Procedure:

1. Sum the intersected areas of the Landslide-susceptibility polygons for each Census block.
2. Multiply this sum by the average weighted grid percentage of the Census block to determine a base exposure value.
3. Multiply the result by the following two factors:
- The developed area building value density of the Census block
- The developed area population density of the Census block
4. Use this final product to model the conservative-case concentration of exposure within the Census block.
5. To calculate Census block densities, divide the total county values (from Hazus 6.0) by the developed land area in square kilometers.
6. Express population equivalence exposure using the Value of Statistical Life to convert it into dollars.

Notes:
• This procedure is used for modeling exposure value within a Census block based on landslide-susceptibility data.
• The Value of Statistical Life is a standard metric used in risk assessment and economic analysis.
exposure value, census block density, developed area, statistical life
Calculate Landslide Exposure Using Census Block Data
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Intersect the Landslide-susceptibility polygons with Census block polygons within the processing database.
2. Create a resulting table containing:
- The polygon’s unique identifier (_LandslideGridCellID_)
-...
Procedure:

1. Intersect the Landslide-susceptibility polygons with Census block polygons within the processing database.
2. Create a resulting table containing:
- The polygon’s unique identifier (_LandslideGridCellID_)
- Census Block number
- Exposure Percent (from Table 58)
- Intersected Area in Square Kilometers (_IntersectedAreaKm2_)
3. Use the sample data format as shown in Table 60 for consistency.

Example Sample Data Format:
|_LandslideGridCellID_|_CensusBlock_|_Exposure Percent_|_IntersectedAreaKm2_|
|---|---|---|---|
|206991169|210019706001006|0.160|0.0000054990|
|207012504|210019706001006|0.080|0.0022838710|

Notes:
• This procedure is used to identify areas of exposure based on the intersection of landslide-susceptibility polygons and Census blocks.
• The data in this table is crucial for further exposure calculations.
census block, landslide exposure, intersection data, susceptibility polygon
Determine Landslide Hazard Potential Based on Grid Code
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify any county that contains a Census block intersecting a Landslide-susceptibility polygon.
2. Check the grid code of the intersected polygon.
3. If the grid code is 1 or higher, assume the county has potential...
Procedure:

1. Identify any county that contains a Census block intersecting a Landslide-susceptibility polygon.
2. Check the grid code of the intersected polygon.
3. If the grid code is 1 or higher, assume the county has potential for a Landslide hazard.

Notes:
• This procedure applies to counties where Census blocks intersect with landslide-susceptibility polygons.
• Grid codes are used as indicators of susceptibility levels.
grid code, landslide hazard, census block, susceptibility polygon
Process Non-Spatial Data for Landslide Frequency Estimation
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. For the Continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i, use a statistical model to estimate county-level landslide frequency.
2. Do not calculate historical occurrence count or period of record for these states, as it...
Procedure:

1. For the Continental United States, Alaska, and Hawai‘i, use a statistical model to estimate county-level landslide frequency.
2. Do not calculate historical occurrence count or period of record for these states, as it is not needed for frequency calculations.
3. For Puerto Rico, calculate the historical occurrence count based on landslides triggered by Hurricanes Maria and Irma.
4. Use data recorded by Hughes et al. (2019) to estimate landslide frequency in Puerto Rico.

Notes:
• This procedure is specific to non-spatial data processing for landslide hazard modeling.
• Historical occurrence count is only required for Puerto Rico due to the extreme event of Hurricane Maria.
non-spatial data, landslide frequency, puerto rico, hurricane maria
Calculate Annualized Frequency of Landslide Events at Census Tract Level
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Obtain the annualized frequency of Landslide events per county, as determined by the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS).
2. Calculate the area-weighted average for USGS Susceptibility values over the area of the Census...
Procedure:

1. Obtain the annualized frequency of Landslide events per county, as determined by the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS).
2. Calculate the area-weighted average for USGS Susceptibility values over the area of the Census tract.
3. Use Equation 98 to calculate the annualized frequency of Landslide events at the Census tract level, using an area-weighted proportional relationship between the county and the tract.
4. This value is inherited by each Census block within the tract for use in Expected Annual Loss calculations.

Details:
• The annualized frequency represents the estimated number of recorded Landslide occurrences per year for a specific area.
• This calculation helps assess risk and plan for disaster mitigation at both county and Census tract levels.
annualized frequency, landslide events, census tract, usgs data, expected annual loss
Calculate Population Equivalence Value Exposed to Landslide Susceptibility at Census Tract Level
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify all Census blocks within a specific Census tract that are exposed to Landslide susceptibility.
2. Sum the total population equivalence value for these blocks in dollars.
3. This sum represents the...
Procedure:

1. Identify all Census blocks within a specific Census tract that are exposed to Landslide susceptibility.
2. Sum the total population equivalence value for these blocks in dollars.
3. This sum represents the population equivalence value exposed to Landslide susceptibility for the Census tract.

Details:
• Use data from the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) to determine which areas are susceptible to landslides.
• Ensure that only population values in these high-risk zones are included in the calculation.
• This value is used as part of the National Risk Index calculations for risk assessment and disaster planning.
population equivalence, landslide susceptibility, census tract, usgs data, national risk index
Create Landslide Susceptibility Models Using Slope Relief Threshold Data
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Obtain high-resolution 10-meter Digital Elevation Model (DEM) data.
2. Access a historical record of over 600,000 landslides across the continental United States, Alaska, Hawai‘i, and Puerto Rico.
3. Use slope and relief variables...
Procedure:

1. Obtain high-resolution 10-meter Digital Elevation Model (DEM) data.
2. Access a historical record of over 600,000 landslides across the continental United States, Alaska, Hawai‘i, and Puerto Rico.
3. Use slope and relief variables from the DEM to identify landslide-susceptible locations.
4. Downsample the resulting data to a 90-meter grid to account for uncertainty in the DEM and historical landslide positions.
