Measurement: Sustained winds are defined as one-minute average wind measured at about 33 ft (10 meters) above the surface.
tropical depression, wind speed, weather definition
Understand Tropical Storm Definitions
Reference Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Definition: A tropical storm is an organized system of strong thunderstorms with a defined surface circulation and maximum sustained winds of 39-73 MPH (34-63 knots).
tropical storm, wind speed, weather definition

Earthquake Response (6) -

Indoor Safety During an Earthquake
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
During an earthquake, if you are indoors:

1. Take cover under a sturdy desk, table, or bench or against an inside wall and hold on.
2. If no table is available, cover your face and head with your arms and crouch in an inside corner of...
During an earthquake, if you are indoors:

1. Take cover under a sturdy desk, table, or bench or against an inside wall and hold on.
2. If no table is available, cover your face and head with your arms and crouch in an inside corner of the building.
3. Stay away from glass, windows, outside doors, walls, and anything that could fall, such as lighting fixtures or furniture.
4. Stay in bed if you are there when the earthquake strikes—hold on and protect your head with a pillow unless you are under a heavy light fixture that could fall. In that case, move to the nearest safe place.
5. Use a doorway for shelter only if it is close by and strongly supported, as not all doorways are safe.
6. Stay inside until shaking stops and it is safe to go outside. Most injuries occur when people enter or exit buildings during shaking.
7. Be aware that electricity may go out, sprinkler systems or fire alarms may activate, and do not use elevators.
indoor earthquake safety, stay inside, avoid glass
Outdoor Safety During an Earthquake
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
During an earthquake, if you are outdoors:

1. Stay where you are and move away from buildings, streetlights, and utility wires.
2. If you are in a moving vehicle:
- Stop as quickly as safety permits and stay inside the vehicle.
-...
During an earthquake, if you are outdoors:

1. Stay where you are and move away from buildings, streetlights, and utility wires.
2. If you are in a moving vehicle:
- Stop as quickly as safety permits and stay inside the vehicle.
- Avoid stopping near or under buildings, trees, overpasses, or utility wires.
- Proceed cautiously after the earthquake has stopped, watching for road and bridge damage.
outdoor earthquake safety, vehicle during quake
Surviving Trapped Under Debris During an Earthquake
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
If you are trapped under debris during an earthquake, follow these steps:

1. Do not light a match or use any open flame.
2. Avoid moving about or kicking up dust, as this can make it harder for rescuers to locate you.
3. **Cover your...
If you are trapped under debris during an earthquake, follow these steps:

1. Do not light a match or use any open flame.
2. Avoid moving about or kicking up dust, as this can make it harder for rescuers to locate you.
3. Cover your mouth with a handkerchief or clothing to avoid inhaling dust.
4. Tap on pipes or walls so rescuers can hear you. If available, use a whistle instead of shouting.
5. Shout only as a last resort, as it may cause you to inhale dangerous amounts of dust.
trapped under debris, earthquake survival
Identify and Practice Safe Spots During an Earthquake
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Before an earthquake, identify safe spots in each room:

1. Locate sturdy tables or inside walls where you can take cover during shaking.
2. Reinforce this information by practicing drills with your family members, using the **Drop,...
Before an earthquake, identify safe spots in each room:

1. Locate sturdy tables or inside walls where you can take cover during shaking.
2. Reinforce this information by practicing drills with your family members, using the Drop, Cover, and Hold On technique.
3. During an earthquake, move only a few steps to reach a safe place, and stay indoors until the shaking stops and it is safe to exit.
safe spots, earthquake drills, drop cover hold on
Be Prepared for Aftershocks After an Earthquake
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
After the main earthquake, be prepared for aftershocks:

1. Aftershocks are usually less violent than the main quake but can still cause damage to weakened structures.
2. Stay alert and remain in a safe location until all shaking has...
After the main earthquake, be prepared for aftershocks:

1. Aftershocks are usually less violent than the main quake but can still cause damage to weakened structures.
2. Stay alert and remain in a safe location until all shaking has ceased.
3. Be ready to take cover again if additional tremors occur.
aftershocks, earthquake safety
Emergency Response to an EMS 8 Earthquake in the UK
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Scenario: An earthquake with intensity up to EMS 8 occurs in the UK, causing:

• Damage to buildings and infrastructure.
• A small number of fatalities or casualties due to falling masonry or interior damage.
• Moderate to severe damage to...
Scenario: An earthquake with intensity up to EMS 8 occurs in the UK, causing:

• Damage to buildings and infrastructure.
• A small number of fatalities or casualties due to falling masonry or interior damage.
• Moderate to severe damage to structures (e.g., extensive cracks in walls, collapse of chimneys).
• Short-term disruption to infrastructure, transport, and communications.

Key Actions for Emergency Response:
1. Immediate Evacuation and Safety Measures:
- Ensure all individuals are moved away from damaged or potentially collapsing structures.
- Avoid entering buildings that may be unstable.
2. Assessment of Damage:
- Conduct safety inspections on high-consequence structures such as:
- Nuclear power plants.
- Dams and reservoirs.
- Bridges and tunnels.
3. Infrastructure and Utility Management:
- Monitor for potential disruptions to:
- Power outages (low likelihood but possible).
- Transport networks.
- Communications systems.
4. Emergency Coordination:
- Coordinate with the British Geological Survey (BGS) for seismic data and hazard assessments.
- Use BGS’s Seismic Monitoring and Information Service to plan future responses.
5. Public Communication:
- Provide clear, timely updates on the situation, safety measures, and recovery efforts.
EMS 8 earthquake response, building damage assessment, infrastructure inspection, emergency coordination, BGS seismic monitoring

Volcanic Eruption Response (6) -

Avoid Mudflows During a Volcanic Eruption
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: Be aware of mudflows and avoid areas near stream channels and low-lying areas.

Details:
• Mudflows can move faster than you can run or walk.
• Look upstream before crossing any bridge to check for approaching mudflow.
• Do not cross...
Action: Be aware of mudflows and avoid areas near stream channels and low-lying areas.

Details:
• Mudflows can move faster than you can run or walk.
• Look upstream before crossing any bridge to check for approaching mudflow.
• Do not cross a bridge if a mudflow is approaching.
• Avoid river valleys and other low-lying areas that are prone to flooding.
mudflow, volcanic eruption, stream channels, low-lying areas, bridge safety
Evacuate Immediately During a Volcanic Eruption
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: Evacuate immediately from the volcano area to avoid flying debris, hot gases, lateral blast, and lava flow.

Details:
• Leave the area as soon as an eruption is detected or warned about.
• Do not delay evacuation in hopes that...
Action: Evacuate immediately from the volcano area to avoid flying debris, hot gases, lateral blast, and lava flow.

Details:
• Leave the area as soon as an eruption is detected or warned about.
• Do not delay evacuation in hopes that conditions will improve.
• Follow official evacuation routes and instructions.
evacuation, volcanic eruption, debris, hot gases, lava flow, emergency response
Protect Yourself from Volcanic Ash Exposure
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: Wear long-sleeved shirts and long pants, use goggles, wear eyeglasses instead of contact lenses, and use a dust mask or hold a damp cloth over your face.

Details:
• Cover all exposed skin to protect against ash particles that can...
Action: Wear long-sleeved shirts and long pants, use goggles, wear eyeglasses instead of contact lenses, and use a dust mask or hold a damp cloth over your face.

Details:
• Cover all exposed skin to protect against ash particles that can cause irritation.
• Use eye protection to prevent ash from entering the eyes.
• Avoid wearing contact lenses as they increase the risk of eye injury.
• Protect your respiratory system by using a dust mask or covering your mouth and nose with a damp cloth.
volcanic ash, protective clothing, goggles, dust mask, respiratory protection
Responding to a Volcanic Eruption Impacting UK Airspace
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Monitor volcanic activity: Track seismic activity, gas emissions, and ground deformation in Iceland and other regions that could affect the UK.
2. Prepare for ash-rich eruptions: Develop contingency plans for scenarios...
Procedure:

1. Monitor volcanic activity: Track seismic activity, gas emissions, and ground deformation in Iceland and other regions that could affect the UK.
2. Prepare for ash-rich eruptions: Develop contingency plans for scenarios involving large ash clouds reaching high altitudes over UK airspace.
3. Coordinate with aviation authorities: Work closely with air traffic control to manage flight disruptions caused by volcanic ash.
4. Implement temporary flight restrictions: Enforce no-fly zones or route changes in areas affected by ash clouds based on meteorological data.
5. Provide passenger and freight updates: Inform passengers of delays, cancellations, or diversions due to volcanic disruption.
6. Support stranded individuals: Coordinate with international partners to assist UK nationals abroad and foreign nationals in the UK who may be delayed in returning home.
7. Monitor eruption duration: Track the length of severe and moderate disruptions (up to 15 days for severe, 10 additional days for moderate) during a 3-month eruption period.
8. Utilize early warning signs: Use volcanic earthquakes, seismic tremors, gas emissions, or glacial meltwater as indicators of potential eruptions.
volcanic eruption,ash cloud,flight disruption,stranded passengers,air traffic control,early warning signs
Stay Indoors During Volcanic Ashfall
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: Stay indoors until the ash has settled unless there is danger of the roof collapsing.

Details:
• Close all doors, windows, and ventilation systems (chimneys, furnaces, air conditioners, fans, etc.) to prevent ash from entering your...
Action: Stay indoors until the ash has settled unless there is danger of the roof collapsing.

Details:
• Close all doors, windows, and ventilation systems (chimneys, furnaces, air conditioners, fans, etc.) to prevent ash from entering your home.
• If you must go outside, clear heavy ash from flat or low-pitched roofs and rain gutters to avoid roof collapse.
• Avoid running car or truck engines as they can stir up volcanic ash that may damage vehicles.
ashfall, indoor shelter, roof safety, ventilation closure, vehicle usage
Drive Safely During Volcanic Ashfall
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: Avoid driving in heavy ash fall unless absolutely required.

Details:
• If you must drive during volcanic ashfall, keep your speed down to 35 MPH or slower.
• Be cautious of reduced visibility and the potential for engine damage from...
Action: Avoid driving in heavy ash fall unless absolutely required.

Details:
• If you must drive during volcanic ashfall, keep your speed down to 35 MPH or slower.
• Be cautious of reduced visibility and the potential for engine damage from ash particles.
• Ensure that your vehicle is equipped with a dust mask or face covering in case of exposure.
driving in ashfall, vehicle safety, speed limit, engine damage, visibility reduction

Earthquake Risk Assessment (6) -

Determine Possibility of Earthquake Hazard Occurrence at the County Level
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. In the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Hazus Earthquake Analysis, assess earthquake risk for every county.
2. Determine that all counties have some degree of earthquake risk, and therefore, all counties are...
Procedure:

1. In the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Hazus Earthquake Analysis, assess earthquake risk for every county.
2. Determine that all counties have some degree of earthquake risk, and therefore, all counties are deemed possible for earthquake occurrence.
3. This assessment is used to inform hazard preparedness and risk mitigation strategies at the local level.
earthquake risk,HAZUS analysis,county-level assessment
Use FEMA Hazus Earthquake Analysis for Expected Annual Loss Values
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Purpose: To extract and use the final Expected Annual Loss (EAL) values at the Census tract and county level from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Hazus Earthquake Analysis.

Steps:
1. Access the **FEMA Hazus Earthquake...
Purpose: To extract and use the final Expected Annual Loss (EAL) values at the Census tract and county level from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Hazus Earthquake Analysis.

Steps:
1. Access the FEMA Hazus Earthquake Analysis database or report.
2. Locate the Expected Annual Loss (EAL) values for each county and Census tract, categorized by consequence type (e.g., building value, population).
3. Use these EAL values to calculate or verify the Historic Loss Ratio using Equation 53 from the National Risk Index Technical Documentation.
4. Ensure that all data used is up-to-date and corresponds to the most recent hazard assessments.

Notes: The EAL values are critical for accurate risk modeling and should be reviewed carefully before use in any analysis or planning.
fema,hazus,expected annual loss,eal,risk modeling
Calculate Annualized Earthquake Loss Using Riemann Sums Method
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Collect Data: Gather exposure data, annualized frequency of earthquakes, and historic loss ratios at the Census tract and county levels.

2. Estimate Annualized Earthquake Losses: Use the Federal Emergency Management...
Procedure:

1. Collect Data: Gather exposure data, annualized frequency of earthquakes, and historic loss ratios at the Census tract and county levels.

2. Estimate Annualized Earthquake Losses: Use the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Hazus Earthquake Analysis data to estimate long-term earthquake losses for building stock in a specified geographic area.

3. Apply Riemann Sums Method:
- Average annual losses associated with various return periods.
- Calculate average annual losses for each of the eight return periods.
- Multiply the resulting losses by their differential probability to develop one loss value per Census tract.

4. Estimate Population Losses:
- Correlate building damage with casualty numbers and severity.
- Use this correlation to estimate population loss values based on earthquake impact.

5. Sum Total Expected Annual Loss: Sum the FEMA Hazus Earthquake Analysis loss values at the Census tract and county levels to calculate the total Expected Annual Loss as defined in Equation 54.

Notes:
• This method provides an annualized estimate of losses, not actual event-specific losses.
• Data sources include FEMA P-366: Hazus Estimated Annualized Earthquake Losses for the United States (2023).
earthquake, annualized loss, Riemann sums method, FEMA Hazus, Expected Annual Loss, Census tract, population loss estimation
Understand Earthquake Historic Loss Ratio Definition
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Definition: The Earthquake Historic Loss Ratio represents the percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to an earthquake occurrence, or the average rate of loss associated with an earthquake occurrence.

Purpose:...
Definition: The Earthquake Historic Loss Ratio represents the percentage of a location’s hazard exposure that experiences loss due to an earthquake occurrence, or the average rate of loss associated with an earthquake occurrence.

Purpose: This metric helps assess the risk and potential impact of earthquakes on buildings and populations in specific geographic areas.

Key Components:
• Expected Annual Loss (EAL): The average annual loss expected for a county due to earthquake events.
• Annualized Frequency: How often an earthquake event is expected to occur within a given area.
• Census Block Exposure Values: Sum of exposure values obtained from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) study, measured in dollars or people.
earthquake,historic loss ratio,natural disaster,risk assessment,fema
Calculate Area-Weighted Fishnet Earthquake Annualized Frequency for a Census Block
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the area-weighted fishnet earthquake annualized frequency for a specific census block, follow these steps:

1. Identify intersecting grid cells: Determine which fishnet grid cells intersect with the census block.
2. **Calculate...
To calculate the area-weighted fishnet earthquake annualized frequency for a specific census block, follow these steps:

1. Identify intersecting grid cells: Determine which fishnet grid cells intersect with the census block.
2. Calculate intersected area per grid cell: For each intersecting grid cell, compute the area (in square kilometers) where the earthquake probability is greater than 0 and overlaps with the census block.
3. Determine earthquake probability for each grid cell: Obtain the probability of an earthquake event for each intersecting fishnet grid cell.
4. Multiply intersected area by earthquake probability: For each grid cell, multiply the intersected area (in square kilometers) by the earthquake probability for that grid cell.
5. Sum weighted probabilities: Add together the results from step 4 for all intersecting grid cells to get the total weighted probability.
6. Divide by total census block area: Divide the result from step 5 by the total area of the census block (in square kilometers) to obtain the area-weighted annualized frequency of earthquake for that census block.
area-weighted, fishnet grid, earthquake probability, census block, annualized frequency
Understand Limitations of Earthquake Risk Assessment Methodology
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

To understand the limitations and implications of the current Earthquake risk assessment methodology, follow these steps:

1. Recognize that earthquake risk is based on probabilistic data for earthquake events, not...
Procedure:

To understand the limitations and implications of the current Earthquake risk assessment methodology, follow these steps:

1. Recognize that earthquake risk is based on probabilistic data for earthquake events, not historical records.
2. Understand that this means loss amounts and ratings may differ from expected figures when looking solely at historical records.
3. Be aware that the surrogate frequency for Earthquake measures the true annual frequency of a damaging earthquake (MMI6) occurring, which is different from how frequency is measured in other hazards where it refers to hazard occurrence regardless of damage.
4. Review additional assumptions and limitations detailed in Section 3.3 Assumptions and Limitations of the National Risk Index Technical Documentation for non-hazard-specific considerations.

Notes:
• This information helps users interpret the data correctly and understand potential discrepancies between modeled risk and actual historical outcomes.
• It is crucial for informed decision-making based on the National Risk Index.
earthquake risk limitations, probabilistic data, mmi6 frequency, hazard assessment

Heat Wave Risk Assessment (6) -

Aggregate Heat Wave Exposure Values at the Census Tract and County Levels
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Determine Building Value Exposed to Heat Waves at the Census Tract Level:
- Calculate the total building value exposed to Heat Wave event-days for each Census tract, measured in dollars.
- This is done by summing...
Procedure:

1. Determine Building Value Exposed to Heat Waves at the Census Tract Level:
- Calculate the total building value exposed to Heat Wave event-days for each Census tract, measured in dollars.
- This is done by summing the values of all buildings within each Census block that are exposed to Heat Wave events, across all blocks within the tract.

2. Determine Building Value Exposed to Heat Waves at the County Level:
- Repeat the process for counties, aggregating building values from all Census blocks within a county that are exposed to Heat Wave event-days.

3. Calculate Population Equivalence Value Exposed to Heat Waves at the Census Tract Level:
- Compute the total population equivalence value (in dollars) for each Census tract, based on the number of people exposed to Heat Wave events within each block, summed across all blocks in the tract.

4. Calculate Population Equivalence Value Exposed to Heat Waves at the County Level:
- Repeat the process for counties, aggregating population equivalence values from all Census blocks within a county that are exposed to Heat Wave event-days.

5. Determine Agriculture Value Exposed to Heat Waves at the Census Tract Level:
- Calculate the total agriculture value (in dollars) for each Census tract, based on the sum of all agricultural areas within each block that are exposed to Heat Wave events, across all blocks in the tract.

6. Determine Agriculture Value Exposed to Heat Waves at the County Level:
- Repeat the process for counties, aggregating agriculture values from all Census blocks within a county that are exposed to Heat Wave event-days.

Notes:
• These aggregated exposure values are used in conjunction with annualized frequency calculations to estimate potential economic and social impacts of future Heat Waves.
• The exposure values apply to both Injury and Fatality consequence subtypes, as outlined in Section 5.4.4.
heat wave, exposure calculation, census tract, county level, risk assessment
Calculate Annualized Frequency of Heat Waves at Census Block Level
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Collect Data on Heat Wave Event-Days:
- Gather historical data on the number of recorded Heat Wave occurrences, measured in event-days, over a period of record (e.g., 19.0 years).
- A Heat Wave event can last for several...
Procedure:

1. Collect Data on Heat Wave Event-Days:
- Gather historical data on the number of recorded Heat Wave occurrences, measured in event-days, over a period of record (e.g., 19.0 years).
- A Heat Wave event can last for several days or just one day; therefore, an event-day basis is used to better capture variability in duration between events.

2. Identify Intersection Between Heat Wave Event-Days and Census Block Polygons:
- Use geographic information systems (GIS) tools to determine the intersection of Heat Wave event-day polygons with Census block polygons.
- This step ensures that each Heat Wave event is mapped accurately at the Census block level.

3. Count Distinct Heat Wave Event-Day Polygons per Census Block:
- For each Census block, count how many distinct Heat Wave event-day polygons intersect with it over the period of record.
- This count represents the number of times a Heat Wave occurred in that area on an annualized basis.

4. Calculate Annualized Frequency at the Census Block Level:
- Use Equation 64 to calculate the annualized frequency of Heat Wave event-days for each Census block.
- This value is used as input into the Expected Annual Loss calculations, which are essential for risk assessment and planning.

5. Use in Expected Annual Loss Calculations:
- The annualized frequency calculated at the Census block level should be applied to relevant exposure values (e.g., building, population, agriculture) to estimate potential losses from future Heat Wave events.

Notes:
• This procedure is critical for risk modeling and disaster preparedness planning at both local and national levels.
• The use of event-day basis ensures that the frequency accounts for varying durations of Heat Waves.
heat wave, annualized frequency, census block, risk assessment, exposure calculation
Understanding Heat Wave Methodology Limitations
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Overview: This procedure outlines key limitations in the methodology used for assessing heat wave risks as presented by the US National Risk Index 2025.

### Key Limitations:
1. Inconsistent Reporting of Heat Events:
- National Weather...
Overview: This procedure outlines key limitations in the methodology used for assessing heat wave risks as presented by the US National Risk Index 2025.

### Key Limitations:
1. Inconsistent Reporting of Heat Events:
- National Weather Service Warnings and Advisories are issued based on regionally determined thresholds, which may lead to an inconsistent record of dangerous heat events across different regions (e.g., Florida vs. Alaska).
2. Data Suppression for Mortality Statistics:
- CDC Compressed Mortality data is suppressed at the county level or any geographic level where fewer than 10 reported fatalities occur.
3. Residence-Based Reporting of Mortality:
- Mortality statistics are based on the decedent’s residence, not necessarily the location where the fatality occurred.
4. Lack of Injury Data for Heat Waves:
- SHELDUS does not provide adequate injury information to distinguish an injury Historic Loss Ratio from a fatality Historic Loss Ratio.
5. ICD-10 Code Limitations:
- CDC WONDER ICD-10 Codes only consider the underlying cause of death, not contributing causes related to heat hazards.

### Implications:
• These limitations may affect the accuracy and completeness of risk assessments for heat wave events.
• Users should be aware that data suppression and reporting inconsistencies can lead to underestimation or misrepresentation of risks in certain areas.
heat wave, methodology limitations, risk assessment, data suppression, mortality statistics
Calculate Heat Wave Expected Annual Loss for Injuries
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the Heat Wave Expected Annual Loss for injuries within a specific Census block, follow these steps:

1. Identify the following variables from the data source:
- **Baseline population Expected Annual Loss value due to Heat...
To calculate the Heat Wave Expected Annual Loss for injuries within a specific Census block, follow these steps:

1. Identify the following variables from the data source:
- Baseline population Expected Annual Loss value due to Heat Wave for the Census block.
- Population value exposed to Heat Wave occurrences for the Census block.
- Heat Wave annualized frequency (event-days per year) for the Census block.
- Bayesian-adjusted population Historic Loss Ratio for Heat Wave in the Census block.

2. Use these variables in the calculation formula provided in Equation 69, which is used to determine the scaled population equivalence Expected Annual Loss injury value due to Heat Wave for a specific Census block.

3. Ensure that all values are up-to-date and sourced from reliable data sets such as SHELDUS (the National Multi-Hazard Database) or other official risk assessment tools.

4. The final result will be the scaled population equivalence Expected Annual Loss injury value due to Heat Wave for that Census block, which can then be used in further calculations.

5. This value is a critical component of Equation 70, where it is summed with fatality Expected Annual Loss values to determine the total population equivalence Expected Annual Loss value due to Heat Wave for the Census block (in dollars).
heat wave,expected annual loss,injuries,census block,risk assessment,national risk index
Estimate Historical Event-Day Count for Heat Wave Events
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To estimate the historical event-day count for heat wave events within a specific time range, follow these steps:

1. Extract the Heat Wave event-day count from SHELDUS using the intersection between the Heat Wave event-day polygons (from...
To estimate the historical event-day count for heat wave events within a specific time range, follow these steps:

1. Extract the Heat Wave event-day count from SHELDUS using the intersection between the Heat Wave event-day polygons (from 2005-2024) and the Census block polygons used to calculate exposure and annualized frequency.
2. Calculate an annual rate by dividing the event-day count by the period of record (19.0 years).
3. Multiply this annual rate by the SHELDUS period of record (28 years) to estimate a historical event-day count for the appropriate time range.
4. If the number of Heat Wave event-day records from SHELDUS is less than the scaled event-day count for the county, insert zero-loss records equal to the difference into the Loss Ratio per Basis table with zero values for the consequence ratios.

> Note: This process ensures that the event count in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation aligns with the historical event count experienced within the SHELDUS period of record (1996 through 2023).
event-day count, historical event, sheldus, annual rate, census blocks
Heat Wave Hazard Data Collection and Analysis Procedure
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Objective: Collect and analyze heat wave hazard data for risk assessment.

Steps:
1. Data Sources: Use the following sources:
- Annualized Frequency: Iowa Environmental Mesonet (2005-2024)
- Exposure Extent: Iowa Environmental...
Objective: Collect and analyze heat wave hazard data for risk assessment.

Steps:
1. Data Sources: Use the following sources:
- Annualized Frequency: Iowa Environmental Mesonet (2005-2024)
- Exposure Extent: Iowa Environmental Mesonet
- Historic Loss Ratio: SHELDUS (1996-2023)
2. Hazard Characteristics:
- Hazard Type: Heat Wave
- Consequence Types: Population, Building, Agriculture
- Annualized Frequency Period of Record: 2005-2024
- Exposure Type: Developed area and agriculture density applied to the area of the average hazard event
3. Loss Allocation Parameters:
- Loss Allocation Date Expansion: Yes (maximum of 31 days)
- Loss Aggregation: Single day
- Zero-Loss Record Padding: Yes (Period of Record: 2005-2024)
4. **Bayesian Settings for Agriculture:
- County, Surrounding Area - 208km, Census Regions, National:
- Filter: N/A
- Set Usage Threshold: 25 Loss Ratio per Basis records in Non-Zero Pad Record Count mode (for some settings)
- SHELDUS back testing applied: Yes
- Smoothing applied: Yes - exponential
5. Filter Settings:
- LCMAs & Non-LCMAs
- Set Usage Threshold: N/A for some settings
6. **Data Processing:
- Ensure data is processed with exponential smoothing and SHELDUS back testing.
7. **Record Padding:
- Apply zero-loss record padding to ensure completeness of historical records.
heat wave hazard, data collection, risk assessment, Iowa Environmental Mesonet, SHELDUS, Bayesian settings

Ice Storms (6) -

Calculate Annualized Frequency of Ice Storm Event-Days for a Census Block
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the annualized frequency of ice storm event-days for a census block, follow these steps:

1. Prepare Data: Obtain historical data on ice storm footprints and their corresponding duration days from pre-processed source data.
2....
To calculate the annualized frequency of ice storm event-days for a census block, follow these steps:

1. Prepare Data: Obtain historical data on ice storm footprints and their corresponding duration days from pre-processed source data.
2. Create Fishnet Grid: Generate a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid over the area of interest.
3. Intersect Footprint Polygons with Grid Cells: For each ice storm footprint polygon, intersect it with the fishnet grid cells to determine which grid cells are affected by the event.
4. Sum Event-Days per Grid Cell: Calculate and record the total number of ice storm event-days for each grid cell that is intersected by any footprint polygon.
5. Determine Census Block Affiliation: Identify which census blocks intersect with each fishnet grid cell.
6. Transfer Event-Day Count to Census Blocks: Each census block inherits the aggregated event-day count from the grid cell that encompasses it.
7. Handle Multiple Grid Cells: If a census block intersects multiple grid cells, calculate an area-weighted average of the event-day counts from those grid cells (see Appendix D – Fishnet Occurrence Count for details on this calculation).
8. Calculate Annualized Frequency: Use Equation 82 to compute the annualized frequency as follows:

$$\text{Annualized Frequency} = \frac{\text{Total Event-Days for Census Block}}{\text{Period of Record (67.16 years)}}$$

Where:
• Annualized Frequency is the area-weighted annualized frequency of ice storm event-days determined for a specific census block (event-days per year).
• Total Event-Days for Census Block is the number of historical ice storm event-days calculated for the census block.
• Period of Record is 67.16 years, representing the total duration over which historical data was collected.
annualized frequency, ice storm event-days, census block, fishnet grid, area-weighted average
Calculate Building Expected Annual Loss Rate for a Community
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the Building Expected Annual Loss Rate for a community based on ice storm data:

1. Determine the total Expected Annual Loss (EAL) to ice storms for buildings in the community: This is calculated by summing losses across all...
To calculate the Building Expected Annual Loss Rate for a community based on ice storm data:

1. Determine the total Expected Annual Loss (EAL) to ice storms for buildings in the community: This is calculated by summing losses across all census blocks within the community.
2. Identify the total building value of the community: This includes the combined value of all structures within the area.
3. Divide the EAL for buildings by the total building value to get the expected annual loss rate (in dollars per dollar of building value).

This metric helps assess how much damage is expected relative to the overall value of infrastructure in a given community.
building loss rate, ice storms, calculation
Calculate Expected Annual Loss to Ice Storms for a County
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To calculate the Expected Annual Loss to ice storms for a specific county, follow these steps:

1. Identify the total Expected Annual Loss (EAL) for each census block within the county: This includes both building and population equivalence...
To calculate the Expected Annual Loss to ice storms for a specific county, follow these steps:

1. Identify the total Expected Annual Loss (EAL) for each census block within the county: This includes both building and population equivalence losses.
2. Sum all individual EAL values across all census blocks in the county: This will give you the total expected annual loss to ice storms for the entire county, measured in dollars.
3. Use the formula provided in Equation 86 (as described in the source document) to compute the final value.

> Note: The exact formula is not visible due to omitted images; however, it involves summing building and population equivalence losses across all blocks within a county.

This calculation helps assess risk exposure for communities based on historical ice storm data.
expected annual loss, county, ice storms, calculation
Determine Population Equivalence Value Exposed to Ice Storm Event-Days at the Census Tract Level
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To determine the population equivalence value exposed to ice storm event-days at the census tract level, follow these steps:

1. Identify Population Data for Each Census Block: Locate and quantify the population equivalence value (a measure...
To determine the population equivalence value exposed to ice storm event-days at the census tract level, follow these steps:

1. Identify Population Data for Each Census Block: Locate and quantify the population equivalence value (a measure representing the number of people in terms of their impact or exposure) for each census block within a specific census tract.
2. Calculate Total Population Equivalence Value per Census Tract: Sum the values of all population equivalences across all census blocks that make up the census tract to determine the total population equivalence value exposed to ice storm event-days at the census tract level.
3. Record Exposure Data: Document the summed value of all population equivalence exposed to ice storms for each census block within the census tract (in dollars) as a reference for future risk assessments and planning.
population equivalence, census tract, ice storm exposure, population valuation
Recognize Data Age Limitations in Ice Storm Occurrences
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
The underlying data for ice storm occurrences used in the National Risk Index is sourced from the United States Army Corps of Engineers’ Cold Regions Research and Engineering Laboratory, but it has not been updated since 2014:

• This means that...
The underlying data for ice storm occurrences used in the National Risk Index is sourced from the United States Army Corps of Engineers’ Cold Regions Research and Engineering Laboratory, but it has not been updated since 2014:

• This means that any changes to trends in frequency or geographic extent of ice storms from 2014 to the present are not reflected in current loss estimations.
• Users should be aware that risk assessments based on this data may not fully represent current conditions due to outdated information.

> This limitation is important for users relying on these figures for planning or mitigation efforts.
data age, ice storm trends, outdated information
Understand Limitations of Ice Storm Data in SHELDUS Database
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
The SHELDUS database includes multiple peril hazards that may be classified as either ice storms or winter weather, which can lead to ambiguity in categorization:

• The current methodology defines and separates these hazards based on the data...
The SHELDUS database includes multiple peril hazards that may be classified as either ice storms or winter weather, which can lead to ambiguity in categorization:

• The current methodology defines and separates these hazards based on the data development team's criteria.
• However, this classification may differ from users' conceptualizations or expectations.

> This means that some losses attributed to ice storms might be overlapping with other winter weather events, potentially affecting the accuracy of risk assessments.
data limitations, sheldus database, ice storm classification

Heat Stress (5) -

WBGT Kit Setup and Use (NSN 6665-00159–2218)
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Kit Components:
• Aluminum case with three thermometers:
- Stationary wet bulb thermometer exposed to the sun and prevailing wind.
- “Black globe” thermometer with a black sheath over the bulb, inside a transparent perforated plastic...
Kit Components:
• Aluminum case with three thermometers:
- Stationary wet bulb thermometer exposed to the sun and prevailing wind.
- “Black globe” thermometer with a black sheath over the bulb, inside a transparent perforated plastic shield.
- Dry bulb thermometer with its bulb shielded from direct sunlight by a white-painted shield.

Setup Instructions:
1. Attach the kit to a standard lightweight photographers tripod using the threaded hole in the bottom of the case (tripod not included).
2. Place the kit so that the thermometers are facing the sun, with the “black globe” thermometer closest to the sun.
3. Use the attached slide rule and readings from the three different thermometers to determine the WBGTI.
wbgt kit setup, heat stress meter use
WBGT Meter (NSN 7G 6685-01-055-5298) Setup and Use
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Instrument Description:
• Also known as the Heat Stress Meter, this is a compact electronic instrument that independently measures:
- Dry-bulb temperature
- Wet-bulb temperature
- Globe temperature

Functionality:
• The meter...
Instrument Description:
• Also known as the Heat Stress Meter, this is a compact electronic instrument that independently measures:
- Dry-bulb temperature
- Wet-bulb temperature
- Globe temperature

Functionality:
• The meter displays each of these values individually.
• It also computes and displays the WBGTI automatically.

Usage Guidelines:
• Place the instrument in an open area where it is not shielded from sun or wind.
• Ensure that the globe portion of the device is positioned to capture accurate radiation measurements.
• Use the displayed WBGTI values for planning and monitoring heat stress exposure during training.
wbgt meter setup, heat stress monitor use
WBGTI Calculation Formula
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Formula:

The Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature Index (WBGTI) is calculated as follows:

• Dry-Bulb Temperature × 0.1
• Wet-Bulb Temperature × 0.7
• Globe Temperature × 0.2

Total: WBGTI = Sum of the above values.

**Usage...
Formula:

The Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature Index (WBGTI) is calculated as follows:

• Dry-Bulb Temperature × 0.1
• Wet-Bulb Temperature × 0.7
• Globe Temperature × 0.2

Total: WBGTI = Sum of the above values.

Usage Guidelines:
• Readings should be taken every hour on the hour from 0800 to 1700 (local time) or until training is completed, whichever comes first.
• All thermometer readings must be taken and recorded at the same time.
• Training programs in warm weather should be planned provisionally based on WBGTI values.
wbgti formula, heat stress index calculation
Assemble WBGT Station
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Materials Required:
• Clamps
• Stoppers
• Flasks (not explicitly detailed but required)
• Shelter Instrument Thermoscreen (Stock Number: 5410-00-267-8898, ML-41)
• Copper Hemispheres (6 inches in diameter), obtained in pairs from Arthur Harris...
Materials Required:
• Clamps
• Stoppers
• Flasks (not explicitly detailed but required)
• Shelter Instrument Thermoscreen (Stock Number: 5410-00-267-8898, ML-41)
• Copper Hemispheres (6 inches in diameter), obtained in pairs from Arthur Harris and Company
• Mercury Thermometers (30 cm length, range 30° to 150° F) from Nurnberg Thermometer Company, Inc.

Installation Steps:
1. Use the Shelter Instrument Thermoscreen as per specifications.
2. Mount the globe thermometer on a 6-foot vertical support with a horizontal arm about 36 inches long.
3. The globe should be suspended by a sturdy braided flexible wire from the outboard end of the horizontal arm.
4. Ensure the center of the globe is 48 inches from the ground and that the arm points south to avoid shadows.
5. Mount the wet-bulb thermometer on the same upright used for the globe thermometer, ensuring it is in an unshaded position exposed to natural air movement and solar radiation.
6. The dry-bulb thermometer must be placed inside the thermoscreen shelter.
7. Ensure all instruments are installed in a widely open area, with grass or gravel below the globe, avoiding asphalt surfaces.
wbgt station, installation, heat stress, thermometer setup
Maintain WBGT Station Instruments
Standard Source: NAVMED P 5010 US Navy Manual of Preventive Medicine
Globe Thermometer Maintenance:
• Keep the surface of the globe free of dust and streaks.
• Repaint the globe each year to maintain its matte black finish.
• After rain, remove the thermometer, turn the globe upside down to empty...
Globe Thermometer Maintenance:
• Keep the surface of the globe free of dust and streaks.
• Repaint the globe each year to maintain its matte black finish.
• After rain, remove the thermometer, turn the globe upside down to empty any water that may have leaked in.

General Maintenance:
• Ensure all instruments are installed in a widely open area with no obstructions from sun or wind.
• Avoid placing the globe over asphalt surfaces; prefer grass or gravel.
• The thermoscreen shelter must be kept clean and functional.
wbgt maintenance, globe thermometer care

Chemical Attack (5) -

Decontamination After Chemical Exposure
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation:
- If medical help is not immediately available, proceed with decontamination.
- Use extreme caution when helping others who have been exposed to chemical agents.

2. **Remove contaminated clothing...
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation:
- If medical help is not immediately available, proceed with decontamination.
- Use extreme caution when helping others who have been exposed to chemical agents.

2. Remove contaminated clothing and items:
- Remove all clothing and other items in contact with the body.
- Contaminated clothing normally removed over the head should be cut off to avoid contact with the eyes, nose, and mouth.
- Put contaminated clothing and items into a plastic bag and seal it.

3. Decontaminate hands:
- Wash hands thoroughly using soap and water.

4. Remove eyewear:
- Remove eyeglasses or contact lenses.
- Place glasses in a pan of household bleach to decontaminate them, then rinse and dry.

5. Flush eyes with water:
- Rinse the eyes thoroughly with clean water.

6. Wash face and hair:
- Gently wash the face and hair with soap and water before thoroughly rinsing with water.

7. Decontaminate other body areas:
- Blot (do not swab or scrape) contaminated areas with a cloth soaked in soapy water, then rinse with clear water.

8. Change into uncontaminated clothes:
- Change into clean clothing that has been stored in drawers or closets and is likely to be uncontaminated.

9. Seek medical attention:
- Proceed to a medical facility for screening and professional treatment.
decontamination, chemical attack, first aid, emergency response, hazardous materials
Detect Chemical Agents Using the Five Senses and General Indications
High Priority Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion and Recovery Multiservice Procedures
Procedure:

1. (1) Use smell to detect chemical agents: Many agents have little or no odor.
2. (2) Use sight: Many agents are colorless:
- (a) Look for yellow, orange, or red smoke or mist.
- (b) Look for oily, dark patches...
Procedure:

1. (1) Use smell to detect chemical agents: Many agents have little or no odor.
2. (2) Use sight: Many agents are colorless:
- (a) Look for yellow, orange, or red smoke or mist.
- (b) Look for oily, dark patches on leaves, ground, etc.
- (c) Observe gas that appears as a mist immediately after shell burst.
- (d) Note solid agents that have some color.
3. (3) Use sound: Muffled explosions may indicate chemical agent bombs.
4. (4) Use feel: Irritation to the nose, eyes, or skin and/or moisture on the skin are danger signs.
5. (5) Use taste: Strange taste in food or water indicates contamination.
6. (6) Watch for general indications: Tears, difficult breathing, choking, itching, coughing, dizziness.
7. (7) Observe wildlife: Presence of sick or dying animals may indicate chemical agents.
detecting chemical agents, signs of chemical attack, smell sight sound feel taste
Detect Chemical Agents by Sound and Feel
High Priority Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Procedure:

1. Detect chemical agents using the following methods:
- Sound: Muffled explosions are possible indications of chemical agent bombs.
- Feel: Irritation to the nose, eyes, or skin and/or moisture on the skin are danger...
Procedure:

1. Detect chemical agents using the following methods:
- Sound: Muffled explosions are possible indications of chemical agent bombs.
- Feel: Irritation to the nose, eyes, or skin and/or moisture on the skin are danger signs.
detect chemical agents, muffled explosions, skin irritation, moisture on skin
Protect Yourself from Chemical Agents
High Priority Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Procedure:

1. Use protective equipment.
2. Avoid contaminated areas by:
- Exiting contaminated area by moving crosswind.
- Selecting routes on high ground.
- Avoiding cellars, ditches, trenches, gullies, valleys, etc.
- Avoiding...
Procedure:

1. Use protective equipment.
2. Avoid contaminated areas by:
- Exiting contaminated area by moving crosswind.
- Selecting routes on high ground.
- Avoiding cellars, ditches, trenches, gullies, valleys, etc.
- Avoiding woods, tall grasses, and bushes as they tend to hold chemical agent vapors.
3. Decontaminate body and equipment as soon as possible by:
- Removing: Pinch-blotting.
- Neutralizing: Warm water.
- Destroying: Burying.
protect from chemical agents, decontamination, protective equipment, crosswind exit, high ground routes
Self-Aid in Chemically Contaminated Areas
High Priority Source: FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion Recovery
Procedure:

1. If a chemical defense ensemble is available:
- Use all protective equipment.
- Follow antidote directions when needed.
2. If a chemical defense ensemble is not available:
- Remove or tear away contaminated clothing.
-...
Procedure:

1. If a chemical defense ensemble is available:
- Use all protective equipment.
- Follow antidote directions when needed.
2. If a chemical defense ensemble is not available:
- Remove or tear away contaminated clothing.
- Rinse contaminated areas with water.
- Improvise a breathing filter using materials available (T-shirt, handkerchief, fabric, etc.).
self-aid chemical contamination, breathing filter, improvised protection, rinsing contaminated areas

Uxo Hazard (5) -

React to UXO Hazard
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Do not touch or disturb the UXO or any wires, parachutes, or anything attached or surrounding the UXO. Do not move any closer to the UXO.

If any peculiar smells, liquids, or dead animals are present, **chemical or biological agents may be...
Do not touch or disturb the UXO or any wires, parachutes, or anything attached or surrounding the UXO. Do not move any closer to the UXO.

If any peculiar smells, liquids, or dead animals are present, chemical or biological agents may be present; do mask and MOPP gear immediately.
UXO hazard, chemical agent, biological agent, MOPP gear, do not touch
Report the UXO Hazard Using the 9-Line Explosive Hazard Spot Report
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Report UXO hazard to your chain of command. You or your chain of command should request explosive ordnance disposal (EOD) support using the 9-Explosive Hazard Spot Report:

(1) Date and time of the fall, impact, or finding of the UXO...
Report UXO hazard to your chain of command. You or your chain of command should request explosive ordnance disposal (EOD) support using the 9-Explosive Hazard Spot Report:

(1) Date and time of the fall, impact, or finding of the UXO item(s).

(2) The exact location of item(s), including grid coordinate (8-digit minimum), landmarks, reference points, or street addresses.

(3) The name and organization of person reporting the incident, including call sign or phone number.

(4) Identify UXO by type (dropped, projected, thrown, placed) and subgroup.

(5) Any CBRN contamination present?

(6) What resources are threatened?

(7) How the UXO has affected unit mission.

(8) The safety measures that have been taken including the evacuation distances that have been accomplished.

(9) The requested priority for receiving EOD support (immediate, indirect, minor, no threat).

Be prepared to provide a guide for the EOD team.
report UXO hazard, EOD support, 9-line report, CBRN contamination
Mark the UXO Hazard
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Mark the location (without approaching any closer) with some sort of recognizable material such as white engineer tape, marking ribbon, clothing, or signs.

**Place marker above ground at waist level if possible. Note physical terrain features...
Mark the location (without approaching any closer) with some sort of recognizable material such as white engineer tape, marking ribbon, clothing, or signs.

Place marker above ground at waist level if possible. Note physical terrain features of the location and route back to the UXO in order for the EOD team to return to dispose of the UXO.
mark UXO hazard, EOD team, terrain features, marker placement
Brief Soldier for UXO Hazard Evaluation Task
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Tell the Soldier that he/she will be evaluated on his/her ability to properly recognize, react, mark, and report a UXO hazard and also determine the appropriate evacuation distance.

**The Soldier will identify the UXO by type and subgroup, take...
Tell the Soldier that he/she will be evaluated on his/her ability to properly recognize, react, mark, and report a UXO hazard and also determine the appropriate evacuation distance.

The Soldier will identify the UXO by type and subgroup, take appropriate actions to react to the hazards, and report the hazard using the 9-line Explosive Hazard Spot Report (formally the UXO Spot Report).
brief soldier, UXO evaluation task, 9-line report
Evaluation Preparation for UXO Hazard Recognition and Reporting
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Setup: Do not evaluate this task using live UXO. Use training items from your local installation training support center.

**Preliminary instruction of this task can be obtained by requesting support from the nearest Army EOD company. Evaluate...
Setup: Do not evaluate this task using live UXO. Use training items from your local installation training support center.

Preliminary instruction of this task can be obtained by requesting support from the nearest Army EOD company. Evaluate this task during any training exercise where UXO is likely to be encountered. Place UXO training aid near personnel, facilities, or equipment (within the Soldier's area of responsibility).

Soldier should identify UXO from a distance of 5 to 10 meters away or by using binoculars.
evaluation preparation, UXO training aid, EOD company support

Avalanche Survival (5) -

Actions to Take if Caught in an Avalanche
Critical Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
If caught in an avalanche, follow these steps:

1. Attempt to remove skis or snowshoes to reduce drag and allow for better movement.
2. Assess the best line of escape based on terrain and direction of the flow.
3. Delay departure if...
If caught in an avalanche, follow these steps:

1. Attempt to remove skis or snowshoes to reduce drag and allow for better movement.
2. Assess the best line of escape based on terrain and direction of the flow.
3. Delay departure if possible, allowing as much of the avalanche to pass you before moving.
4. Work toward the side of the flow where there is less force.
5. Swim out using a double action backstroke or roll away at a 45-degree angle to move upward through the snow.
6. Make a supreme effort to get to the surface as the avalanche settles.
7. Create an air space to breathe by moving your arms and legs to form a pocket of air.
8. Move toward the surface if possible, keeping your head above the snow.
9. Establish orientation once on the surface to determine direction and location.
10. Do not panic, as this can lead to exhaustion and poor decision-making.
avalanche survival, escape from avalanche, actions during avalanche
Avalanche and Ice Hazard Awareness
Critical Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Objective: Understand avalanche and ice hazards, their characteristics, dangers, and how to protect yourself from them.

Details:
• Avalanches can occur suddenly and are often triggered by natural or human factors.
• Ice hazards include...
Objective: Understand avalanche and ice hazards, their characteristics, dangers, and how to protect yourself from them.

Details:
• Avalanches can occur suddenly and are often triggered by natural or human factors.
• Ice hazards include unstable terrain, crevasses, and thin ice that may break under weight.
• Protection measures include:
- Avoiding areas with signs of recent avalanches or unstable snowpack
- Using proper equipment such as avalanche beacons, probes, and shovels
- Learning how to escape from an avalanche if caught
- Being aware of weather conditions that increase the risk of avalanches
avalanche hazards, ice hazards, survival in snow
Evaluate Avalanche Hazard Using the Four Variables
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Snow Pack:
- Assess whether the snow is capable of sliding.
- Look for weak layers in the snow pack that may not bond well with other layers.
- Estimate how much snow could be released if triggered.

2. Weather:
...
Procedure:

1. Snow Pack:
- Assess whether the snow is capable of sliding.
- Look for weak layers in the snow pack that may not bond well with other layers.
- Estimate how much snow could be released if triggered.

2. Weather:
- Determine if weather conditions are contributing to instability.
- Avalanches typically occur during or shortly after a storm due to new weight on the snow pack.
- Consider these factors:
- Precipitation (especially heavy snowfall)
- Wind loading (leeward slopes may be overloaded)
- Temperature changes (rapid warming, freezing, prolonged above-freezing periods)

3. Terrain:
- Analyze the slope and path of potential avalanches.
- Note if terrain is channeled or unchanneled, as this affects avalanche behavior.

4. Human Factor:
- Consider how human activities (e.g., travel, construction) may influence snow stability.
- Be aware that even stable conditions can be disrupted by human presence.
avalanche hazard evaluation, snow pack analysis, weather factors, terrain assessment, human factor
Identify Parts of an Avalanche
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Starting Zone:
- Locate the steep area (usually over 30 degrees) where snow begins to slide.
- This zone receives large amounts of snow and is where instability starts.

2. Avalanche Track:
- Identify the path...
Procedure:

1. Starting Zone:
- Locate the steep area (usually over 30 degrees) where snow begins to slide.
- This zone receives large amounts of snow and is where instability starts.

2. Avalanche Track:
- Identify the path between the starting zone and run out zone.
- Tracks can be channeled (e.g., gullies, couloirs) or unchanneled (open slopes with possible trees).
- Note that multi-branch tracks may cause multiple avalanches in quick succession.

3. Run Out Zone:
- Locate the area at the bottom of the path where snow and debris pile up.
- This zone is influenced by weather patterns, which can change its position from year to year.
- Be cautious as this is where avalanches come to rest after sliding down.
avalanche parts, starting zone, avalanche track, run out zone, terrain features
Recognize Signs of Avalanche Instability
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

Look for the following signs that indicate unstable snow conditions:

1. Recent avalanche activity on similar slopes or small avalanches underfoot.
2. Booming – an audible collapse of snow layers, indicating instability.
3....
Procedure:

Look for the following signs that indicate unstable snow conditions:

1. Recent avalanche activity on similar slopes or small avalanches underfoot.
2. Booming – an audible collapse of snow layers, indicating instability.
3. Visible cracks shooting out from underfoot, showing stress in the snow pack.
4. Sluffing debris, which is evidence of recent avalanche activity.
5. Sunballing – caused by rapid warming of the snow surface, leading to instability.
6. Weather patterns that contribute to instability:
- Heavy snow loading (e.g., 1 inch per hour for 24 hours)
- Heavy rains that warm and weaken the snow pack
- Significant wind loading causing leeward slopes to become overloaded
- Long, cold, clear, calm periods followed by heavy precipitation or wind loading
- Rapid temperature rises above freezing after a long cold period
- Prolonged periods of above-freezing temperatures (more than 24 hours)
- Cold snow temperatures (equal to or less than 25°F) that slow the strengthening process, allowing unstable conditions to persist
avalanche signs, instability indicators, snow pack instability, weather effects on avalanches

Shelter Operations (5) -

Establishing Mass Care Shelters in Radiation Emergencies
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Pre-Existing Plans: Jurisdictions should have pre-existing plans and procedures for establishing all-hazards, general population mass care shelters.
2. Mutual Aid Agreements: Neighboring jurisdictions should also have...
Procedure:

1. Pre-Existing Plans: Jurisdictions should have pre-existing plans and procedures for establishing all-hazards, general population mass care shelters.
2. Mutual Aid Agreements: Neighboring jurisdictions should also have plans to provide mutual aid to impacted jurisdictions, including provisions for mass care shelters for people evacuating the impacted area.
3. CDC Shelter Guide Considerations: CDC _A Guide to Operating Public Shelters in a Radiation Emergency_ recommendations may apply to emergency or temporary mass care shelters in areas with elevated radiation, but these types of mass care shelters are not the guide’s focus. The mass care shelters described in the CDC Shelter Guide are long-term mass care shelters in areas where radiation levels are at or near natural background levels.
4. Suitability of Facilities: Many organizations have mass care shelter plans for facilities in their communities. Depending on the nature of the radiation emergency, some of these facilities may not be suitable locations for mass care shelter operations due to utility outages, infrastructure damage, or elevated environmental radiation levels.
5. Environmental Radiation Levels: Mass care shelters should be established in uncontaminated or low background radiation areas with environmental radiation levels below 1 µSv/h (0.1 mR/hr).
6. Access to Maps and Guidance: In the first 24‒48 hours after the incident, emergency managers and radiation control officials are likely to have access to detailed maps identifying radiation control zones, and they can help mass care shelter operators determine if their existing or proposed mass care shelter locations are in low background radiation areas.
7. Relocation Plans: Mass care shelters may be considered short-term operations initially until environmental monitoring activities near the incident site are completed and radiation control zones are established. Mass care shelters require relocation plans in the event they must move to lower background radiation areas.
8. ADA Compliance: Mass care shelters must prepare for residents with disabilities, functional needs, or access needs in accordance with the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990 (ADA). For examples of how to make mass care shelters more accessible, visit the CDC’s Disability and Health Emergency Preparedness page, particularly the section on Resources to Assess Shelters.
9. Animal Considerations: Planners should also reference Appendix 5.3: Screening and Decontaminating Service Animals and Pets, to develop plans for people arriving with animals.
mass care shelters,radiation emergency,ADA compliance,animal screening,shelter relocation
Identifying Alternate Mass Care Shelter Locations in Radiation Emergencies
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Assess Existing Locations: If previously established mass care shelter locations are not suitable due to their proximity to the impacted areas, identify alternate locations.
2. Consider Environmental Radiation Levels:...
Procedure:

1. Assess Existing Locations: If previously established mass care shelter locations are not suitable due to their proximity to the impacted areas, identify alternate locations.
2. Consider Environmental Radiation Levels: Ensure that alternate locations are in uncontaminated or low background radiation areas with environmental radiation levels below 1 µSv/h (0.1 mR/hr).
3. Use Available Maps and Guidance: In the first 24‒48 hours after the incident, emergency managers and radiation control officials can provide detailed maps identifying radiation control zones to help determine suitable shelter locations.
4. Coordinate with Radiation Control Officials: Work closely with radiation control officials to ensure that alternate shelter locations are safe and meet all necessary criteria for operation.
5. Plan for Relocation: Develop relocation plans in case the initial shelter location becomes unsuitable due to elevated radiation levels or other hazards.
alternate shelter locations,radiation control zones,shelter relocation,environmental radiation assessment
Coordinate Evacuee Decontamination and Monitoring
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure for Coordinating Evacuee Decontamination and Monitoring:

1. Ensure coordination among congregate care facilities and mass care operations for evacuees who have been monitored and, if necessary, decontaminated.

2. Identify a system or...
Procedure for Coordinating Evacuee Decontamination and Monitoring:

1. Ensure coordination among congregate care facilities and mass care operations for evacuees who have been monitored and, if necessary, decontaminated.

2. Identify a system or method used to track evacuees (and where applicable, service animals, pets, and vehicles) who had been monitored, decontaminated as appropriate, and registered.

• This identifier should be clearly visible and accessible for all relevant staff and facilities.

3. Establish clear communication channels between monitoring/decontamination sites and congregate care facilities to ensure seamless transfer of information and individuals.
evacuee,decontamination,monitoring,registration,shelter operations
Establish Shelter Operations for Evacuees
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure for Establishing Shelter Operations:

1. Determine if evacuees, service animals, and pets have been monitored for contamination, decontaminated as appropriate, and registered before entering the facility.

2. Ensure that staff check...
Procedure for Establishing Shelter Operations:

1. Determine if evacuees, service animals, and pets have been monitored for contamination, decontaminated as appropriate, and registered before entering the facility.

2. Ensure that staff check for arriving individuals’ confirmation of monitoring/decontamination.

3. Confirm that the Office of Response and Recovery (ORO) has appropriately planned for the population expected at this location.

• This includes assessing capacity, resource availability, and staffing needs.

4. Verify that congregate care centers and operations in host/support jurisdictions are sufficient to support the expected number of evacuees.
shelter operations,evacuee,decontamination,monitoring,resource planning
Manage Congregate Care Center Operations
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure for Managing Congregate Care Center Operations:

1. Identify the agency or agencies responsible for managing the congregate care center.

2. Determine the capacity of the congregate care center to ensure it can accommodate the expected...
Procedure for Managing Congregate Care Center Operations:

1. Identify the agency or agencies responsible for managing the congregate care center.

2. Determine the capacity of the congregate care center to ensure it can accommodate the expected number of evacuees.

3. Assess available resources for evacuees (real or simulated) arriving at the congregate care center, including:
- Food and water supplies
- Medical services
- Shelter space
- Communication tools
- Security personnel

4. Ensure that all necessary equipment and procedures are in place to support the operation of the facility.
congregate care,resource management,evacuee capacity,shelter operations

Storm Preparedness (5) -

Respond to Severe Storms in the UK
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Monitor Weather Warnings:
- Use the Met Office National Severe Weather Warning Service for advance notice of storms up to 7 days ahead.
- Pay close attention to warnings about wind speeds, directions, and potential...
Procedure:

1. Monitor Weather Warnings:
- Use the Met Office National Severe Weather Warning Service for advance notice of storms up to 7 days ahead.
- Pay close attention to warnings about wind speeds, directions, and potential hazards such as snow or falling debris.

2. Prepare for High Wind Speeds:
- Inland, lowland areas may experience mean wind speeds in excess of 55 mph with gusts over 85 mph.
- Secure loose objects outdoors to prevent them from becoming airborne during the storm.

3. Protect Property and People:
- Reinforce windows and doors if necessary to withstand strong winds.
- Ensure that all individuals, especially vulnerable members of the population, are safe indoors during the storm.

4. Plan for Disruption:
- Be prepared for potential power outages, transport disruptions, and school closures.
- Have emergency supplies on hand such as food, water, flashlights, and batteries.

5. Post-Storm Actions:
- After the storm has passed, check for damage to property and infrastructure.
- Report fallen trees or power lines to local authorities immediately.
- Be aware that recovery may take 1–4 days in urban areas and more than 5 days in remote rural locations.
severe storms, wind speed, emergency supplies, power outages
Assess Ramifications of Severe Thunderstorm Outage
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Step 1: Identify the potential effects of a thunderstorm with an electrical outage lasting between 2 (average) to 48 hours (severe).

Ramifications include:
• Food spoilage possible due to lack of refrigeration.
• **Lack of air...
Step 1: Identify the potential effects of a thunderstorm with an electrical outage lasting between 2 (average) to 48 hours (severe).

Ramifications include:
• Food spoilage possible due to lack of refrigeration.
• Lack of air conditioning/furnace may lead to uncomfortable or dangerous indoor temperatures.
• Damage to house or car from nearby trees falling during the storm.
• Possible local flooding if the storm is severe enough.
• Local transportation impaired by fallen trees, wires, making travel difficult or impossible.
• Lightning damage/fire potential could pose a direct threat to life and property.
thunderstorm,flooding,electrical outage,storm preparedness
Prepare for Severe Storms
High Priority Source: All Hazard Preparedness Workbook
Stay safe during severe storms by following these guidelines:

• Stay indoors during a severe storm if possible.
• If outdoors, take shelter wherever you can find it.
• Learn the meanings of weather terminology and listen to daily weather...
Stay safe during severe storms by following these guidelines:

• Stay indoors during a severe storm if possible.
• If outdoors, take shelter wherever you can find it.
• Learn the meanings of weather terminology and listen to daily weather reports.
• Pay special attention to forecasts if you have to drive during poor weather conditions.
• If there is a severe storm warning, avoid traveling until the storm has passed.
severe storms,weather reports,shelter,outdoors,travel warning
Storm Monitoring and Preparedness Procedure
High Priority Source: US Antarctic Continental Field Manual
Purpose: Prepare for potential storms by monitoring weather patterns.

Procedure:
1. Monitor surroundings and weather patterns daily for indications of coming storms.
2. Use available tools such as barometers, wind speed meters, or...
Purpose: Prepare for potential storms by monitoring weather patterns.

Procedure:
1. Monitor surroundings and weather patterns daily for indications of coming storms.
2. Use available tools such as barometers, wind speed meters, or satellite imagery to track changes in conditions.
3. If a storm is expected, implement the following:
- Secure all loose equipment
- Reinforce shelters
- Ensure communication systems are operational
storm monitoring, weather patterns, preparedness
Prepare for Storm-Related Infrastructure Damage
Standard Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Monitor weather forecasts closely to anticipate storm activity.
2. Coordinate with local authorities, industry, and government to prepare for potential damage.
3. Ready engineers and other workers for deployment.
4....
Procedure:

1. Monitor weather forecasts closely to anticipate storm activity.
2. Coordinate with local authorities, industry, and government to prepare for potential damage.
3. Ready engineers and other workers for deployment.
4. Cut down trees near infrastructure to prevent damage during storms.
5. Set up welfare stations for members of the public affected by the storm.
6. Prepare backup generators to reconnect small numbers of customers quickly after a power outage.
7. Tailor response strategies differently for urban and rural areas, considering population density and infrastructure dependence.
8. Coordinate with network operators and Strategic Coordinating Groups to provide welfare support to affected customers.
9. Provide enhanced support, such as alternative accommodation, for vulnerable individuals impacted by the storm.
storm preparation, infrastructure damage, welfare stations, backup generators, urban response, rural response

Levee Management (5) -

Evacuation Plan Activation for Levee Personnel
Critical Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Coordinate evacuation with police, fire department, and first responders immediately.
2. Follow the predetermined routes.
3. Meet in predetermined locations and immediately confirm the safety of all levee personnel.
evacuation plan,police coordination,safety confirmation
Maintaining Pumping Stations During Flooding Events
Critical Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Ensure competent pumping station operators are on duty whenever it appears that an operation is imminent during flooding events, even when station operation has been automated.
2. Operators should thoroughly understand the...
Procedure:

1. Ensure competent pumping station operators are on duty whenever it appears that an operation is imminent during flooding events, even when station operation has been automated.
2. Operators should thoroughly understand the manner in which the pumping station was designed to operate and be capable of manual operation should automated equipment or sensors fail.
3. Use portable pumps may be needed to pump water over the levee if water is ponding in undesirable areas or is rising too quickly in ponding areas.
pumping station operators,portable pumps,manual operation,flooding events
Continuous Patrol During High Water Events
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Patrol continuously, 24 hours per day, 7 days per week (as the situation requires), especially if there are ponding areas during high water.
2. Ensure all of the closures and gates are in place, and all maintenance is complete...
Procedure:

1. Patrol continuously, 24 hours per day, 7 days per week (as the situation requires), especially if there are ponding areas during high water.
2. Ensure all of the closures and gates are in place, and all maintenance is complete as described in Phase I: Preliminary Response Activities.
3. Remove padlocks from access gates to facilitate patrols.
4. Monitor inventory of flood fighting equipment, materials, and supplies as they are used.
continuous patrol,high water,padlock removal,equipment inventory
Monitoring and Maintaining Debris Basins and Trash Racks
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Monitor debris basins and trash racks for sediment and accumulated debris.
2. Remove debris from trash racks at pumping stations periodically when the station is in operation.
3. As debris and sediment continue to be...
Procedure:

1. Monitor debris basins and trash racks for sediment and accumulated debris.
2. Remove debris from trash racks at pumping stations periodically when the station is in operation.
3. As debris and sediment continue to be deposited in the basin, debris loads will substantially block racks, and sediment deposits will block the entrance to the basin, forcing the flow against the sides.
4. Any large accumulation of debris on racks or flow directed on the sides of basins will cause local erosion and scour.
5. Closely monitor levees and concrete structures that are part of the debris basin facility to ensure optimal performance.
debris basins,trash racks,erosion,monitoring
Assigning a Safety Officer for Public Interaction on Levees
Standard Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. If there are many observers on the levees or at floodwalls, assign an additional person to each patrol team to act as a safety officer.
2. The patrol team is not responsible for ordering observers off the levees and...
Procedure:

1. If there are many observers on the levees or at floodwalls, assign an additional person to each patrol team to act as a safety officer.
2. The patrol team is not responsible for ordering observers off the levees and floodwalls.
3. Each team should carry and distribute instruction cards that describe the community evacuation plan in order to inform observers of potential danger.
safety officer,public interaction,evacuation plan,instruction cards

Radiation Safety (5) -

Determining When It Is Safe to Go Outside After Fallout
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
The radiological team must determine when it is safe to leave the shelter:

• A person can withstand up to 6 R of radiation per day in a nuclear war context without serious ill effects.
• Once the outside radiation dose rate has fallen to...
The radiological team must determine when it is safe to leave the shelter:

• A person can withstand up to 6 R of radiation per day in a nuclear war context without serious ill effects.
• Once the outside radiation dose rate has fallen to 2 R per hour, it becomes generally safe to go outside for short periods.
• At this level, a person who has not been heavily exposed before can stay outside for up to three hours per day.
• As the dose rate continues to decrease, the time spent outside can be increased proportionately.

Note: Refer to Table 20 in Appendix F for more detailed information on safe exposure times.
safe exposure time, radiation levels, fallout safety
Checking Radiation Levels in Upper Bunks for Safety
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Title: Checking Radiation Levels in Upper Bunks for Safety

Procedure:
1. If using bunk beds in a shelter, check the radiation dose levels of the upper bunks.
2. Initially place all individuals on the floor until it is safe to sleep at...
Title: Checking Radiation Levels in Upper Bunks for Safety

Procedure:
1. If using bunk beds in a shelter, check the radiation dose levels of the upper bunks.
2. Initially place all individuals on the floor until it is safe to sleep at higher levels based on measured radiation exposure.
3. Refer to figure 16 (illustration of radiation levels in several basement areas) for guidance on determining safe sleeping heights.
