2. Protect mask filters: Cover the mask’s air inlets with gauze or your hands to keep the mask filters dry.

3. Decontaminate mask and face: Wipe the exposed areas of the patient’s face, including the neck and behind the ears. Do not remove the protective mask.
decon,decontaminate,mask,face,exposed skin,chemical agent
Remove Gross Contamination From the Patient's Protective Overgarment
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing Gross Contamination from the Patient's Protective Overgarment:

1. Use decon solution: Remove all visible contamination spots from the overgarment by using an SDK (preferred method) or a 0.5 percent chlorine solution.
overgarment,decon,gross contamination,SDK,chemical agent
Remove and Decontaminate the FMC (Face Mask Cover)
High Priority Source: FM 3 5 NBC Decontamination
Procedure for Removing and Decontaminating the FMC:

1. Cut the FMC tie wire: Cut the patient's FMC tie wire, allowing the FMC to fall into a plastic bag.

2. Seal the plastic bag: Seal the plastic bag and rinse the outside of the bag...
Procedure for Removing and Decontaminating the FMC:

1. Cut the FMC tie wire: Cut the patient's FMC tie wire, allowing the FMC to fall into a plastic bag.

2. Seal the plastic bag: Seal the plastic bag and rinse the outside of the bag with a 5 percent chlorine solution.

3. Secure the bag: Place the plastic bag under the back of the protective mask’s head straps.

4. Note: The FMC will remain with the patient in the contaminated area, and a clean copy will be made before the patient is moved to the clean area.
FMC,face mask cover,remove,decon,plastic bag,chemical agent

Radiation Protection (4) -

Prepare for an Imminent Nuclear Attack at Home
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Order of Importance:

1. Do not panic.
2. Think through the consequences of all your actions.
3. Gather your family together.
4. Turn on a television or battery powered radio (for official broadcasts).
5. **Begin taking a...
Order of Importance:

1. Do not panic.
2. Think through the consequences of all your actions.
3. Gather your family together.
4. Turn on a television or battery powered radio (for official broadcasts).
5. Begin taking a “Thyroid Blocking Agent (Potassium Iodide - KI)” follow the instruction on the bottle.
6. Go to the basement or lowest or center-most part of the house or building.
7. Build a shelter (if one does not already exist) - See preceding pages for sheltering materials.
8. Turn off the gas or propane.
9. Turn off the electricity.
10. Fill all tubs, basins, and empty containers (preferably with lids) with water.
11. Turn off the water.
12. Bring filled water containers and non-perishable foods into your shelter (enough for 3 weeks).
13. Bring sanitation measures and items into your shelter.
14. Bring clothing, bedding, and sanitary supplies into your shelter.
15. Close and lock all windows and doors.
16. Fill all basement window wells with sand, dirt, books, or any other dense material (either from the outside or inside on the window ledge depending on how much time you have).
17. Turn off and cover chimneys, air conditioners, vents, etc. to prevent fallout from coming inside.
18. Close all drapes, curtains and blinds to reflect the flash of the blast and discourage burglars.
19. Go to or send one person to the nearest food store with all available cash to buy all of the non-perishable food that they can, if you do not have a food storage in place.
20. Disconnect or lower all antennas over 12" and unplug all electric and electronic equipment.
nuclear attack, home preparation, radiation fallout, emergency sheltering
Minimize Radiation Dose from Fallout by Taking Shelter
Critical Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Procedure:

1. Identify a protective structure such as a building or shelter to take cover in during fallout exposure.
2. Enter the structure immediately to reduce external radiation exposure.
3. Close all windows and doors to...
Procedure:

1. Identify a protective structure such as a building or shelter to take cover in during fallout exposure.
2. Enter the structure immediately to reduce external radiation exposure.
3. Close all windows and doors to prevent radioactive particles from entering the shelter.
4. Remove contaminated clothing or skin if exposed, and wash thoroughly with clean water to stop further exposure.
5. Stay inside the shelter until official guidance indicates it is safe to leave.

> Note: Even buildings with broken windows can provide adequate protection from ground shine dose.

> Reference: Chapter 3 for more information on sheltering.
fallout, radiation exposure, sheltering, protective structure, ground shine dose
Detecting Fallout Particles
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
To detect the arrival of fallout particles:

1. Place a white plate or sheet of paper outside on the ground and check it every few minutes.
2. When fallout arrives, it will appear as a sprinkling of grit on the paper or plate.
3. At night,...
To detect the arrival of fallout particles:

1. Place a white plate or sheet of paper outside on the ground and check it every few minutes.
2. When fallout arrives, it will appear as a sprinkling of grit on the paper or plate.
3. At night, you can see the grains descending like snow through the beam of a flashlight.

Note: Although radiation is invisible, the fallout grains are not.
fallout detection, radiation monitoring, nuclear emergency, fallout particles
Determine Safe Radiation Dosage Levels for Humans
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
The following table outlines the maximum average radiation dosage (r.) that various organisms can withstand:

| Organism | Maximum Average Radiation Dosage |
|---|---|
| Animals & Plants | See below |
| Pig, Dog, Cat | 300 |
| Goat | 350 |
|...
The following table outlines the maximum average radiation dosage (r.) that various organisms can withstand:

| Organism | Maximum Average Radiation Dosage |
|---|---|
| Animals & Plants | See below |
| Pig, Dog, Cat | 300 |
| Goat | 350 |
| Human | 400 |
| Monkey, Mouse | 450 |
| Sheep | 540 |
| Fish | 550 |
| Cow, Horse, Rat | 630 |
| Rabbit | 800 |
| Chicken | 1,000 |
| Insect | 5,000 + |
| Turtle | 5,000 + |
| Bacteria, Virus | 100,000 + |
| Onions | 2,000 |
| Oats | 3,300 |
| Barley, Rye, Wheat, Corn | 4,300 |
| Fruits, Grasses | 5,000 |
| Potatoes | 12,000 |
| Cabbage, Spinach | 14,000 |
| Tomatoes | 15,000 |

Note: These values are for informational purposes only and should not be used as a guide for exposure in real-world scenarios. Radiation exposure can have long-term effects even below these levels.
radiation dosage, human radiation tolerance, nuclear fallout, radiation safety

Emergency Response (4) -

Contacting the Emergency Operations Center (EOC) in an Antarctic Emergency
Critical Source: US Antarctic Field Manual
Procedure for contacting the Emergency Operations Center (EOC):

1. Identify the emergency situation: Determine if it is an injury or illness, spill, aircraft mishap, vehicle accident, loss of shelter, or any other urgent issue.
2. **Contact...
Procedure for contacting the Emergency Operations Center (EOC):

1. Identify the emergency situation: Determine if it is an injury or illness, spill, aircraft mishap, vehicle accident, loss of shelter, or any other urgent issue.
2. Contact Central Comms: Reach out to Central Comms immediately for initial reporting.
3. Provide essential information:
- Your full name
- Phone number
- Current location (specific coordinates if available)
4. EOC activation: The EOC will be initiated by the NSF station manager upon receiving this information.
5. Situation classification and assessment: The EOC staff will classify the situation and gather additional details to assess needs, risks, and determine appropriate actions.
6. Initiate search-and-rescue (SAR) if necessary:
- If a SAR is required, it may involve the USAP SAR team and/or the Joint USAP SAR Team (JSART), which includes both USAP and Antarctica New Zealand (AntNZ) personnel.
7. Follow instructions: Cooperate fully with EOC staff and follow any guidance provided for your safety and the safety of others.
EOC, emergency contact, SAR, Antarctica, USAP, JSART
Decide to Stay or Change Location During Emergencies
High Priority Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
When an unexpected event occurs, take a few seconds to assess the situation and determine your next steps. Identify the type of event that has occurred and whether there is immediate danger, such as damaged buildings or downed power lines. The goal...
When an unexpected event occurs, take a few seconds to assess the situation and determine your next steps. Identify the type of event that has occurred and whether there is immediate danger, such as damaged buildings or downed power lines. The goal is to be safe and stay out of harm’s way.

Next, decide whether it is safe to remain in your current location. In some situations, staying where you are may be the best option, while in others, changing locations may be necessary. For example:

• If you are inside during a tornado, stay indoors and seek shelter immediately.
• In an active shooter situation, try to run to another location if it is safe to do so.
emergency response, stay or leave, tornado, active shooter
Implement Protective Actions for Populations in Plume Phase Exposure Pathways
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Implement precautionary protective actions, including evacuation or sheltering, for all populations within the plume and ingestion exposure pathway exclusion protection zones (EPZs). This includes individuals with access and...
Objective: Implement precautionary protective actions, including evacuation or sheltering, for all populations within the plume and ingestion exposure pathway exclusion protection zones (EPZs). This includes individuals with access and functional needs, students, and institutionalized individuals.

Steps:
1. Identify populations at risk within the plume and ingestion exposure pathway EPZs, including those with access and functional needs, students, and institutionalized individuals.
2. Determine the appropriate protective action decision (PAD) based on the nature of the hazard and the level of risk to the population.
3. Coordinate with relevant agencies and stakeholders to ensure that all populations are informed about the PAD and have access to necessary resources.
4. Execute the PAD, which may include:
- Evacuation: Moving individuals out of the EPZ to a safe location.
- Sheltering in place: Keeping individuals within their current location for protection from external hazards.
5. Ensure that protective actions are implemented in accordance with established protocols and guidelines (e.g., NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1, Rev. 2).
6. Monitor the effectiveness of the PAD and make adjustments as necessary based on ongoing risk assessments.
7. Provide support services such as medical care, food, water, shelter, and communication assistance to affected populations during implementation of protective actions.
8. Document all actions taken for accountability and future reference.

Core Capabilities Involved: Operational Coordination; Public Information and Warning; Environmental Response/Health and Safety; Critical Transportation; Health and Social Services; Housing; Natural and Cultural Resources; Planning.

Planning Reference: NUREG-0654/FEMA-REP-1, Rev. 2 (A.4, C.2.a, G.1, J.11, J.11.a, J.11.b, J.11.c, J.11.e, J.11.g, and O.1).
protective actions,evacuation,sheltering in place,epz,plume phase,nureg-0654,fema-rep-1
Responding to Emergency Warnings
High Priority Source: Be Prepared Estonia Crisis Guide Paasteamet ERB
Procedure:

• Always take warnings seriously and follow official instructions. They are prompted by real danger.
• If you are outdoors and hear a siren, go inside and stay there.
• Follow the media and other reliable sources of information.
• If...
Procedure:

• Always take warnings seriously and follow official instructions. They are prompted by real danger.
• If you are outdoors and hear a siren, go inside and stay there.
• Follow the media and other reliable sources of information.
• If an alarm sounds in the building you are in, exit the building or follow the instructions given on site.
• Any warnings from unofficial sources should be verified via official channels (e.g. the website or social media channel of the agency or institution dealing with the crisis).
• If you are given a warning, pass it on to others in the danger zone.
• If you receive no information on what to do in the event of danger or a warning, assess the situation yourself and act according to these guidelines.

Important:
• Emergency warnings may be issued via media (including network and social media), websites and social media channels of agencies and institutions, sirens, mobile phone calls and text messages, loudspeaker announcements, door-to-door notification, and the ‘Be prepared!’ app.
• Download the ‘Be prepared!’ app, which also operates offline.
emergency warnings, official instructions, siren response, media sources, verification of information, be prepared app

Tsunamis (4) -

Respond to Tsunami Warnings and Evacuate Immediately
Critical Source: DoD Emergency Preparedness Guide
Procedure:

1. If you are in a tsunami area and experience an earthquake:
- Protect yourself from the earthquake as outlined in the earthquake section of this guide.
2. When the shaking stops, if there are natural signs or official warnings...
Procedure:

1. If you are in a tsunami area and experience an earthquake:
- Protect yourself from the earthquake as outlined in the earthquake section of this guide.
2. When the shaking stops, if there are natural signs or official warnings of a tsunami:
- Move immediately to a safe place that is high and far inland as possible.
3. Listen to authorities for instructions but do not wait for tsunami warnings or evacuation orders.
4. If you are outside the tsunami hazard zone and receive a warning, stay where you are unless officials tell you otherwise.
5. If told to evacuate, leave immediately following marked evacuation routes (e.g., signs with a wave of water and an arrow pointing toward higher ground).
tsunami warning,evacuation,higher ground,emergency alerts
Survive in Water During a Tsunami
Critical Source: DoD Emergency Preparedness Guide
Procedure:

1. If you are in the water during a tsunami:
- Grab onto something that floats (e.g., a raft, tree trunk, dislodged floating door or piece of furniture).
2. If you are in an operational boat:
- Direct it toward the approaching...
Procedure:

1. If you are in the water during a tsunami:
- Grab onto something that floats (e.g., a raft, tree trunk, dislodged floating door or piece of furniture).
2. If you are in an operational boat:
- Direct it toward the approaching waves and head out to sea.
3. If you are in a harbor:
- Go inland immediately.
surviving tsunami,water safety,floating objects
Take Protective Measures During a Tsunami
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Objective: Ensure safety during a tsunami by taking immediate protective actions.

Procedure:
1. Monitor for warnings: Turn on your radio or use emergency alerts to stay informed about tsunami advisories, watches, and warnings.
2....
Objective: Ensure safety during a tsunami by taking immediate protective actions.

Procedure:
1. Monitor for warnings: Turn on your radio or use emergency alerts to stay informed about tsunami advisories, watches, and warnings.
2. Evacuate immediately if a warning is issued: Move inland to higher ground as soon as possible and remain there until it is safe to return.
3. Heed natural signs of danger: If you notice the water receding from the shoreline (a phenomenon known as drawdown), this is a critical warning sign that a tsunami is imminent. Leave the area immediately.
4. Avoid low-lying areas: Stay away from coastal regions, especially those less than 25 feet above sea level and within one mile of the shoreline, as these are at highest risk.

Hazards to be aware of:
• Drowning due to powerful waves
• Structural damage from tsunami impact
• Flooding and contamination of water supplies
• Fires caused by ruptured gas lines or tanks

Keywords: Tsunami safety, evacuation, warning signs, coastal hazards.
Tsunami safety, evacuation, warning signs, coastal hazards
Understand Tsunami Warning Levels
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Objective: Recognize the different levels of tsunami alerts and their implications.

Procedure:
1. Advisory: An earthquake has occurred in the Pacific basin that might generate a tsunami. This is not an immediate threat, but it indicates...
Objective: Recognize the different levels of tsunami alerts and their implications.

Procedure:
1. Advisory: An earthquake has occurred in the Pacific basin that might generate a tsunami. This is not an immediate threat, but it indicates potential risk.
2. Watch: A tsunami may have been generated, and it is at least two hours away from reaching the area under Watch status. Prepare for possible evacuation or relocation to higher ground.
3. Warning: A tsunami has been generated that could cause damage. Immediate action is required—evacuate all people in the warned area to higher ground as quickly as possible.

Keywords: Tsunami warning levels, advisory, watch, warning.
Tsunami warning levels, advisory, watch, warning

Weather Alerts (4) -

Understand Weather Alerts
High Priority Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

1. Learn the difference between a watch and a warning:
- A watch means conditions are favorable for a severe weather event to develop.
- A warning indicates that a severe weather event is imminent or already...
Procedure:

1. Learn the difference between a watch and a warning:
- A watch means conditions are favorable for a severe weather event to develop.
- A warning indicates that a severe weather event is imminent or already occurring.
2. Stay informed by listening to the radio, television, or using a weather alert app.
3. Follow instructions from local officials and avoid taking unnecessary risks during a watch or warning.

Important: Do not ignore warnings; they are issued for your safety.
weather watch,weather warning,emergency alerts
Register for Heat-Health and Cold Weather Alert Systems
Standard Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Access the alert registration system provided by UKHSA in collaboration with the Met Office.
2. Complete the registration process to receive impact-based alerts during summer (Heat-Health Alert System) and winter (Cold...
Procedure:

1. Access the alert registration system provided by UKHSA in collaboration with the Met Office.
2. Complete the registration process to receive impact-based alerts during summer (Heat-Health Alert System) and winter (Cold Weather Alert System).
3. Ensure all family members, especially vulnerable individuals, are registered for these alerts.
4. Keep contact information updated in case of changes in phone numbers or email addresses.
5. Review alert notifications regularly to stay informed about potential heatwaves or cold weather events.

Important: These systems provide critical early warnings that can help reduce the risk of health impacts and improve preparedness.
heatwave,cold weather,alert registration,UKHSA,Met Office
Handle Changes in National Weather Service Forecast Zones Over Time
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To manage changes in forecast zone definitions over time:

1. Recognize that the National Weather Service maintains a Change History log of modifications to Public Forecast Zones since 1997.
2. Access this file from NWS or contact them directly...
To manage changes in forecast zone definitions over time:

1. Recognize that the National Weather Service maintains a Change History log of modifications to Public Forecast Zones since 1997.
2. Access this file from NWS or contact them directly for historical data.
3. Note that the Change History log does not include pre- or post-modification shapes of forecast zones.
4. For cases where historical zone shapes are unavailable, use the simplified Mesonet boundary version shape as a substitute.
5. The differences in spatial resolution between Mesonet and NWS boundaries can be seen in Figure 24 (omitted from this text).

Note: Matching alert records to specific forecast zones at the time of issuance may require additional archival research.
forecast zone changes, NWS change history, Mesonet boundary, historical data
Understand Forecast Zone Composition and County Correlation
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
To analyze the relationship between National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones and counties:

1. Use the Zone-County Correlation File, available at [https://www.weather.gov/gis/ZoneCounty](https://www.weather.gov/gis/ZoneCounty).
2. This...
To analyze the relationship between National Weather Service Public Forecast Zones and counties:

1. Use the Zone-County Correlation File, available at [https://www.weather.gov/gis/ZoneCounty](https://www.weather.gov/gis/ZoneCounty).
2. This file helps identify which forecast zones have single-county coverage, sub-county zones, or cover portions of multiple counties.
3. Approximately:
- 70% of the zones are single-county coverage.
- 20% are cases where a county is subdivided into multiple zones.
- 10% are zones that span parts of multiple contiguous counties.
4. For forecast zones covering a single county, use the Topologically Integrated Geographic Encoding and Referencing (TIGER) 2021 county boundaries (or TIGER 2024 for Connecticut).

Note: This correlation is essential for accurate spatial analysis of weather alerts.
forecast zone composition, county correlation, TIGER boundaries, NWS zones

Flood Safety (4) -

Pump Out Flooded Basements Safely
High Priority Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
Procedure:
1. If your basement has flooded, pump out the water slowly, removing about one third of the water per day.
2. Avoid pumping out the basement while the surrounding ground is still saturated to prevent structural damage such...
Procedure:
1. If your basement has flooded, pump out the water slowly, removing about one third of the water per day.
2. Avoid pumping out the basement while the surrounding ground is still saturated to prevent structural damage such as wall collapse or floor buckling.
basement flooding,pumping water,structural damage
Seek High Ground During Flooding
High Priority Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
If you expect flooding, seek high ground immediately. Avoid basements and lower floors if trapped in a building. Do not climb into a closed attic as it may trap you if floodwater rises. Go onto the roof only if necessary and signal for help.
high ground, flooding, safety, attic, roof
Avoid Wading in Floodwater After a Flood
High Priority Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
After a flood, avoid wading in floodwater. It may contain dangerous debris and be contaminated. Underground or downed power lines can electrically charge the water.
floodwater, contamination, debris, safety
Do Not Enter Flooded Buildings Until Authorized
High Priority Source: FEMA Citizen Preparedness Guide
Do not enter flooded buildings until an authority figure tells you it is safe to do so.
flooded buildings, safety, authority, entry

Earthquake Recovery (4) -

Management of Aftershocks After Major Earthquake
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Monitor Seismic Activity: Continuously monitor for aftershocks following a major earthquake (magnitude 5 or higher).
2. Prepare for Disruption: Anticipate further damage and delays in recovery efforts due to potential...
Procedure:

1. Monitor Seismic Activity: Continuously monitor for aftershocks following a major earthquake (magnitude 5 or higher).
2. Prepare for Disruption: Anticipate further damage and delays in recovery efforts due to potential aftershocks.
3. Implement Safety Measures: Ensure that all temporary shelters, emergency services, and infrastructure are reinforced against future seismic activity.
4. Coordinate with Emergency Services: Maintain communication with emergency response teams to manage any additional risks posed by aftershocks.
5. Provide Public Information: Keep the public informed about the possibility of aftershocks and what actions they should take if one occurs.

Details:
• Aftershocks can last for weeks or months after a major earthquake.
• This procedure is critical to minimizing further harm and ensuring ongoing recovery.
aftershock management, earthquake recovery, seismic monitoring, public information
Post-Earthquake Safety and Inspection Procedures
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
After an earthquake, follow these steps:

1. Open cabinets cautiously and be aware of objects that may fall from shelves.
2. Stay away from damaged areas unless you have been specifically asked to assist by police, fire, or relief...
After an earthquake, follow these steps:

1. Open cabinets cautiously and be aware of objects that may fall from shelves.
2. Stay away from damaged areas unless you have been specifically asked to assist by police, fire, or relief organizations.
3. If you live in a coastal area, be aware of the possibility of tsunamis, also known as seismic sea waves (not tidal waves).
4. If a tsunami warning is issued, stay away from the beach and follow local authorities' instructions immediately.
post-earthquake safety, tsunami warning
Restoration of Essential Services After Earthquake
Standard Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Assess Damage: Conduct a thorough assessment of damaged infrastructure, including gas, water, electricity, and communication networks.
2. Activate Plans: Implement pre-established plans by government departments and Local...
Procedure:

1. Assess Damage: Conduct a thorough assessment of damaged infrastructure, including gas, water, electricity, and communication networks.
2. Activate Plans: Implement pre-established plans by government departments and Local Resilience Forums (LRFs) to restore essential services.
3. Specialist Intervention: Deploy specialist teams for repairs to power and communications networks that have been disrupted.
4. Mutual Aid Agreements: Utilize mutual aid agreements with neighboring local authorities or LRFs to provide additional support where needed.
5. National Support: If necessary, mobilize national resources such as specialist Fire and Rescue equipment held as national assets.

Details:
• This procedure ensures the restoration of essential services following an earthquake.
• Coordination between government departments and local authorities is critical for a swift response.
earthquake recovery, essential services restoration, LRFs, mutual aid agreements, specialist intervention
Temporary and Permanent Rehousing After Earthquake
Standard Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Assess Residential Properties: Evaluate residential properties for safety and habitability after an earthquake.
2. Identify Unsafe Properties: Determine which homes are unsafe or uninhabitable due to lack of access,...
Procedure:

1. Assess Residential Properties: Evaluate residential properties for safety and habitability after an earthquake.
2. Identify Unsafe Properties: Determine which homes are unsafe or uninhabitable due to lack of access, services, or structural damage.
3. Provide Temporary Housing: Arrange temporary rehousing for affected residents until permanent solutions can be implemented.
4. Rehouse Commercial Premises and Infrastructure: If necessary, temporarily relocate commercial premises, schools, or other infrastructure that has been damaged and deemed unsafe.
5. Coordinate with Authorities: Work closely with local authorities to manage the relocation process and ensure continuity of essential services.

Details:
• Vulnerable individuals, including children, may require additional support during this period.
• Mental health services should be made available for those affected by the disaster.
temporary rehousing, permanent rehousing, earthquake recovery, infrastructure relocation

Explosions (4) -

During an Explosion - Immediate Actions
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
- Get under a sturdy table or desk if things are falling around you.
• When they stop falling, leave quickly, watching for obviously weakened floors and stairways.
• As you exit from the building, be especially watchful of falling debris.
-...
- Get under a sturdy table or desk if things are falling around you.
• When they stop falling, leave quickly, watching for obviously weakened floors and stairways.
• As you exit from the building, be especially watchful of falling debris.
• Do not use elevators.
explosion response, emergency evacuation, avoid elevators
Surviving While Trapped in Debris After an Explosion
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
- If possible, use a flashlight to signal your location to rescuers.
• Avoid unnecessary movement so you don’t kick up dust.
• Cover your nose and mouth with anything you have on hand. (Dense-weave cotton material can act as a good...
- If possible, use a flashlight to signal your location to rescuers.
• Avoid unnecessary movement so you don’t kick up dust.
• Cover your nose and mouth with anything you have on hand. (Dense-weave cotton material can act as a good filter. Try to breathe through the material.)
• Tap on a pipe or wall so rescuers can hear where you are.
• If possible, use a whistle to signal rescuers.
• Shout only as a last resort. Shouting can cause a person to inhale dangerous amounts of dust.
trapped in debris, signaling for help, breathing protection
After Exiting a Building Following an Explosion
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
- Do not stand in front of windows, glass doors, or other potentially hazardous areas.
• Move away from sidewalks or streets to be used by emergency officials or others still exiting the building.
post-explosion safety, evacuation protocol
Responding to a Telephoned Bomb Threat
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
- Get as much information from the caller as possible.
• Keep the caller on the line and record everything that is said.
• Notify the police and the building management.
bomb threat, emergency response, recording call, notify authorities

Levee Emergency Management (4) -

Designate an Incident Commander for Levee Emergencies
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Identify and formally designate one individual as the Incident Commander responsible for managing all emergency response activities related to levees.
2. The Incident Commander may be a senior manager or another...
Procedure:

1. Identify and formally designate one individual as the Incident Commander responsible for managing all emergency response activities related to levees.
2. The Incident Commander may be a senior manager or another appropriate individual, depending on the levee system management structure.
3. Establish alternate individuals who can serve in place of the Incident Commander if they are unavailable or need relief due to prolonged emergencies.
4. Ensure that the emergency preparedness plan clearly identifies the Incident Commander and their alternates.

Additional Considerations:
• The Incident Commander is responsible for deciding when to activate the emergency preparedness plan based on factors such as potential flooding, structural failure of levees, or requests from State or local emergency management agencies.
• Once activated, the Incident Commander oversees the overall response effort and coordinates activities with other entities (e.g., State and local emergency management agencies, adjacent levee systems).
• In some cases, duties may be delegated to a liaison officer or public information officer, but the Incident Commander remains ultimately responsible.
• Levee owners and operators should build relationships with emergency management agencies and public safety officials before an emergency occurs.
incident commander,emergency response,levee system,emergency management,nims/ics
Establish Safety Responsibilities During Levee Emergencies
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Designate a safety officer who is responsible for all employee and public safety during levee-related emergencies.
2. Ensure that the safety officer reports directly to the Incident Commander.
3. The safety officer should be...
Procedure:

1. Designate a safety officer who is responsible for all employee and public safety during levee-related emergencies.
2. Ensure that the safety officer reports directly to the Incident Commander.
3. The safety officer should be trained in emergency response, risk assessment, and incident management.
4. Develop protocols for identifying and mitigating risks to employees and the public during an emergency.
5. Coordinate with other emergency management personnel (e.g., PIOs, liaison officers) to ensure that safety concerns are addressed promptly and effectively.

Additional Considerations:
• The safety officer must be included in all planning and response activities related to levee emergencies.
• Safety protocols should be reviewed and updated regularly as part of the emergency preparedness plan.
safety officer,emergency response,safety protocols,levee system
Assign and Train Public Information Officers for Levee Emergencies
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Identify individuals to serve as Public Information Officers (PIOs) during levee-related emergencies.
2. Ensure that PIOs have experience or training in effective communication, particularly in high-stress, emergency...
Procedure:

1. Identify individuals to serve as Public Information Officers (PIOs) during levee-related emergencies.
2. Ensure that PIOs have experience or training in effective communication, particularly in high-stress, emergency scenarios.
3. Designate PIOs as the primary point of contact for the public during an emergency related to levees.
4. Ensure that all messages issued by the PIO are accurate and timely to maintain public trust and prevent misinformation.

Additional Considerations:
• The PIO reports directly to the Incident Commander.
• Communication should be consistent with the overall response strategy and priorities established by the Incident Commander.
• In some cases, the PIO may also serve as a liaison officer or have other responsibilities, but their primary role is public communication.
public information officer,emergency communication,levee system,incident commander
Coordinate with Emergency Management Agencies Before Emergencies
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Levee owners and operators should establish relationships with State and local emergency management agencies, public safety officials, and other relevant entities before an emergency occurs.
2. Coordinate with these agencies...
Procedure:

1. Levee owners and operators should establish relationships with State and local emergency management agencies, public safety officials, and other relevant entities before an emergency occurs.
2. Coordinate with these agencies to ensure a smooth response during emergencies, including communication protocols, resource sharing, and joint planning efforts.
3. Identify points of contact within the State and local emergency management agencies who can be reached quickly in case of an emergency.
4. Participate in regular drills or exercises with emergency management agencies to test preparedness plans and improve coordination.

Additional Considerations:
• Coordination should include all relevant stakeholders, such as adjacent levee systems, government entities, and community organizations.
• These relationships should be documented and included in the emergency preparedness plan.
emergency management,coordination,levee system,preparedness planning

Radiological Incident Preparedness (4) -

Develop Dose Assessment Plans/Procedures
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Create detailed plans/procedures for dose assessment as required by Evaluation Criterion I.8.

Steps:
1. Describe personnel and equipment involved in dose assessment, including roles, responsibilities, and the types of...
Objective: Create detailed plans/procedures for dose assessment as required by Evaluation Criterion I.8.

Steps:
1. Describe personnel and equipment involved in dose assessment, including roles, responsibilities, and the types of equipment used (e.g., radiation detectors, dosimeters).
2. Document the dose assessment computer software to be used, including:
- Software name and version
- Documentation provided with the software
- Data input procedures for running the model
3. Include alternate calculation methods, such as hand calculations or manual estimation techniques, in case of software failure or unavailability.
4. Specify information/variables required to run the dose assessment model, ensuring all units of measure are clearly defined (e.g., Becquerels per cubic meter for airborne contamination).
5. Outline procedures for obtaining initial data, such as:
- Data from licensee monitors
- Inventory estimates or other available sources
6. Describe how field data will be used to verify and modify model results, including protocols for comparing real-world measurements with simulated outcomes.
7. Include procedures for comparing dose results with those of other organizations that perform dose assessments, ensuring consistency and accuracy across agencies.

Notes:
• These plans/procedures must address how activities will be carried out during the three phases of radiological incident management: early, intermediate, and late.
• Ensure alignment with EPA PAGs (Protective Action Guides) and FDA DILs (Dose Interpretation Levels) for accurate comparison.
dose assessment, radiological incident, EPA PAGs, FDA DILs, software documentation, field data verification
Developing and Modifying Sampling Plans for Radiological Incidents
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Establish a process for developing and modifying sampling plans:
- Ensure that the plan includes provisions for ongoing sampling throughout all three phases of a radiological incident (initial, intermediate, late).
-...
Procedure:

1. Establish a process for developing and modifying sampling plans:
- Ensure that the plan includes provisions for ongoing sampling throughout all three phases of a radiological incident (initial, intermediate, late).
- Initially focus on determining if populations have been evacuated from areas where public health and safety is impacted by contamination levels.
- Modify sampling plans as appropriate during the intermediate phase to include consideration of the human food chain.
- Further evolve sampling plans into decision-making on reentry during the late phase.

2. Identify laboratories capable of processing samples:
- Include a list of identified laboratories in the plan that can process radiological samples.
- Ensure these laboratories are capable of conducting all necessary types of analysis, including complex analyses such as strontium analysis.

3. Describe each laboratory’s sampling capability and capacity:
- Provide detailed descriptions of each identified laboratory's sampling capabilities, processing capacity, and expected processing timeframe for samples.
- Include any restrictions or limitations that may be posed by the designated laboratories (e.g., limitations on analyzing radiation levels of samples).

4. Update plans as needed:
- Continuously evaluate and update the sampling plan based on new data, evolving incident phases, and changes in laboratory capabilities.
- Ensure that additional analyses are included when necessary for decision-making on reentry or public safety.
sampling plan, radiological incident, laboratory analysis, public health, contamination levels, strontium analysis
Establishing Periodic Radiological Assessments for Public Exposure
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Develop a method for periodically conducting radiological assessments of public exposure:
- Ensure that the plan includes provisions for regular and ongoing monitoring of radiation levels in areas where the public may be...
Procedure:

1. Develop a method for periodically conducting radiological assessments of public exposure:
- Ensure that the plan includes provisions for regular and ongoing monitoring of radiation levels in areas where the public may be exposed.
- Use established guidelines such as those provided by the EPA-400/R-17/001 _PAG Manual: Protective Action Guides and Planning Guidance for Radiological Incidents_ to determine appropriate assessment intervals and methodologies.

2. Implement a systematic approach:
- Conduct assessments at regular intervals, especially in areas where contamination is suspected or confirmed.
- Use standardized tools and procedures for collecting and analyzing data on public exposure levels.

3. Document and report findings:
- Maintain detailed records of all radiological assessments conducted.
- Report findings to relevant authorities and stakeholders as needed, ensuring transparency and timely communication.

4. Adjust based on assessment results:
- Use the data collected from periodic assessments to inform decisions related to protective actions, evacuation orders, or reentry criteria.
- Update plans and procedures accordingly to ensure they remain effective and responsive to changing conditions.
radiological assessment, public exposure, monitoring, radiation levels, protective actions
Plan for Airborne Radioactive Plume Tracking and Characterization
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Develop procedures to locate, track, and characterize airborne radioactive plumes as required by Evaluation Criterion I.9.

Steps:
1. Identify resources available for plume tracking, including:
- Federal agencies (e.g.,...
Objective: Develop procedures to locate, track, and characterize airborne radioactive plumes as required by Evaluation Criterion I.9.

Steps:
1. Identify resources available for plume tracking, including:
- Federal agencies (e.g., FEMA, EPA)
- State and local governments
- Tribal governments
- Licensee resources (e.g., radiation monitoring equipment from the facility involved in the incident)
2. Plan to locate and track the airborne radioactive plume using available resources, including:
- Deployment of mobile and fixed radiation monitoring systems
- Use of air sampling devices near or within the plume’s peak concentration area
3. Characterize the plume by taking peak plume measurements, ensuring that:
- Measurements are sufficiently above background radiation levels to indicate submersion within the plume
- Data collected is accurate and representative of the plume's intensity and spread
4. Ensure procedures include identification methods for determining when a measurement is high enough to be reasonably above background readings, such as using statistical thresholds or comparison with historical baseline data.

Notes:
• These plans must be integrated into broader radiological emergency response strategies.
• Ensure that all personnel involved in plume tracking are trained on the use of equipment and interpretation of results.
airborne plume tracking, radiological incident, peak plume measurement, background radiation comparison

Maritime Pollution (4) -

Manage a Major Oil Spill Scenario
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure for Managing a Major Oil Spill Scenario:

1. Assume the scenario involves the spillage of 100,000 tonnes of crude oil into UK coastal waters due to causes such as vessel collision, fire, or grounding.
2. **Estimate the impact...
Procedure for Managing a Major Oil Spill Scenario:

1. Assume the scenario involves the spillage of 100,000 tonnes of crude oil into UK coastal waters due to causes such as vessel collision, fire, or grounding.
2. Estimate the impact area, which may result in contamination of up to 200km of UK coastline and associated environmental effects on land, water, animal welfare, agriculture, waste management, and air quality.
3. Implement extensive shoreline cleanup operations as required to address the scale of contamination.
4. Enforce long-term restrictions on local fishing in affected areas until the environment is deemed safe for marine life and human consumption.
5. Ensure that the vessel does not sink or prevent access to liquified natural gas terminals or port infrastructure, as per key assumptions for this scenario.
oil spill scenario,shoreline cleanup,fishing restrictions,vessel integrity,port access
Respond to Major Maritime Pollution Incident
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure for Responding to a Major Maritime Pollution Incident:

1. Assess the risk of major maritime pollution in UK waters, which may result from accidental spillage of oil from tankers or leakage from pipelines.
2. **Implement existing...
Procedure for Responding to a Major Maritime Pollution Incident:

1. Assess the risk of major maritime pollution in UK waters, which may result from accidental spillage of oil from tankers or leakage from pipelines.
2. Implement existing plans and procedures to respond quickly to limit and reduce environmental impacts on marine habitats, coastal communities, and local ecosystems.
3. Coordinate with local authorities, resilience forums, and emergency services to communicate and plan for such eventualities.
4. Monitor for incidents, as they are infrequent but do occur, and ensure that response capabilities are ready at all times.
5. Record and report spills promptly, including the quantity of oil spilled (e.g., hundreds of barrels into the Irish Sea in 2022 following a pipeline leak off the coast of Wales).
oil spill,pipeline leakage,response planning,environmental impact,monitoring
Coordinate Local Level Response to Casualty Landing
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure for Coordinating Local Level Response to Casualty Landing:

1. Activate local level plans to coordinate the response to provide medical assistance, decontamination, accommodation, and repatriation to people landed.
2. **Ensure...
Procedure for Coordinating Local Level Response to Casualty Landing:

1. Activate local level plans to coordinate the response to provide medical assistance, decontamination, accommodation, and repatriation to people landed.
2. Ensure casualty triage is conducted promptly to prioritize treatment based on severity of injuries or conditions.
3. Implement decontamination procedures for individuals exposed to hazardous materials or contaminants.
4. Establish family reunification protocols to facilitate the reuniting of families affected by the incident.
5. Provide language interpretation services for foreign nationals who may require assistance in understanding medical, legal, or administrative processes.
6. Coordinate with border force and Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) to assist persons without documentation, medication, or accommodation.
7. Ensure port security measures are in place, especially at the landing point, to prevent unauthorized access and maintain order.
8. Maintain strong communications capabilities between agencies landside and maritime at the landing point to ensure seamless coordination of response efforts.
9. Implement a robust capability to count and track casualties and survivors throughout the incident to manage resources effectively.
casualty triage,decontamination,family reunification,port security,language interpretation,FCDO,border force,communications capability,casualty tracking
Manage Recovery from Shoreside Impacts
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure for Managing Recovery from Shoreside Impacts:

1. Initiate recovery operations in the order of days and weeks following the incident, managed through existing local level plans and recovery arrangements.
2. **Provide support to...
Procedure for Managing Recovery from Shoreside Impacts:

1. Initiate recovery operations in the order of days and weeks following the incident, managed through existing local level plans and recovery arrangements.
2. Provide support to remote communities, such as the Western Isles, where the incident may have a lasting impact on the community, including mental health support and long-term assistance.
3. Coordinate vessel recovery through the National Contingency Plan and commercial salvage routes to ensure safe and efficient removal of damaged or contaminated vessels.
4. Implement pollution management procedures in accordance with the National Contingency Plan to address environmental impacts from oil spills or other contaminants.
5. Monitor access to ports, as incidents may impact port operations, potentially extending recovery timelines to weeks or months if necessary.
recovery operations,port access,vessel recovery,pollution management,community support

Communication Failure (4) -

Prepare for Simultaneous Loss of All Fixed and Mobile Communication
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Assess the risk of severe weather events (e.g., storms, flooding) that could cause a simultaneous loss of all fixed and mobile communication services in your area.
2. Ensure emergency communication systems are not reliant...
Procedure:

1. Assess the risk of severe weather events (e.g., storms, flooding) that could cause a simultaneous loss of all fixed and mobile communication services in your area.
2. Ensure emergency communication systems are not reliant on the same infrastructure as public networks. These should be powered by backup generators or alternative energy sources.
3. Install backup power supplies at critical domestic premises, such as uninterruptible power supply (UPS) units or battery backups, to maintain essential communication during outages.
4. Maintain physical infrastructure resilience, including overhead cables and mobile cell sites, through regular maintenance and reinforcement against weather damage.
5. Develop contingency plans for emergency services to communicate with the public using alternative methods such as radio broadcasts, SMS alerts, or community-based notification systems.
6. Train personnel in emergency communication protocols and ensure they have access to backup equipment during outages.

Warnings:
• A complete loss of communication can delay emergency response times significantly.
• Most domestic premises may lack backup power supplies, increasing the risk of prolonged outages.
fixed and mobile communication loss, severe weather, emergency response, backup power
Respond to Simultaneous Communication Outage Caused by Severe Weather
Critical Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Activate emergency communication protocols immediately upon detecting a loss of all fixed and mobile communication services in the affected region.
2. Deploy backup generators at mobile cell sites to restore temporary...
Procedure:

1. Activate emergency communication protocols immediately upon detecting a loss of all fixed and mobile communication services in the affected region.
2. Deploy backup generators at mobile cell sites to restore temporary mobile network functionality as quickly as possible.
3. Coordinate with Ofcom and other regulatory bodies to ensure compliance with obligations for uninterrupted access to emergency organizations.
4. Utilize alternative communication methods, such as radio broadcasts, SMS alerts, or community-based notification systems, to disseminate critical information to the public.
5. Monitor power supply at customer premises and prioritize restoration of electricity to homes and businesses that rely on fixed-line connections for essential services.
6. Provide guidance to affected customers on how to access emergency services without mobile or internet connectivity, such as using landline phones (if available) or visiting local emergency centers.

Warnings:
• Prolonged outages may lead to a breakdown in public safety and coordination efforts.
• Without backup power at customer premises, recovery times for fixed-line connections can be significantly extended.
severe weather, communication outage, emergency protocols, Ofcom guidelines
Implement Resilient Backup Systems for PNT Services
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify critical infrastructure that relies on Positioning, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) services, such as transportation systems, power grids, and financial institutions.
2. Deploy resilient backup systems, including...
Procedure:

1. Identify critical infrastructure that relies on Positioning, Navigation, and Timing (PNT) services, such as transportation systems, power grids, and financial institutions.
2. Deploy resilient backup systems, including ground-based alternatives like GPS augmentation systems or terrestrial radio navigation aids.
3. Enhance space situational awareness by investing in monitoring capabilities to detect threats such as jamming, spoofing, or severe space weather events that could disrupt satellite PNT services.
4. Conduct regular testing and maintenance of backup systems to ensure they are operational during an outage.
5. Coordinate with national and international partners to share data on potential threats to PNT services and develop joint response strategies.

Warnings:
• Failure to implement backup systems may result in prolonged outages affecting critical infrastructure.
• Space weather events can be unpredictable, so continuous monitoring is essential.
PNT services, backup systems, space situational awareness, communication failure
Mitigate PNT Service Disruption from Jamming or Spoofing Activities
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Procedure:

1. Monitor for signs of jamming or spoofing activities, such as unexpected signal interference or GPS anomalies, using space situational awareness tools.
2. Implement anti-jamming and anti-spoofing technologies, including...
Procedure:

1. Monitor for signs of jamming or spoofing activities, such as unexpected signal interference or GPS anomalies, using space situational awareness tools.
2. Implement anti-jamming and anti-spoofing technologies, including frequency hopping systems or cryptographic authentication methods to protect PNT signals.
3. Diversify PNT sources by integrating multiple satellite constellations (e.g., GPS, Galileo, GLONASS) and ground-based augmentation systems into critical infrastructure.
4. Conduct regular drills and simulations to test the resilience of backup systems in response to jamming or spoofing events.
5. Collaborate with international organizations to share threat intelligence and coordinate responses to cross-border PNT disruptions.

Warnings:
• Jamming or spoofing can be difficult to detect without advanced monitoring tools.
• A lack of diversified PNT sources may leave systems vulnerable to targeted attacks.
jamming, spoofing, PNT disruption, anti-jamming technology

Drought Management (4) -

Prepare for and Respond to Severe Drought Conditions in South and East England
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Objective: Mitigate the impacts of severe droughts on water supply, economy, and environment.

Procedure:
1. Monitor weather patterns and water levels closely using systems managed by the Environment Agency (EA) and water companies.
2. In...
Objective: Mitigate the impacts of severe droughts on water supply, economy, and environment.

Procedure:
1. Monitor weather patterns and water levels closely using systems managed by the Environment Agency (EA) and water companies.
2. In the event of three consecutive dry winters, initiate early preparation measures such as public awareness campaigns and regulatory restrictions.
3. Implement water supply restrictions in South and East England to ensure equitable distribution of limited resources.
4. Coordinate with neighboring regions like the Midlands and South West to manage cross-border impacts and share resources where possible.
5. Prepare for economic disruptions by working with industry stakeholders to develop contingency plans.
6. Address environmental damage through reforestation, soil conservation, and water management initiatives post-drought.

Details: This scenario assumes a severe drought following three consecutive dry years, requiring national coordination via the National Drought Group.
severe drought,response planning,national coordination
Enforce Temporary Water Usage Bans During Severe Drought Conditions
High Priority Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Objective: Limit water abstraction to ensure sustainable use during severe droughts.

Procedure:
1. Monitor water levels and usage patterns through the Environment Agency (EA) and water companies.
2. In the event of a severe or emergency...
Objective: Limit water abstraction to ensure sustainable use during severe droughts.

Procedure:
1. Monitor water levels and usage patterns through the Environment Agency (EA) and water companies.
2. In the event of a severe or emergency drought, implement Temporary Usage Bans on non-essential water use by businesses and farmers.
3. Ensure that essential services such as healthcare, food production, and fire-fighting are exempt from these bans.
4. Communicate the restrictions clearly to the public through official channels, including government websites, social media, and local news outlets.
5. Enforce compliance with the bans using regulatory mechanisms and penalties for non-compliance where necessary.

Details: These measures should be coordinated by the National Drought Group and DEFRA to ensure consistency across regions.
water bans,drought response,regulatory measures
Implement Water Efficiency Campaigns During Drought Conditions
Standard Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Objective: Reduce water demand during drought periods.

Procedure:
1. Launch public awareness campaigns through media, social platforms, and community outreach to educate citizens on reducing water usage.
2. Encourage the use of water-saving...
Objective: Reduce water demand during drought periods.

Procedure:
1. Launch public awareness campaigns through media, social platforms, and community outreach to educate citizens on reducing water usage.
2. Encourage the use of water-saving devices such as low-flow showerheads, dual-flush toilets, and water-efficient appliances.
3. Promote behavioral changes, including shorter showers, fixing leaks promptly, and using water only when necessary (e.g., turning off taps while brushing teeth).
4. Coordinate with local governments to enforce temporary water usage restrictions where necessary.
5. Provide incentives for households and businesses that adopt water-saving practices.

Details: Campaigns should be supported by clear messaging from national authorities, including the Environment Agency and Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA).
water conservation,drought management,campaigns,public awareness
Support Farmers During Drought Conditions
Standard Source: UK National Risk Register 2025
Objective: Mitigate the economic and environmental impacts of drought on agriculture.

Procedure:
1. Provide financial assistance to farmers affected by drought through government grants or subsidies.
2. Offer research funding for water...
Objective: Mitigate the economic and environmental impacts of drought on agriculture.

Procedure:
1. Provide financial assistance to farmers affected by drought through government grants or subsidies.
2. Offer research funding for water management innovations, such as improved irrigation techniques and drought-resistant crops.
3. Protect water rights for agricultural use where possible during droughts, ensuring that farming communities are not disproportionately impacted.
4. Collaborate with the Environment Agency to monitor and manage water abstraction from rivers and groundwater sources.
5. Encourage farmers to adopt sustainable practices such as crop rotation, soil conservation, and efficient irrigation systems.

Details: These interventions should be part of a broader strategy managed by DEFRA and the National Drought Group.
farmer support,drought relief,agricultural water use

Data Analysis (4) -

Calculate Historical Occurrence Count of Strong Wind Events
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Create fishnet grid: Use a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid to spatially analyze Strong Wind event data.
2. Count intersections: Calculate the number of distinct Strong Wind event polygons that intersect each grid cell.
3....
Procedure:

1. Create fishnet grid: Use a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid to spatially analyze Strong Wind event data.
2. Count intersections: Calculate the number of distinct Strong Wind event polygons that intersect each grid cell.
3. Buffer wind points: Buffer the original Strong Wind point data to spread influence to nearby areas that may be susceptible but have not experienced frequent events.
4. Adjust for overestimation: Apply a national scaling factor (see Equation 108) to correct for potential overestimation of frequency:
- Scaling Factor = (Number of Strong Wind Events in the U.S.) / (Total Number of Grid Cell Intersections)
historical occurrence, strong wind events, fishnet grid, scaling factor
Access and Utilize Storm Events Database for Historical Loss Data
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Locate the National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) Storm Events Database:
- Visit the official NCEI website to access the database.
2. Understand the Data Scope:
- The database includes historical loss...
Procedure:

1. Locate the National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI) Storm Events Database:
- Visit the official NCEI website to access the database.
2. Understand the Data Scope:
- The database includes historical loss data such as building damage, agricultural damage, and population death/injuries due to hazard and peril occurrences.
3. Differentiate from SHELDUS:
- Unlike SHELDUS, the Storm Events Database includes records of peril occurrences even if there were no reported losses.
4. Handle Forecast Zone Data:
- Some records are designated with a forecast zone rather than a county.
- Join these records to a county using a time-dependent county-forecast zone correlation table for accurate analysis.
5. Use the Data for Risk Assessment and Planning:
- Use the historical data to assess risks, prepare for future events, and improve emergency response strategies.
storm events database, historical loss data, NCEI, risk assessment, natural disaster analysis
Direct Calculation of Annualized Frequency at the County Level for Specific Hazard Types
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
For a few hazard types, annualized frequency is calculated directly at the Census tract level, after which the Census block simply inherits the value from its parent Census tract (see Table 5).

• Avalanche and Inland Flooding are...
For a few hazard types, annualized frequency is calculated directly at the Census tract level, after which the Census block simply inherits the value from its parent Census tract (see Table 5).

• Avalanche and Inland Flooding are the only hazard types for which annualized frequency is calculated at the county level directly, and the Census tracts and Census blocks then inherit the value of their parent county.
avalanche,inland flooding,county level,data analysis
Calculate Historical Drought Occurrence Count
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Compute the historical occurrence count of drought in event-days at the Census tract level.
2. Use the number of distinct drought event-week polygons that intersect a Census tract, multiplied by seven (to...
Procedure:

1. Compute the historical occurrence count of drought in event-days at the Census tract level.
2. Use the number of distinct drought event-week polygons that intersect a Census tract, multiplied by seven (to convert weeks to days).
3. This data is used to compute annualized frequency at the Census tract level.
4. At the county level, calculate the historical occurrence count as the number of distinct drought event-week polygons that intersect the county, multiplied by seven.
historical occurrence count, event-day calculation, census tract analysis, county-level data

Cold Wave Preparedness (4) -

Determine Possibility of Cold Wave Occurrence
High Priority Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Understand Local Definitions: Recognize that a Cold Wave is locally defined by each area’s Weather Forecast Office. For example, definitions may vary between Texas and New York.
2. Assess All Counties: Since Cold Waves can...
Procedure:

1. Understand Local Definitions: Recognize that a Cold Wave is locally defined by each area’s Weather Forecast Office. For example, definitions may vary between Texas and New York.
2. Assess All Counties: Since Cold Waves can occur almost anywhere in the United States, all counties are considered possible for Cold Wave occurrence.
Cold Wave, local definition, Weather Forecast Office, preparedness
Understand Cold Wave Definitions and Local Criteria
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
A Cold Wave is defined as a rapid fall in temperature within 24 hours, followed by extreme low temperatures for an extended period. The specific temperature thresholds that classify an event as a Cold Wave are location-dependent and...
A Cold Wave is defined as a rapid fall in temperature within 24 hours, followed by extreme low temperatures for an extended period. The specific temperature thresholds that classify an event as a Cold Wave are location-dependent and determined by the local National Weather Service (NWS) weather forecast office.

This definition is based on hazard modeling data from the NWS, which uses Mesonet data to identify impact areas for cold wave events. These models rely on historical data and warnings/advisories issued by the NWS.
cold wave, temperature thresholds, national weather service, hazard modeling, mesonet data
Review Cold Wave Hazard Modeling Data Sources
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
The Cold Wave hazard model uses the following key data sources:

1. Mesonet data: Used to identify impact areas based on NWS warnings and advisories.
2. SHELDUS data: Historical loss data for cold wave events, used to calculate...
The Cold Wave hazard model uses the following key data sources:

1. Mesonet data: Used to identify impact areas based on NWS warnings and advisories.
2. SHELDUS data: Historical loss data for cold wave events, used to calculate Historic Loss Ratios.
3. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) Compressed Mortality Database: Provides mortality data that supplements SHELDUS data.
4. Hazus 6.0 exposure values: Used for calculating the Expected Annual Loss in terms of Building, Population, and Agriculture consequences.

These datasets are combined to assess risk across three consequence types: Building, Population, and Agriculture.
hazard modeling, sheldus data, hazus 6.0, cold wave risk assessment, mortality data
Access Hazard Data Characteristics for Cold Wave Analysis
Reference Source: US National Risk Index 2025
For a detailed understanding of the data characteristics used in Cold Wave hazard modeling, refer to Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison in the US National Risk Index 2025. This section provides a comprehensive overview...
For a detailed understanding of the data characteristics used in Cold Wave hazard modeling, refer to Appendix B – Hazard Data Characteristics Comparison in the US National Risk Index 2025. This section provides a comprehensive overview of:

• The types and sources of data used for modeling.
• How historical loss data is integrated with exposure values.
• The methodology behind calculating Expected Annual Loss for different consequence types (Building, Population, Agriculture).

This information is critical for risk assessment, planning, and resource allocation in areas prone to cold wave events.
hazard data characteristics, risk assessment, expected annual loss, cold wave modeling

Cold Wave Processing (4) -

Convert Cold Wave Event Duration to Event-Days
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. For each Cold Wave event record with a duration greater than one day:
- Split the original alert into multiple records, one for each day of the event’s duration.
2. If an event lasts less than 6 hours on any given day:
-...
Procedure:

1. For each Cold Wave event record with a duration greater than one day:
- Split the original alert into multiple records, one for each day of the event’s duration.
2. If an event lasts less than 6 hours on any given day:
- Assign the event to the day with the greatest duration within the event period.
3. For events lasting more than 6 hours on a given day:
- Assign the event to all days where 6 or more hours of the event occur.

4. Example:
- If an alert is issued from 2 AM to 6 PM on January 1 (14-hour duration), assign it to January 1 only.
- If an alert spans from 11 PM on January 1 to 1 PM on January 2, assign the event to January 2 only.
- If an alert spans from 6 PM on January 1 to 9 AM on January 2, assign it to both January 1 and January 2.
event-day conversion, duration processing, cold wave alerts
Extract Cold Wave Weather Events from Dataset
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Filter dataset records based on the following criteria:
- Valid Time Extent Code (SIG field): Only include records with SIG = ‘W’ (Warning) or SIG = ‘Y’ (Advisory).
- Phenomena type (PHENOM or TYPE field): Include...
Procedure:

1. Filter dataset records based on the following criteria:
- Valid Time Extent Code (SIG field): Only include records with SIG = ‘W’ (Warning) or SIG = ‘Y’ (Advisory).
- Phenomena type (PHENOM or TYPE field): Include only records with PHENOM = “Extreme Cold” (EC) or PHENOM = “Wind Chill” (WC).

2. Exclude any other types of alerts or warnings that do not match the above criteria.

3. Use this filtered dataset for further processing and analysis related to Cold Wave events.
Cold Wave, data filtering, SIG field, PHENOM field, weather alerts
Handle Data Quality Issues in Cold Wave Records
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify records where the expiration date is earlier than the issue date.
2. For these invalid records:
- Use a single record and assign it to the issue date instead of the expiration date.
3. Ensure that all Cold Wave...
Procedure:

1. Identify records where the expiration date is earlier than the issue date.
2. For these invalid records:
- Use a single record and assign it to the issue date instead of the expiration date.
3. Ensure that all Cold Wave event records have valid, non-contradictory time ranges for accurate analysis.
data quality, invalid dates, cold wave records
Replace Low-Resolution Event Areas with Higher Resolution Shapes
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify event areas in the dataset that use simplified shapes from Mesonet data.
2. Replace these low-resolution shapes with higher resolution versions:
- Use National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shape associated...
Procedure:

1. Identify event areas in the dataset that use simplified shapes from Mesonet data.
2. Replace these low-resolution shapes with higher resolution versions:
- Use National Weather Service Public Forecast Zone shape associated with the alert record.
- If the forecast zone is for a single county, substitute it with a better resolution version of the county boundary area.

3. Ensure that all spatial data used in subsequent analysis reflects this higher-resolution replacement to improve accuracy.
event areas, shape substitution, forecast zone, county boundary

Tornado Data Processing (4) -

Calculate Tornado Buffer Radii for Spatial Analysis
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Determine buffer radius options: Use one of the following methods to calculate a buffer radius for tornado event path polygons:
- Option 1: Half of the tornado width as specified in Table 70 (converted from yards to...
Procedure:

1. Determine buffer radius options: Use one of the following methods to calculate a buffer radius for tornado event path polygons:
- Option 1: Half of the tornado width as specified in Table 70 (converted from yards to meters).
- Option 2: The calculated radius extrapolated from the tornado’s length and width (converted to meters) as provided in the source data.
- Option 3: The average impact radius for a storm of that magnitude based on F- or EF-scale category according to Table 71.

2. Select the largest buffer radius: Use whichever method yields the largest radius for buffering the given line or point.

Details:
• This ensures a conservative estimate of the largest possible area affected by each tornado event.
• The selected buffer radius is used to create tornado event path polygons for spatial analysis.
buffer radii,tornado spatial processing,impact area estimation,f-scale categories
Create Additional Buffer for Annualized Frequency Estimation
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Apply 80-km buffer: Create an additional 80-km buffer around the category-buffered tornado event path polygons.
2. Intersect with fishnet grid: Intersect these buffered polygons with a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid to estimate...
Procedure:

1. Apply 80-km buffer: Create an additional 80-km buffer around the category-buffered tornado event path polygons.
2. Intersect with fishnet grid: Intersect these buffered polygons with a 49-by-49-km fishnet grid to estimate annualized frequency at the Census tract level.

Details:
• The 80-km buffer represents an area where the tornado event could have occurred, not necessarily its actual impact area.
• This helps spread the influence of historical events for more accurate annualized frequency estimation.
80-km buffer,frequency estimation,fishnet grid,census tract analysis
Create Tornado Event Path Polygons for Spatial Analysis
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Buffer tornado paths and touchdown points: Use the selected buffer radius (from previous step) to create tornado event path polygons from both path lines and touchdown points.
2. Intersect with Census blocks: Determine...
Procedure:

1. Buffer tornado paths and touchdown points: Use the selected buffer radius (from previous step) to create tornado event path polygons from both path lines and touchdown points.
2. Intersect with Census blocks: Determine which Census blocks and parent counties were traversed by each tornado event by intersecting the buffered tornado polygons with Census block data.
3. Use for loss ratio calculation: Use this spatial relationship in the Historic Loss Ratio calculation, as well as to determine historical tornado event counts at the Census tract and county level.

Details:
• This helps assess potential losses from each tornado event.
• The intersection with Census blocks allows for detailed geographic analysis of tornado impacts.
event path polygons,census block intersection,historic loss ratio,spatial analysis
Process Tornado Spatial Data to Create Complete Geometries
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify incomplete geometries: Check the path shapefile provided by the Storm Prediction Center for tornado records with empty geometries.
2. Replace missing geometries: Replace any path records with empty geometries...
Procedure:

1. Identify incomplete geometries: Check the path shapefile provided by the Storm Prediction Center for tornado records with empty geometries.
2. Replace missing geometries: Replace any path records with empty geometries using the record with the same unique ID from the point shapefile.
3. Filter out invalid data: Remove any tornadoes outside the period of record or those with an F- or EF-scale of -9 (indicating insufficient data).

Details:
• This ensures a complete set of geometries for all tornadoes within the period of record.
• The point shapefile contains accurate geometry data that can be used to replace missing path information.
spatial data processing,tornado geometries,storm prediction center,point shapefile,path shapefile

Winter Weather (4) -

Calculate Historical Occurrence Count for Winter Weather Events
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Use area-weighted sum of distinct Winter Weather event-day polygons that intersect a Census block to compute the historical occurrence count in event-days.
2. Ensure this method uses the same **Winter Weather...
Procedure:

1. Use area-weighted sum of distinct Winter Weather event-day polygons that intersect a Census block to compute the historical occurrence count in event-days.
2. Ensure this method uses the same Winter Weather expansion Census block intersection table used for exposure calculations at the Census block level.
3. Provide historical event-day counts also at the Census tract and county levels, as the number of distinct Winter Weather event-day polygons that intersect these areas.
historical occurrence count, winter weather events, census block, area-weighted sum
Determine Period of Record for Winter Weather Data
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Procedure:

1. Identify that the National Weather Service (NWS) has been recording Winter Weather Warnings (WWA) since the 1990s, but automated processing was difficult until 2005.
2. Note that in 2005, the **Valid Time Extent Code...
Procedure:

1. Identify that the National Weather Service (NWS) has been recording Winter Weather Warnings (WWA) since the 1990s, but automated processing was difficult until 2005.
2. Note that in 2005, the Valid Time Extent Code system was implemented to allow for easy automated parsing of alert data.
3. Confirm that the period of record for Winter Weather data is from Nov. 12, 2005, to Nov. 12, 2024, spanning a total of 19.0 years.
period of record, winter weather data, nws, valid time extent code
Recognizing Delineation of Winter Weather vs Other Cold-Based Hazards
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Important Consideration:

• The data development team made several decisions regarding the delineation between what is considered Winter Weather versus other cold-based hazards.

• These decisions have been outlined in this Technical...
Important Consideration:

• The data development team made several decisions regarding the delineation between what is considered Winter Weather versus other cold-based hazards.

• These decisions have been outlined in this Technical Document and are critical for understanding how different types of cold-related events are categorized within the National Risk Index.

Actionable Insight:

• Review the technical documentation to understand how winter weather is differentiated from other cold-based hazards (e.g., frost, freezing temperatures without precipitation).

• This distinction affects risk assessments and preparedness planning.
Winter Weather,Cold-Based Hazards,Delineation,Technical Documentation
Understanding Limitations of Winter Weather Data from NWS Warnings
Standard Source: US National Risk Index 2025
Important Consideration:

• National Weather Service (NWS) Warnings and Advisories are issued at the discretion of each individual National Weather Service Forecast Office, based on regionally determined criteria.

• This results in an...
Important Consideration:
