A neighbor suggests closing windows and doors on the storm side of the house and opening those on the windward side, while taping windows to prevent glass damage.

Is this a good idea?

• No. This is not a safe practice.
• Taping windows does not prevent them from breaking during a hurricane.
• It is better to use storm shutters or 5/8" marine plywood for protection.
• Closing windows on the windward side and opening those on the leeward side can increase pressure differences, potentially causing more damage.
window taping safety, hurricane knowledge check, windward vs leeward windows

Earthquake Preparation (1) -

Secure Heavy Objects and Appliances Before an Earthquake
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Before an earthquake, take the following steps to protect your home:

1. Repair defective electrical wiring, leaky gas lines, and inflexible utility connections. Always seek professional help for these tasks; do not attempt them yourself.
2....
Before an earthquake, take the following steps to protect your home:

1. Repair defective electrical wiring, leaky gas lines, and inflexible utility connections. Always seek professional help for these tasks; do not attempt them yourself.
2. Bolt down and secure heavy appliances such as water heaters, refrigerators, furnaces, and gas appliances to wall studs.
3. If recommended by your gas company, install an automatic gas shut-off valve that activates during strong vibrations.
4. Place large or heavy objects on lower shelves to prevent them from falling during shaking.
5. Fasten shelves, mirrors, and large picture frames to walls, and brace high or top-heavy objects for stability.
6. Store breakables such as bottled foods, glass, china, and other fragile items on low shelves or in cabinets with secure shutters.
7. Anchor overhead lighting fixtures to prevent them from falling during an earthquake.
8. Ensure your residence is firmly anchored to its foundation for added structural integrity.
9. Install flexible pipe fittings for gas and water lines, as they are more resistant to breakage than rigid ones.
earthquake preparation, securing furniture, gas lines, electrical wiring, flexible fittings

Volcanic Eruption Preparedness (1) -

Prepare Eye and Respiratory Protection for Volcanic Eruption
Standard Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: Add a pair of goggles and a disposable breathing mask for each family member to your disaster supplies kit.

Details:
• Ensure that all family members have access to protective eyewear and respiratory masks in case of volcanic ash...
Action: Add a pair of goggles and a disposable breathing mask for each family member to your disaster supplies kit.

Details:
• Ensure that all family members have access to protective eyewear and respiratory masks in case of volcanic ash exposure.
• Store these items in an easily accessible location within the emergency supply kit.
goggles, breathing mask, volcanic eruption, disaster kit, eye protection, respiratory protection

Landslide Recovery (1) -

Post-Landslide Actions and Safety Measures
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Post-Landslide Actions and Safety Measures:

• Stay away from the slide area, as there may be a danger of additional slides.
• Check for injured or trapped persons near the slide, but do not enter the direct slide area. Direct rescuers...
Post-Landslide Actions and Safety Measures:

• Stay away from the slide area, as there may be a danger of additional slides.
• Check for injured or trapped persons near the slide, but do not enter the direct slide area. Direct rescuers to their locations.
• Watch for associated dangers such as broken electrical, water, gas, and sewage lines, as well as damaged roadways and railways.
• Replant damaged ground as soon as possible to prevent erosion caused by loss of ground cover, which can lead to flash flooding and additional landslides in the near future.
• Seek advice from a geotechnical expert for evaluating landslide hazards or designing corrective techniques to reduce landslide risk.
• Follow the instructions for returning home provided in Part 5 of the guide.
post landslide,landslide recovery,safety after landslide,geotechnical expert

Tsunami Evacuation (1) -

Evacuation After a Tsunami
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Stay away from flooded and damaged areas until officials declare it safe to return.
2. Avoid debris in the water, as it can pose safety hazards to both people and boats.
3. Do not attempt to retrieve personal property...
Procedure:

1. Stay away from flooded and damaged areas until officials declare it safe to return.
2. Avoid debris in the water, as it can pose safety hazards to both people and boats.
3. Do not attempt to retrieve personal property during or immediately after a tsunami, as no possession is worth risking your life.
4. Seek higher ground away from the coast and remain there until authorities confirm it is safe to return.
tsunami, evacuation, safety, debris, flooded areas

Fire Recovery (1) -

Follow Instructions for Recovering from a Fire Disaster
Standard Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: After a fire:

• Follow the instructions provided in Part 5 of *Are You Ready?* for recovering from a disaster.
• This includes steps such as insurance considerations, property valuation, document replacement, and salvage hints.
disaster recovery after fire, FEMA resources, post-fire procedures

Wildfire Preparation (1) -

Prepare Your Property for Wildfires
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Objective: Reduce wildfire risk to your home and property.

Steps to Prepare Before a Wildfire:
1. Mark property entrance clearly: Install visible address signs at the entrance of your property so emergency responders can locate it...
Objective: Reduce wildfire risk to your home and property.

Steps to Prepare Before a Wildfire:
1. Mark property entrance clearly: Install visible address signs at the entrance of your property so emergency responders can locate it quickly.
2. Maintain yard and roof cleanliness: Keep lawns trimmed, leaves raked, and ensure that roofs and rain gutters are free from debris such as dead limbs and leaves.
3. Store firewood safely: Stack firewood at least 30 feet away from your residence to reduce the risk of ignition.
4. Secure flammable materials: Store flammable liquids, solvents, and other combustible items in metal containers outside your home, at least 30 feet away from structures and wooden fences.
5. Create defensible space:
- Thin trees and brush within the first 30 feet around your residence.
- Beyond 30 feet, remove dead wood, debris, and low-hanging tree branches.
6. Use fire-resistant landscaping: Plant fire-resistant vegetation such as hardwood trees instead of flammable species like pine, evergreen, eucalyptus, or fir.
7. Ensure water source accessibility: Make sure that hydrants, ponds, swimming pools, and wells are accessible to the fire department in case of an emergency.
8. Use fire-resistant building materials: Install protective roofing made from stone, brick, or metal instead of wood, which offers minimal fire protection.
9. Protect vents and openings: Cover all exterior vents, attics, and eaves with metal mesh screens no larger than 6 millimeters (1/4 inch) to prevent debris accumulation and keep sparks out.
10. Install protective windows: Use multi-pane windows, tempered safety glass, or fireproof shutters to protect large windows from radiant heat.
11. Use fire-resistant draperies: Install fire-resistant window coverings for additional protection.
12. Maintain heating systems: Have chimneys, wood stoves, and all home heating systems inspected and cleaned annually by a certified specialist.
13. Insulate chimneys: Ensure that chimneys are insulated and equipped with spark arresters. The chimney should be at least 3 feet above the roof.
14. Remove overhanging branches: Eliminate any branches hanging above or around the chimney to reduce fire risk.
wildfire preparation, fire-resistant materials, defensible space, chimney maintenance, property safety

Hazardous Materials Incidents (1) -

Take Protective Measures for Hazardous Materials Incidents
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Protective measures are essential to minimize the risks associated with hazardous materials incidents.

### Steps to Take:
1. Identify potential hazards in your home and community:
- Know where hazardous materials are stored or used, such...
Protective measures are essential to minimize the risks associated with hazardous materials incidents.

### Steps to Take:
1. Identify potential hazards in your home and community:
- Know where hazardous materials are stored or used, such as at service stations, hospitals, or waste sites.
- Be aware of any chemical storage areas near your residence or workplace.
2. Store household chemicals safely:
- Keep all chemicals in their original containers with labels intact.
- Store them out of reach of children and pets, preferably in a locked cabinet or separate room.
- Avoid mixing different chemicals unless you are certain they are compatible.
3. Dispose of hazardous materials properly:
- Do not pour chemicals down drains, onto the ground, or into waterways.
- Contact your local waste management authority for information on proper disposal methods.
4. Prepare an emergency plan:
- Include procedures for what to do in case of a chemical spill or release.
- Ensure all family members know how to evacuate quickly and safely if needed.
5. Stay informed about technological hazards:
- Learn about the risks associated with hazardous materials and nuclear power plant failures.
- Keep up-to-date on local emergency alerts and warnings related to chemical incidents.
6. Recognize important terms related to hazardous materials:
- Familiarize yourself with terms such as 'hazardous waste,' 'toxic exposure,' and 'chemical spill.'
7. Know what actions to take if an event occurs:
- Evacuate immediately if instructed by authorities.
- Avoid contact with spilled chemicals or contaminated areas.
- Seek medical attention if you suspect chemical exposure.
8. Identify resources for more information about technological hazards:
- Refer to FEMA publications such as *Wildfire: Are You Prepared?* (L-203) for additional guidance on preparedness and mitigation techniques.

### Additional Tips:
• Keep a list of emergency contacts, including local hazardous materials response teams and poison control centers.
• Maintain an emergency kit that includes items like gloves, masks, and absorbent materials for cleaning up spills.
• Stay informed about your community's disaster plans and participate in drills if possible.
hazardous materials,chemical safety,disposal,emergency plan,protective measures

Home Inspection After Disaster (1) -

Inspecting a Damaged Home Safely
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Use a battery-powered flashlight to inspect a damaged home.
- Important: Turn the flashlight on outside before entering the home. This prevents sparks from the battery from igniting leaking gas, if present.
2. **Watch...
Procedure:

1. Use a battery-powered flashlight to inspect a damaged home.
- Important: Turn the flashlight on outside before entering the home. This prevents sparks from the battery from igniting leaking gas, if present.
2. Watch for potential hazards such as:
- Loose power lines
- Gas leaks (smell gas or hear hissing/blowing sounds)
- Structural damage (cracks in roof, foundation, or chimney)
3. Avoid entering the home if any of the following are present:
- You smell gas
- Floodwaters remain around the building
- The home was damaged by fire and authorities have not declared it safe
4. If you have doubts about safety, have your residence inspected by a qualified building inspector or structural engineer before entering.
5. Walk carefully around the outside of the home and check for:
- Loose power lines
- Gas leaks
- Structural damage
inspect, damaged home, flashlight, gas leak, structural damage, safety

Flood Damage Recovery (1) -

Handling Flood Damage in the Home
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Do not enter a home if floodwaters remain around the building.
2. If your basement has flooded:
- Pump it out gradually (about one third of the water per day) to avoid damage.
- Avoid pumping out the basement while the...
Procedure:

1. Do not enter a home if floodwaters remain around the building.
2. If your basement has flooded:
- Pump it out gradually (about one third of the water per day) to avoid damage.
- Avoid pumping out the basement while the surrounding ground is still waterlogged, as this may cause walls to collapse or floors to buckle.
3. Throw out all food and other supplies that you suspect may have become contaminated or come into contact with floodwater.
4. If pipes are damaged:
- Turn off the main water valve.
- Check with local authorities before using any water; it could be contaminated.
- Pump out wells and have the water tested by authorities before drinking.
5. Do not flush toilets until you know that sewage lines are intact.
flood damage, basement flooding, contaminated water, sewage lines, water testing

Wildlife Safety After Disaster (1) -

Dealing with Wildlife After a Disaster
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Be aware of increased wildlife activity after disasters, as they may be displaced or more aggressive due to stress.
2. Learn how to identify and avoid dangerous animals (especially poisonous snakes).
3. Use a stick to poke...
Procedure:

1. Be aware of increased wildlife activity after disasters, as they may be displaced or more aggressive due to stress.
2. Learn how to identify and avoid dangerous animals (especially poisonous snakes).
3. Use a stick to poke through debris when inspecting damaged areas to avoid contact with hidden animals.
4. If you encounter wildlife:
- Do not approach them.
- Keep pets inside or secured.
- Contact local animal control if necessary.
wildlife, snakes, animal safety, disaster response, wildlife management

Wildlife Safety (1) -

How to Respond to Injured or Stranded Wildlife
Standard Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Do not approach or attempt to help an injured or stranded animal. Call your local emergency management office or health department for assistance.

Do not corner wild animals or try to rescue them. Wild animals will likely feel threatened...
Do not approach or attempt to help an injured or stranded animal. Call your local emergency management office or health department for assistance.

Do not corner wild animals or try to rescue them. Wild animals will likely feel threatened and may endanger themselves by dashing off into floodwaters, fire, and so forth.

Do not approach wild animals that have taken refuge in your home. Wild animals such as snakes, opossums, and raccoons often seek refuge from floodwaters on upper levels of homes and have been known to remain after water recedes. If you encounter animals in this situation:
• Open a window or provide another escape route.
• The animal will likely leave on its own.
• Do not attempt to capture or handle the animal.
• Should the animal stay, call your local emergency management office or health department for help and instructions.

Do not attempt to move a dead animal. Animal carcasses can present serious health risks. Contact your local emergency management office or health department for help and instructions.

If bitten by an animal, seek immediate medical attention.
animal safety,wildlife,stranded animals,dead animal,bite response

Levee Emergency Response (1) -

Establish Planning Section for Levee Emergency Response
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure:

1. Designate a Leader: Assign the person responsible for the Planning section based on the size and complexity of the levee system. This could be the individual in charge of engineering support or another qualified personnel.
2....
Procedure:

1. Designate a Leader: Assign the person responsible for the Planning section based on the size and complexity of the levee system. This could be the individual in charge of engineering support or another qualified personnel.
2. Collect Information: Gather data regarding the nature of the emergency, including:
- Manpower requirements.
- Facilities impacted.
- Materials requirements.
3. Disseminate Information: Share collected information with all members of the emergency management organization to ensure everyone is informed about the emergency and progress being made.
4. Develop Strategy: In coordination with other parts of the management team, develop an overall strategy for responding to the emergency. This responsibility may be assigned elsewhere (e.g., Incident Commander or Operations section) depending on system size and complexity.
5. Maintain Documentation: Keep detailed records of actions taken during the emergency through status reports and documentation.
planning section,strategy development,emergency management,status reports,information collection

Levee Safety (1) -

Conduct Levee Patrol with Safety Measures
High Priority Source: fema emergency preparedness guidelines for levees 2018
Procedure for Conducting a Safe Levee Patrol:

1. Form the patrol team correctly:
- Members should walk side-by-side.
- One person should be on the waterside of the levee near the surface.
- Another should be at the **top of the...
Procedure for Conducting a Safe Levee Patrol:

1. Form the patrol team correctly:
- Members should walk side-by-side.
- One person should be on the waterside of the levee near the surface.
- Another should be at the top of the levee.
- The third should be on the landside toe of the levee.

2. Move slowly:
- Ensure that the member closest to the water can probe below the surface with a rod to detect erosion.

3. Safety line requirement:
- The person walking closest to the water should be wearing a safety line for protection.

4. Observe floating objects:
- Be especially observant of floating objects, such as uprooted trees with limbs and roots extending above the surface, which can strike individuals near the edge of the water.

5. Walk upstream when patrolling waterside:
- This increases the chance of identifying floating objects.

6. Patrol floodwalls safely:
- Do not walk along the top of the wall.
- Focus on potential problem areas on the landside (inside) of the wall.

7. Use ladders for observation when needed:
- If the wall is more than five feet above the landside ground level, select observation points every 100 yards and use ladders to observe the waterside of the floodwall.

8. Familiarize with evacuation plan:
- Each patrol member should be thoroughly familiar with the community evacuation plan and signals.

9. Evacuation protocol:
- If evacuation is necessary, move to a predetermined location and keep the team intact.
- When returning after an evacuation, be aware that physical conditions may have changed significantly, especially if the levee was overtopped.

10. Resume patrols only when safe:
- If overtopping occurs during nightfall, it is recommended to not resume patrols until daylight unless circumstances require otherwise.

11. Watch for suspicious activity:
- Be alert for anyone or any activity that seems out of place, such as individuals who may be terrorists or otherwise trying to exploit dangerous situations on levees or floodwalls.

12. Report suspicious activities immediately:
- Contact local law enforcement, State and local fusion centers, or the FBI if any suspicious activity is observed.
levee patrol, safety line, floating objects, floodwall inspection, evacuation plan, suspicious activity

Emergency Classification (1) -

Establish Initial Emergency Classification and Action Level (ECL) Scheme
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with the licensee to create the initial emergency classification and action level (ECL) scheme.
2. Ensure that the ECL system forms the basis for determining the level of response to an incident, which will be...
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with the licensee to create the initial emergency classification and action level (ECL) scheme.
2. Ensure that the ECL system forms the basis for determining the level of response to an incident, which will be coordinated with the licensee.
3. Agree on the initial ECL scheme with the licensee.
4. Incorporate the agreed-upon ECL portion into plans/procedures after it is approved by the NRC.
5. Review and revise the ECL scheme annually to ensure appropriate coordination and alignment of expected actions.

Notes:
• The ECL system classifies incidents based on severity, allowing for greater levels of response as the significance of the incident increases.
• The four standard ECLs are:
- Notification of Unusual Event (NOUE): Indicates potential degradation of plant safety or a security threat. No releases of radioactive material requiring offsite response are expected unless further degradation occurs.
- Alert: Indicates actual or potential substantial degradation of plant safety or life-threatening risk to site personnel due to hostile action. Limited releases may occur, but they are expected to be below EPA PAG exposure levels.
- Site Area Emergency (SAE): Indicates major failure of plant functions needed for public protection or intentional damage to the facility. Releases are not expected to exceed EPA PAG exposure levels beyond the site boundary.
- General Emergency (GE): Indicates actual or imminent substantial core degradation, potential loss of containment integrity, or loss of physical control of the facility. Releases can reasonably be expected to exceed EPA PAG exposure levels offsite for more than the immediate site area.
ECL, emergency classification, action level, NOUE, Alert, SAE, GE

Radiological Incident Response (1) -

Integrate Radiological Monitoring Resources During a Nuclear/Radiological Incident
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with Federal, State, Tribal, and Local Resources:
- Establish agreements (e.g., Letters of Agreement [LOAs]) with federal agencies, licensees, universities, contractors, mutual-aid organizations, or other...
Procedure:

1. Coordinate with Federal, State, Tribal, and Local Resources:
- Establish agreements (e.g., Letters of Agreement [LOAs]) with federal agencies, licensees, universities, contractors, mutual-aid organizations, or other entities to provide radiological monitoring data.
- Ensure that these arrangements are referenced in organizational plans/procedures as described in evaluation criterion A.4.

2. Utilize External Monitoring Data:
- If the organization is only tracking the outer edges of a plume, rely on external resources (e.g., Federal, licensee, or university teams) to collect air samples and conduct centerline measurements near peak exposure areas.
- Ensure that these data are integrated into the organization’s monitoring efforts.

3. Share Data and Analytical Results:
- Coordinate with other organizations performing radiological assessment functions (e.g., NNSA RAP Teams, FRMAC, A-Team) to share monitoring data, analytical results, and trending information.
- Include procedures for integrating Federal agency support in monitoring, analysis, and data management as described in evaluation criterion I.10.

4. Update Emergency Tracking Estimates (ETEs):
- Licensees must update ETEs periodically to ensure they reflect current conditions and capabilities.
- Ensure that protective actions for both plume exposure pathway and ingestion exposure pathway EPZ are consistent with Federal guidance.
radiological monitoring, integration of resources, plume tracking, data sharing, potassium iodide, ETEs, protective actions

Emergency Response Planning (1) -

Develop Site-Specific Protective Action Strategy and Decision-Making Process
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Establish a site-specific protective action strategy and decision-making process for the licensee and ORO.

Steps:
1. Coordinate between the licensee and ORO to develop the strategy/decision-making process.
2. Consider current...
Objective: Establish a site-specific protective action strategy and decision-making process for the licensee and ORO.

Steps:
1. Coordinate between the licensee and ORO to develop the strategy/decision-making process.
2. Consider current site-specific ETE (Emergency Trajectory Evaluation) studies when developing strategies/decision-making processes.
3. Take into account factors such as:
- Population densities
- Fluctuation related to transient populations
- Road capacity
- Access and functional needs facilities/populations in each subarea of the plume exposure pathway EPZ (Exclusion Zone)
4. Incorporate current Federal guidance and methodologies.
5. Reference the strategy/decision-making process appropriately in plans/procedures.
6. Plan for various factors that may impact implementation of a protective action, such as:
- Evacuation
- Sheltering
- Combination of both evacuation and sheltering
protective action strategy,decision-making process,evacuation,sheltering,Federal guidance,site-specific planning

Evacuation Plans (1) -

Establish Evacuation Plans/Procedures for Plume Exposure Pathway EPZ
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Objective: Develop evacuation plans/procedures to ensure the safe and efficient evacuation of individuals within the plume exposure pathway (EPZ) in case of an emergency.

Steps:
1. Identify all individuals within the EPZ, including those...
Objective: Develop evacuation plans/procedures to ensure the safe and efficient evacuation of individuals within the plume exposure pathway (EPZ) in case of an emergency.

Steps:
1. Identify all individuals within the EPZ, including those who are capable of using public transportation.
2. Address the evacuation needs of individuals with access and functional needs or those confined in institutions as outlined in evaluation criterion J.11.a.
3. Consider results from the Evacuation Time Estimate (ETE) study when selecting evacuation as a protective action.
4. Identify the version of the ETE study used to base the evacuation plan on.
5. Determine how far in advance an evacuation may need to occur, based on population densities and time estimates for individual evacuation areas.
6. Evaluate traffic capacities on selected evacuation routes and consider alternative routes when applicable.
7. Include measures to promote a smooth flow of evacuation traffic and assist persons who have no means of transportation.
8. Identify traffic control points (TCPs) along evacuation routes and establish procedures for converting two-way roads to one-way to increase traffic capacity.
9. Specify the personnel and equipment necessary to control traffic flow during an evacuation.
10. Include a means for identifying alternative routes and the resources needed to redirect traffic, if necessary (e.g., during a HAB incident or other emergencies).

Notes:
• Ensure that all steps are clearly documented in the plans/procedures.
• Coordinate with local authorities and transportation agencies to ensure accurate route planning.
evacuation plan,epz,plume exposure pathway,traffic control,alternative routes,public transportation

Food Safety (1) -

Implement Sampling and Analysis Protocols for Contaminants in Food and Agricultural Products
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Develop a plan that outlines the rationale for selecting sampling areas based on risk assessment.
2. Identify resources required to collect human food and agricultural product samples from areas of concern.
3. Use chain-of-custody...
Procedure:

1. Develop a plan that outlines the rationale for selecting sampling areas based on risk assessment.
2. Identify resources required to collect human food and agricultural product samples from areas of concern.
3. Use chain-of-custody documentation to ensure proper tracking of all collected samples.
4. Ensure that laboratories have the capability to analyze various types of samples, including radionuclides.
5. Establish detection limits (DILs) for radionuclides in food and agricultural products based on public action guides (PAGs).
6. Use sampling protocols capable of detecting contaminants at levels equal to or lower than the DILs.
7. Ensure that all laboratory analysis methods are validated and accurate.

Notes:
• This process is essential for determining contamination levels in food and agricultural products.
• Proper documentation and analysis ensure reliable results for decision-making.
sampling, analysis, contaminants, food safety, radionuclides

Mapping (1) -

Maintain and Use Maps for Recording Data on Contaminated Areas and Ingestion Doses
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Maintain up-to-date maps that can be used to record field survey readings, projected ingestion doses, and land use information.
2. Use electronic systems such as geographic information systems (GIS) to capture and map survey data...
Procedure:

1. Maintain up-to-date maps that can be used to record field survey readings, projected ingestion doses, and land use information.
2. Use electronic systems such as geographic information systems (GIS) to capture and map survey data and dose estimates.
3. Record the location of agribusiness activities on maps, including dairies, food processing plants, surface water supplies, and water supply intakes.
4. Identify processing plants located within the ingestion exposure pathway exclusion zone (EPZ) and those receiving products from inside or outside the EPZ.
5. Include provisions for accessing information about crop locations and harvesting operations in areas of concern.
6. Ensure that maps are regularly updated with current data, including changes in land use and crop status.
7. Coordinate with county or local agriculture extension offices to obtain real-time information on harvesting activities.

Notes:
• Maps are essential for tracking contamination levels and managing food safety risks.
• Regular updates ensure accurate planning and response efforts.
maps, data recording, ingestion doses, land use, GIS

Emergency Medical Care (1) -

Identify Primary and Backup Medical Facilities for Emergency Response
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Determine the Need for Medical Care: Identify the need for arranging medical care for both the general public and emergency workers at each NPP site.
2. Select a Primary Hospital/Medical Facility: Choose one primary local...
Procedure:

1. Determine the Need for Medical Care: Identify the need for arranging medical care for both the general public and emergency workers at each NPP site.
2. Select a Primary Hospital/Medical Facility: Choose one primary local hospital or medical facility that will be responsible for evaluating and treating potentially contaminated, injured, and/or exposed individuals.
3. Select a Backup Medical Facility: Identify one backup hospital or medical facility to serve as an alternative in case the primary facility is unavailable or overwhelmed.
4. Location Requirements:
- The primary and backup hospitals/medical facilities should be located at least 5 miles beyond the boundaries of the plume exposure pathway EPZ.
- Ideally, they should be located at least 10 miles outside the plume exposure pathway EPZ.
5. Consider Emergency Medical Transportation Services: Ensure that emergency medical transportation services (excluding normal EMS providers) are also available and located at a sufficient distance from the EPZ.
6. Agreements with Facilities: Include or reference written agreements with hospital/medical facilities and technical staff not employed by the facility to ensure cooperation during an emergency.
7. Refer for Additional Information: For more details on medical and public health written agreements, refer to evaluation criterion A.4.
medical facilities, backup hospital, emergency response, plume exposure pathway EPZ, medical agreements

Legal Framework (1) -

Understand and Apply the Stafford Act for Federal Disaster Response
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
The Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act is the statutory authority for most Federal disaster response activities, especially as they pertain to FEMA and its programs. This law amends the Disaster Relief Act of 1974...
The Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act is the statutory authority for most Federal disaster response activities, especially as they pertain to FEMA and its programs. This law amends the Disaster Relief Act of 1974 (Pub. L. 93-288) and was signed into law on November 23, 1988. It provides the legal basis for Federal disaster response efforts, including assistance provided through FEMA programs.

Key points:
• This Act is essential for understanding the scope of Federal involvement in disaster relief.
• It outlines the responsibilities and authorities of FEMA during emergency situations.
• Familiarity with this law is critical for local, state, and federal officials involved in disaster preparedness and response.
stafford act,federal disaster response,fema programs,disaster relief law

Communication (1) -

Recognize and Manage Rumors During Emergencies
Standard Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Rumors are pieces of information that may or may not be true, circulated by individuals or organizations during an emergency.

Key points:
• Rumors can spread quickly and cause confusion, fear, or panic among the public.
• It is important to...
Rumors are pieces of information that may or may not be true, circulated by individuals or organizations during an emergency.

Key points:
• Rumors can spread quickly and cause confusion, fear, or panic among the public.
• It is important to verify information through official sources before acting on it.
• Emergency managers should monitor and address rumors promptly to prevent misinformation from impacting response efforts.
rumors,emergency communication,misinformation,public safety

Emergency Protective Actions (1) -

Define and Understand the Concept of a Shelter-in-Place Order
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Shelter-in-place is a protective action that includes going indoors, closing all windows and doors, turning off heating and air conditioning systems using outside air, and listening to an EAS radio or television station for instructions.

**Key...
Shelter-in-place is a protective action that includes going indoors, closing all windows and doors, turning off heating and air conditioning systems using outside air, and listening to an EAS radio or television station for instructions.

Key points:
• This action is used when it is safer to remain inside than to evacuate due to external threats (e.g., chemical, biological, radiological hazards).
• It involves securing the building and minimizing exposure to hazardous materials.
• Individuals should stay informed through official media sources during this time.
shelter in place,protective action,emergency sheltering

Utility Coordination (1) -

Coordinate with Utilities During Emergency Response
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Establish communication with utility providers (electricity, water, gas) as soon as possible after an emergency.
2. Identify the type of utility disruption and its potential impact on public safety and infrastructure.
3....
Procedure:

1. Establish communication with utility providers (electricity, water, gas) as soon as possible after an emergency.
2. Identify the type of utility disruption and its potential impact on public safety and infrastructure.
3. Coordinate with local authorities to determine the scope of the utility issue and necessary actions for restoration.
4. Ensure that all coordination efforts are documented and shared with relevant FEMA teams and stakeholders.
5. Follow established protocols outlined in FEMA GM, Revision 1, RAC Coordination with Utilities for ongoing collaboration during emergency response operations.
FEMA, utilities, coordination, emergency response

Child Protection (1) -

Implement Protective Actions for School Children During Emergencies
Critical Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Develop and implement a protective action plan specifically tailored to school children during emergencies.
2. Ensure all staff, teachers, and emergency responders are trained on the procedures outlined in **GM EV-2, Protective...
Procedure:

1. Develop and implement a protective action plan specifically tailored to school children during emergencies.
2. Ensure all staff, teachers, and emergency responders are trained on the procedures outlined in GM EV-2, Protective Actions for School Children.
3. Identify safe zones within schools where students can be relocated if necessary (e.g., basements or interior rooms).
4. Coordinate with local authorities to ensure timely evacuation or shelter-in-place orders are communicated effectively to school personnel and parents.
5. Review and update the protective action plan annually, incorporating feedback from drills and real-world events.
school children, emergency protection, FEMA

Federal Response (1) -

Operate the Federal Response Center (FRC) During Emergencies
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Activate the Federal Response Center (FRC) as outlined in GM FR-1, Federal Response Center when a major emergency or disaster is declared.
2. Ensure that all personnel assigned to the FRC are trained and prepared for their...
Procedure:

1. Activate the Federal Response Center (FRC) as outlined in GM FR-1, Federal Response Center when a major emergency or disaster is declared.
2. Ensure that all personnel assigned to the FRC are trained and prepared for their roles during an activation.
3. Coordinate with federal agencies, state and local governments, and nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) through the FRC to manage resources and response efforts.
4. Maintain continuous communication between the FRC and on-the-ground emergency operations centers (EOCs).
5. Document all activities, decisions, and communications during the activation of the FRC for post-event analysis and reporting.
FRC, FEMA, emergency response

Exposure Pathways (1) -

Assess Ingestion Exposure Pathways During Emergencies
High Priority Source: FEMA REP Program Manual Dec 2019
Procedure:

1. Identify potential ingestion exposure pathways (e.g., contaminated food and water) during an emergency or disaster.
2. Conduct a risk assessment to determine the likelihood of exposure through these pathways.
3. Implement...
Procedure:

1. Identify potential ingestion exposure pathways (e.g., contaminated food and water) during an emergency or disaster.
2. Conduct a risk assessment to determine the likelihood of exposure through these pathways.
3. Implement mitigation strategies, such as distributing safe drinking water and issuing consumption advisories for affected food sources.
4. Coordinate with public health agencies to monitor and report cases of illness related to ingestion exposure.
5. Follow the guidelines provided in GM IN-1, The Ingestion Exposure Pathway to ensure a comprehensive approach to managing this risk.
ingestion exposure, FEMA, public health

Tidal Wave Preparedness (1) -

Prepare for Tidal Waves and Tsunamis
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand tidal wave risks:
- Tidal waves or tsunamis can cause massive destruction along coastlines and inland areas.
- These events are often triggered by underwater earthquakes, landslides, or volcanic eruptions.

2....
Procedure:

1. Understand tidal wave risks:
- Tidal waves or tsunamis can cause massive destruction along coastlines and inland areas.
- These events are often triggered by underwater earthquakes, landslides, or volcanic eruptions.

2. Create a tsunami preparedness plan:
- If you live in a coastal area, know the elevation of your home relative to sea level.
- Keep an emergency kit ready with flashlights, batteries, water, food, medications, and a battery-powered radio.
- Know the location of high ground or shelters in case you need to evacuate.

3. Stay informed:
- Monitor weather forecasts and alerts through radio, TV, or smartphone apps.
- Keep your emergency contact list updated and share it with family members.

4. During a tsunami:
- If you hear an alert or feel the ground shaking, move to higher ground immediately.
- Avoid driving through flooded areas or attempting to cross rivers or streams during the event.
- Stay away from coastal areas until officials declare it safe to return.

5. After a tsunami:
- Avoid entering damaged buildings until they are declared safe by authorities.
- Check for gas leaks, electrical hazards, or structural damage before re-entering your home.
- Disinfect contaminated areas with bleach and clean water to prevent the spread of disease.
tsunami, tidal wave, preparedness, emergency kit, evacuation plan

Volcanic Activity Preparedness (1) -

Prepare for Volcanic Activity and Eruptions
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand volcanic activity risks:
- Volcanic eruptions can cause widespread destruction through lava flows, ash falls, and pyroclastic flows.
- These events are often preceded by seismic activity or changes in gas...
Procedure:

1. Understand volcanic activity risks:
- Volcanic eruptions can cause widespread destruction through lava flows, ash falls, and pyroclastic flows.
- These events are often preceded by seismic activity or changes in gas emissions from the volcano.

2. Create a volcanic preparedness plan:
- If you live near an active volcano, know the evacuation routes and safe zones in your area.
- Keep an emergency kit ready with flashlights, batteries, water, food, medications, and a battery-powered radio.
- Have a supply of masks or respirators to protect against volcanic ash.

3. Stay informed:
- Monitor weather forecasts and alerts through radio, TV, or smartphone apps.
- Keep your emergency contact list updated and share it with family members.

4. During a volcanic eruption:
- If you hear an alert or feel the ground shaking, move to higher ground immediately.
- Avoid areas that are directly in the path of lava flows or pyroclastic flows.
- Stay indoors if possible and cover your nose and mouth with a mask or cloth to avoid inhaling ash.

5. After a volcanic eruption:
- Avoid entering damaged buildings until they are declared safe by authorities.
- Check for gas leaks, electrical hazards, or structural damage before re-entering your home.
- Disinfect contaminated areas with bleach and clean water to prevent the spread of disease.
volcanic_eruption, preparedness, emergency_kit, evacuation_plan

Drought Preparedness (1) -

Prepare for Drought and Water Scarcity
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand drought risks:
- Droughts can lead to water shortages, crop failures, and increased risk of wildfires.
- These events are often preceded by prolonged periods of low rainfall or high temperatures.

2. **Create...
Procedure:

1. Understand drought risks:
- Droughts can lead to water shortages, crop failures, and increased risk of wildfires.
- These events are often preceded by prolonged periods of low rainfall or high temperatures.

2. Create a drought preparedness plan:
- Store enough water for drinking, cooking, and sanitation purposes in case of extended dry periods.
- Keep an emergency kit ready with flashlights, batteries, water, food, medications, and a battery-powered radio.
- Have a supply of seeds or other materials to grow food if necessary.

3. Stay informed:
- Monitor weather forecasts and alerts through radio, TV, or smartphone apps.
- Keep your emergency contact list updated and share it with family members.

4. During a drought:
- Conserve water by using efficient irrigation methods for crops and reducing non-essential usage at home.
- Avoid overusing water resources and prioritize essential needs such as drinking and sanitation.
- Monitor local news for updates on water restrictions or conservation efforts.

5. After a drought:
- Check for any damage to infrastructure caused by the lack of water, such as cracked pipes or dry wells.
- Replenish water sources gradually to avoid overwhelming them with sudden demand.
- Continue monitoring weather patterns for signs of returning rainfall or potential flooding.
drought, water_scarcity, emergency_kit, conservation

Hazardous Materials Accidents (1) -

Identify and Respond to Hazardous Materials Accidents
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize the presence of hazardous materials:
- Be aware that trucks, trains, and other vehicles may be transporting hazardous substances such as chlorine, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), gasoline, liquefied natural gas...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the presence of hazardous materials:
- Be aware that trucks, trains, and other vehicles may be transporting hazardous substances such as chlorine, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), gasoline, liquefied natural gas (LNG), pesticides, high explosives, radioactive materials (including medical goods, power plant waste, fuel rods, and weapons-grade plutonium), and biological agents.
- Look for placards or labels on vehicles indicating the type of hazardous material being transported.

2. Assess the risk:
- If a vehicle carrying hazardous materials is involved in an accident, immediately move to a safe distance (upwind and uphill if possible).
- Avoid direct contact with spilled substances or contaminated floodwaters that may contain hazardous materials.

3. Monitor for signs of exposure:
- Watch for unusual odors, visible vapor clouds, or unexplained symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, or burning sensations in the eyes or throat.

4. Evacuate if necessary:
- If you are near a hazardous materials spill or accident and there is no immediate shelter available, evacuate the area immediately.
- Follow official evacuation routes and warnings from local authorities.

5. Report the incident:
- Call 911 or your local emergency services to report the accident and provide details about the type of hazardous material involved (if known).
- If you are near a flood, be aware that opened containers may contaminate water sources and should be reported immediately.

6. Stay informed:
- Monitor local news or official alerts for updates on the incident, including evacuation orders, safety precautions, and cleanup efforts.
hazardous materials, chemical spills, radiation, emergency response, evacuation

Road Accidents (1) -

Respond to Major Road Accidents and Collisions
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation:
- If involved in or near a major road accident (especially involving large trucks, multiple vehicles, or aircraft), ensure your own safety first by moving to a safe location away from traffic.

2....
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation:
- If involved in or near a major road accident (especially involving large trucks, multiple vehicles, or aircraft), ensure your own safety first by moving to a safe location away from traffic.

2. Check for injuries:
- Look for injured individuals and provide immediate assistance if you are trained to do so (e.g., stop bleeding, perform CPR).

3. Call 911 or local emergency services:
- Report the accident immediately, providing details such as location, number of vehicles involved, any injuries, and whether hazardous materials were spilled.

4. Avoid unnecessary movement near the scene:
- Stay clear of the accident zone to avoid obstructing rescue efforts or causing further harm.

5. Be prepared for detours and delays:
- Major accidents can cause significant traffic disruptions, so plan alternative routes if necessary.

6. Stay informed via local news or emergency alerts:
- Monitor updates regarding road closures, detours, and any potential hazards related to the accident.
traffic accidents, major collisions, emergency response, detour planning

Rail Accidents (1) -

Respond to Train Derailments and Rail Accidents
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize the risk of derailments:
- Be aware that rail systems are prone to accidents, especially at highway crossings or in areas with aging infrastructure.
- Look for signs of a derailment such as derailed cars,...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the risk of derailments:
- Be aware that rail systems are prone to accidents, especially at highway crossings or in areas with aging infrastructure.
- Look for signs of a derailment such as derailed cars, spilled materials, or unusual noises from the tracks.

2. Assess the situation and stay safe:
- If near a derailment involving hazardous materials, move to a safe distance (upwind and uphill if possible).
- Avoid direct contact with spilled substances or contaminated areas.

3. Report the incident immediately:
- Call 911 or local emergency services to report the derailment and provide details about the location, type of materials involved, and any injuries.

4. Evacuate if necessary:
- If you are in an area affected by a derailment involving hazardous materials, follow official evacuation orders.

5. Monitor for signs of exposure or contamination:
- Watch for unusual odors, visible vapor clouds, or unexplained symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, or burning sensations in the eyes or throat.

6. Stay informed via local news or emergency alerts:
- Monitor updates regarding the incident, including evacuation orders and safety precautions.
train derailment, rail accident, hazardous materials, emergency response

Structural Accidents (1) -

Respond to Structural Accidents and Collapses
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize the signs of structural accidents:
- Be aware of potential risks such as fires, building collapses, landslides affecting structures, or damage caused by vehicles, aircraft, or natural events.
- Look for warning...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the signs of structural accidents:
- Be aware of potential risks such as fires, building collapses, landslides affecting structures, or damage caused by vehicles, aircraft, or natural events.
- Look for warning signs like cracks in walls, unusual noises from buildings, or visible instability in large structures (e.g., cranes, storage tanks, radio/TV towers).

2. Ensure personal safety:
- If near a collapsing structure, immediately move to a safe location away from the building.
- Avoid entering damaged areas unless you are trained and equipped for search and rescue operations.

3. Report the incident:
- Call 911 or local emergency services to report the structural accident, providing details about the location, type of structure involved, and any injuries.

4. Evacuate if necessary:
- If you are in a building that is at risk of collapse or has already partially collapsed, follow official evacuation orders immediately.

5. Monitor for hazards such as fires or gas leaks:
- Be alert to secondary dangers like fire, electrical hazards, or gas leaks that may result from structural damage.

6. Stay informed via local news or emergency alerts:
- Monitor updates regarding the incident and any safety precautions related to the area.
building collapse, structural accidents, emergency response, evacuation

Nuclear Incidents (1) -

Prepare for and Respond to Nuclear Power Plant Incidents
Critical Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand the risks of nuclear power plants:
- Be aware that nuclear power plants can experience accidents (e.g., Chernobyl, Three Mile Island) or be targeted by terrorism.
- Know the location of nearby nuclear...
Procedure:

1. Understand the risks of nuclear power plants:
- Be aware that nuclear power plants can experience accidents (e.g., Chernobyl, Three Mile Island) or be targeted by terrorism.
- Know the location of nearby nuclear facilities and understand potential evacuation routes in case of an incident.

2. Monitor for signs of a nuclear emergency:
- Look for official alerts from local authorities or emergency management agencies regarding radiation releases or plant incidents.
- Be alert to unusual odors, visible vapor clouds, or unexplained symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, or burning sensations in the eyes or throat.

3. Evacuate if necessary:
- If an evacuation order is issued due to a nuclear incident, follow it immediately and move to a safe location away from the affected area.

4. Stay informed via local news or emergency alerts:
- Monitor updates regarding radiation levels, evacuation orders, and safety precautions.

5. Avoid exposure to radiation:
- If you are in an area affected by a nuclear incident, avoid contact with contaminated materials or water sources.
- Follow decontamination procedures if exposed (e.g., washing off skin, changing clothes).

6. Report any suspicious activity near nuclear facilities:
- If you notice anything unusual at a nuclear power plant (e.g., unauthorized access, strange behavior), report it immediately to local authorities or the facility itself.
nuclear accident, radiation exposure, emergency response, evacuation

Blast Effects (1) -

Assessing Blast Damage to Vehicles Based on Pressure Levels
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure for Assessing Blast Damage to Vehicles Based on Pressure Levels:

1. Identify the type of vehicle: Determine what kind of vehicle you are assessing (e.g., auto, bus, fire truck, etc.).
2. Determine blast pressure levels:...
Procedure for Assessing Blast Damage to Vehicles Based on Pressure Levels:

1. Identify the type of vehicle: Determine what kind of vehicle you are assessing (e.g., auto, bus, fire truck, etc.).
2. Determine blast pressure levels: Measure or estimate the blast pressure in psi (pounds per square inch) experienced by the vehicle.
3. Evaluate damage based on pressure ranges:
- Auto:
- 3–5 psi: Moderate damage (requires a few hours to repair).
- 5–6 psi: Inoperable.
- Buses, Fire trucks, Repair trucks:
- 6–10 psi: Moderate damage (requires a few hours to repair).
- 10–12 psi: Inoperable.
- Earth movers:
- 20–30 psi: Moderate damage.
- 30–35 psi: Inoperable.
- Truck mounted engineer equipment:
- 12–15 psi: Moderate damage.
- 15–17 psi: Inoperable.
- Railroad cars:
- 15 psi: Moderate damage.
- 25 psi: Inoperable.
- Locomotives:
- 30 psi: Moderate damage.
- 80 psi: Inoperable.
4. Document the assessment: Record the type of vehicle, blast pressure levels, and observed damage for future reference or repair planning.
blast effects, vehicle damage, psi, moderate damage, inoperable

Chemical Biological Warfare (1) -

Understanding Chemical/Biological Warfare Effects on Humans and the Environment
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure for Understanding Chemical/Biological Warfare Effects:

1. Recognize the types of agents: Understand that chemical agents can range from simple defoliants to toxic gases, while biological agents are often modified strains of...
Procedure for Understanding Chemical/Biological Warfare Effects:

1. Recognize the types of agents: Understand that chemical agents can range from simple defoliants to toxic gases, while biological agents are often modified strains of disease-causing organisms (e.g., botulism, typhoid, black plague).
2. Identify primary effects:
- Contaminated air, water, or food.
- Direct exposure to the agent.
3. Assess health impacts: Exposure can lead to severe illness and overwhelm medical facilities due to mass casualties.
4. Evaluate impact on food supply: If agents are used against food animals or plants, a shortage of food is likely to occur.
5. Consider social and economic consequences:
- Contagious diseases may cause people to avoid contact, reducing manpower in businesses, government, and services.
6. Research further information: Use major search engines for additional details on chemical and biological weapons beyond what is covered in this manual.
chemical warfare, biological warfare, health effects, food shortage, medical facilities

Warfare Effects (1) -

Identify Conventional Warfare Effects on Civilian Populations
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize Bombing and Artillery Impact:
- Understand that bombing and artillery fire can cause widespread destruction of cities and infrastructure.
- Be aware that these effects are often the most devastating in...
Procedure:

1. Recognize Bombing and Artillery Impact:
- Understand that bombing and artillery fire can cause widespread destruction of cities and infrastructure.
- Be aware that these effects are often the most devastating in conventional warfare.

2. Assess Small Arms Fire Risk:
- Note that while stray small arms fire is a hazard, it is generally less severe than large-scale bombings or artillery.

3. Be Aware of Atrocities by Individual Soldiers:
- Recognize that individual soldiers may commit atrocities based on personal biases toward ideologies, races, religions, or other groups.
- Understand that these actions can lead to long-term psychological and social trauma for affected populations.

4. Identify Genocide and Ethnic Cleansing:
- Know that when the intent of war is to destroy a specific civilian population, it may be classified as genocide.
- Recognize that when the intent is to force relocation, it is known as ethnic cleansing.

5. Be Cautious of Looting and Foraging:
- Understand that soldiers may loot or forage for supplies even in the absence of genocide or ethnic cleansing.
- Be aware that this can lead to the impoverishment and starvation of civilian populations.

6. Prepare for Refugee Crises:
- Recognize that areas not directly involved in fighting may still be affected by an influx of refugees fleeing conflict zones.
- Understand that refugee crises can strain local resources and infrastructure.

7. Anticipate Disruption of Normal Procedures:
- Be prepared for the disruption of normal daily routines due to war priorities taking precedence over civilian needs.
- Expect shortages, rationing, and the shutdown or conversion of non-essential services and jobs to support the war effort.

8. Understand Civil War Effects:
- Recognize that in civil wars, similar effects occur but may be more intense in terms of atrocities due to internal conflict.
- Note that combat areas are often smaller, and areas outside the fighting zone may experience less direct impact.

9. Be Alert to Terrorist Threats:
- Understand that in terrorist attacks, the primary threats include small arms fire and homemade bombs.
- Be prepared for disruptions to local utilities and services.
- Recognize that if terrorists obtain nuclear, biological, or chemical (NBC) weapons, these effects will become a major concern.
conventional warfare,bombing,artillery fire,genocide,ethnic cleansing,civil war,terrorist activity,NBC weapons

General Preparation (1) -

Prepare for Natural Disasters by Understanding Common Risks
Standard Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Natural disasters such as tornadoes, hurricanes, floods, earthquakes, and lightning can cause significant damage to homes, businesses, and infrastructure.

### Key Points:
• Tornadoes: Can destroy buildings due to high winds (50–250 mph) and...
Natural disasters such as tornadoes, hurricanes, floods, earthquakes, and lightning can cause significant damage to homes, businesses, and infrastructure.

### Key Points:
• Tornadoes: Can destroy buildings due to high winds (50–250 mph) and wind-propelled debris like 2 x 4s or sheet metal. Poor construction practices increase vulnerability.
• Hurricanes: Cause destruction through storm surges, flooding, and powerful winds that can strip roofs and create flying debris. They may also spawn tornadoes.
• Floods: Can ruin home contents and contaminate water supplies from sewers, chemical sites, fuel sources, or dead bodies. Even simple contact with contaminated water can cause health issues.
• Earthquakes: Often damage above-ground structures, leading to buildings collapsing into basements. Poor foundations and lack of bracing increase risk.
• Lightning: Can destroy electrical and electronic equipment, start fires, and strike antennas more frequently than CB or TV antennas.

### Recommendations:
• Understand the risks in your area and prepare accordingly.
• Ensure homes are built with strong foundations and proper bracing to withstand natural disasters.
natural disaster preparation,tornado,hurricane,flood,earthquake,lightning,risk assessment

Flood Preparation (1) -

Protect Against Water Contamination During Floods
High Priority Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Floodwaters can contaminate water supplies, posing serious health risks even without drinking the water.

### Steps to Protect Yourself:
• Avoid contact with floodwater: Even brief exposure can lead to illness from contaminants like sewage,...
Floodwaters can contaminate water supplies, posing serious health risks even without drinking the water.

### Steps to Protect Yourself:
• Avoid contact with floodwater: Even brief exposure can lead to illness from contaminants like sewage, chemicals, fuel, or dead bodies (animal and human).
• Use safe water sources: Ensure access to clean, uncontaminated water for drinking, cooking, and hygiene.
• Disinfect water if necessary: If no safe water is available, boil it for at least 1 minute or use appropriate water purification tablets.
• Avoid using contaminated water for any purpose, including washing hands or cleaning wounds.

### Additional Precautions:
• Store clean water in sealed containers before a flood occurs.
• Monitor local authorities for updates on water safety and contamination risks.
water contamination,flood safety,disinfection,washing hands,safe water storage

Utility Failure Preparation (1) -

Prepare for Post-Disaster Utility Failures and Social Disruption
Standard Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Natural disasters often lead to utility failures, which can cause significant social disruption.

### Steps to Prepare:
1. Have backup power sources: Include generators, solar panels, or battery-powered devices for lighting, heating, and...
Natural disasters often lead to utility failures, which can cause significant social disruption.

### Steps to Prepare:
1. Have backup power sources: Include generators, solar panels, or battery-powered devices for lighting, heating, and communication during outages.
2. Store emergency supplies: Keep a supply of non-perishable food, water, medications, flashlights, batteries, and first-aid kits in an easily accessible location.
3. Plan for alternative communication methods: Use radios or satellite phones if traditional phone lines are down.
4. Stay informed: Monitor local news and emergency alerts to stay updated on utility outages and recovery efforts.
5. Prepare for social disruption: Be ready for potential shortages of food, water, and medical supplies in the aftermath of a disaster.
utility failure,emergency power,social disruption,backup supplies,communication plans

Food Security Preparation (1) -

Understand the Impact of Natural Disasters on Crops and Food Supplies
Standard Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Natural disasters can damage or destroy crops, leading to food shortages for both humans and animals.

### Steps to Mitigate Crop Loss:
• Diversify crop types: Grow a variety of crops that are less susceptible to different disaster...
Natural disasters can damage or destroy crops, leading to food shortages for both humans and animals.

### Steps to Mitigate Crop Loss:
• Diversify crop types: Grow a variety of crops that are less susceptible to different disaster conditions (e.g., drought-resistant plants, flood-tolerant species).
• Use protective measures: Install irrigation systems, raised beds, or greenhouses to protect crops from extreme weather.
• Store food reserves: Keep non-perishable food supplies in a secure location for emergencies.
• Monitor local agricultural advisories: Stay informed about potential crop damage and food shortages due to natural disasters.

### Consequences of Crop Damage:
• Reduced availability of food, leading to malnutrition or hunger.
• Increased prices for essential goods.
• Disruption of livestock feed supplies, affecting animal health and productivity.
crop damage,food security,crop diversity,emergency food storage,agricultural advisories

Home Preparation (1) -

Prepare for Severe Weather and Extreme Comfort Issues in Homes
Standard Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Poorly insulated homes can be uncomfortable or dangerous during extreme weather, such as severe cold or heat.

### Steps to Improve Home Insulation:
1. Install proper insulation: Use materials like fiberglass, foam board, or spray foam to...
Poorly insulated homes can be uncomfortable or dangerous during extreme weather, such as severe cold or heat.

### Steps to Improve Home Insulation:
1. Install proper insulation: Use materials like fiberglass, foam board, or spray foam to insulate walls, floors, and ceilings.
2. Seal air leaks: Use caulk or weatherstripping to prevent drafts from entering the home.
3. Use a good air filtration system: Helps filter out dust, pollen, and other airborne particles during extreme weather events or pestilence outbreaks.
4. Consider building materials: Choose weatherproofed homes with strong foundations that can withstand natural disasters.

### Benefits of Proper Insulation:
• Maintains indoor comfort during extreme temperatures.
• Reduces the risk of health issues for people with respiratory conditions (e.g., asthma, allergies).
• Improves energy efficiency and reduces utility costs.
home insulation,air filtration,extreme weather,weatherproofing,respiratory health

Agricultural Impact (1) -

Understand the Effects of Severe Weather on Crops and Agriculture
Standard Source: FRC Preparedness Handbook
Severe or prolonged extreme weather can damage crops, leading to food shortages.

### Common Crop Damages:
• Frosts and freezes: Can kill plants or reduce yields.
• High heat: May cause wilting, reduced growth, or crop failure.
-...
Severe or prolonged extreme weather can damage crops, leading to food shortages.

### Common Crop Damages:
• Frosts and freezes: Can kill plants or reduce yields.
• High heat: May cause wilting, reduced growth, or crop failure.
• Droughts: Lead to water stress, lower yields, and potential crop death.
• Sand/dust storms: Can bury crops or damage plant structures.

### Steps to Mitigate Crop Damage:
1. Use irrigation systems: Help maintain moisture levels during droughts.
2. Plant frost-resistant crops: Choose varieties that can survive cold temperatures.
3. Protect against sand/dust storms: Use windbreaks, cover crops, or greenhouses.
4. Monitor weather forecasts: Stay informed about potential extreme weather events and take preventive measures.

### Consequences of Crop Damage:
• Food shortages for both humans and animals.
• Economic losses for farmers and agricultural businesses.
crop damage,frost resistance,irrigation,drought protection,sand storm mitigation

Winter Preparedness (1) -

Assess Ramifications of Severe Winter Weather Outage
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Step 1: Identify the potential effects of a severe winter weather event with an electrical power outage lasting between 4 hours (average) to 72 hours (severe).

Ramifications include:
• Furnace operation affected, leading to cold indoor...
Step 1: Identify the potential effects of a severe winter weather event with an electrical power outage lasting between 4 hours (average) to 72 hours (severe).

Ramifications include:
• Furnace operation affected, leading to cold indoor temperatures.
• Exposure problems due to cold and lack of heating.
• Frozen pipes could cause water damage or burst, leading to flooding.
• Disruption of travel and transportation, potentially stranding individuals away from home.
• Possible food shortages as local markets may be empty due to supply chain disruptions.
winter weather,electricity outage,frozen pipes,cold exposure

Transportation Risk (1) -

Assess Ramifications of Nearby Train Derailment
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Step 1: Identify the potential effects of a nearby train derailment.

Ramifications include:
• Possible leak or spill of chemicals, leading to environmental contamination and health risks.
• Short-term exposure problems from chemical...
Step 1: Identify the potential effects of a nearby train derailment.

Ramifications include:
• Possible leak or spill of chemicals, leading to environmental contamination and health risks.
• Short-term exposure problems from chemical fumes or debris.
• Long-term cancer concerns due to prolonged exposure to hazardous materials.
• Evacuation may be necessary if the spill poses an immediate threat to life.
train derailment,chemical spill,cancer risk,evacuation

Building Explosion (1) -

Evacuate During a Building Explosion
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

1. Get out of the building as quickly and calmly as possible.
2. If items are falling from bookshelves or the ceiling, get under a sturdy table or desk for protection.
3. If there is a fire:
- Stay low to the floor and...
Procedure:

1. Get out of the building as quickly and calmly as possible.
2. If items are falling from bookshelves or the ceiling, get under a sturdy table or desk for protection.
3. If there is a fire:
- Stay low to the floor and exit the building as quickly as possible.
- Cover your nose and mouth with a damp cloth to filter out smoke and toxic gases.
- When approaching a closed door, use the palm of your hand and forearm to feel the lower, middle, and upper parts of the door.
- If it is not hot, brace yourself against the door and open it slowly.
- If it is hot to the touch, do not open the door—seek an alternate escape route.
- Heavy smoke and poisonous gases collect first along the ceiling. Stay below the smoke at all times.
explosion, evacuation, fire, smoke, building safety

Chemical Agents (1) -

Chemical Agent Exposure Response
Critical Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

• Chemical agents are poisonous gases, liquids, or solids that can cause serious injury or death.
• The severity of injuries depends on the type and amount of chemical agent used, as well as the duration of exposure.
• If a...
Procedure:

• Chemical agents are poisonous gases, liquids, or solids that can cause serious injury or death.
• The severity of injuries depends on the type and amount of chemical agent used, as well as the duration of exposure.
• If a chemical agent attack occurs:
- Authorities will instruct citizens to either seek shelter where they are and seal the premises or evacuate immediately.
- Exposure to chemical agents can be fatal.
- Leaving the shelter to rescue or assist victims is a deadly decision.
- There is no assistance that untrained individuals can offer that would likely be of any value to the victims of chemical agents.
• Untrained persons should not attempt to rescue people inside a collapsed building. Wait for emergency personnel to arrive.
chemical agents, exposure response, emergency personnel

Emergency Preparation (1) -

Prepare for Natural Disasters by Stocking Emergency Supplies
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Action: Ensure you have a well-stocked emergency kit ready for natural disasters.

Steps:
1. Include items such as blankets, pillows, and warm clothing in case of sudden displacement or cold exposure.
2. Store non-perishable food, water (at...
Action: Ensure you have a well-stocked emergency kit ready for natural disasters.

Steps:
1. Include items such as blankets, pillows, and warm clothing in case of sudden displacement or cold exposure.
2. Store non-perishable food, water (at least one gallon per person per day), and a manual can opener.
3. Keep flashlights, batteries, and a first aid kit readily available.
4. Include medications, personal hygiene items, and important documents in waterproof containers.
5. Ensure you have a portable radio to receive emergency broadcasts.
6. Store cash in case electronic payment systems fail during the disaster.

Details:
• Emergency kits should be easily accessible and stored in multiple locations (e.g., home, car, workplace).
• Consider including items like duct tape, plastic sheeting, and a whistle for signaling help.
• Regularly check expiration dates on food and medications and replace as needed.
emergency supplies,natural disasters,disaster preparedness,stocking kits

Hurricanes Flooding (1) -

Respond to Hurricanes and Flooding
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Action: Take immediate steps during a hurricane or flooding event.

Steps:
1. Monitor weather forecasts for hurricane warnings and evacuate if advised by local authorities.
2. If evacuation is necessary, take only essential items (e.g.,...
Action: Take immediate steps during a hurricane or flooding event.

Steps:
1. Monitor weather forecasts for hurricane warnings and evacuate if advised by local authorities.
2. If evacuation is necessary, take only essential items (e.g., medications, documents, emergency supplies) and leave immediately.
3. Secure your home by boarding up windows, bringing in outdoor furniture, and turning off gas and electricity if instructed to do so.
4. Stay away from flooded areas; never attempt to walk or drive through floodwaters.
5. If you are caught in a flood, move to higher ground and avoid contact with water that may be contaminated or electrified.
6. After the storm has passed, use caution when returning home and check for damage before entering any building.

Details:
• Keep a portable radio handy to receive updates from emergency management officials.
• If you are in an area prone to flooding, consider elevating your home or installing flood barriers.
• Avoid using electrical appliances if there is standing water near them to prevent electrocution.
hurricane survival,flood response,storm preparedness

Flash Floods (1) -

Prepare for Flash Floods and Water Emergencies
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Action: Take steps to prepare for flash floods and water-related emergencies.

Steps:
1. Stay informed about weather forecasts and flood warnings in your area.
2. Avoid building or living in low-lying areas that are prone to flooding.
3. If...
Action: Take steps to prepare for flash floods and water-related emergencies.

Steps:
1. Stay informed about weather forecasts and flood warnings in your area.
2. Avoid building or living in low-lying areas that are prone to flooding.
3. If you live in a high-risk flood zone, consider purchasing flood insurance.
4. Keep emergency supplies on hand, including food, water, flashlights, batteries, and first aid kits.
5. If you are caught in a flash flood, move to higher ground immediately and avoid walking or driving through flooded areas.
6. If your home is flooded, turn off the electricity at the main breaker box if it is safe to do so.

Details:
• Flash floods can occur with little warning, so being prepared is essential.
• Avoid using electrical devices during a flood unless you are certain that they are not in contact with water.
• If you are trapped by rising waters, use a whistle or flashlight to signal for help.
flash flood survival,flood preparedness,water emergencies

Wildfires (1) -

Respond to Wildfires and Fire Emergencies
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Action: Take immediate steps during a wildfire or fire emergency.

Steps:
1. Monitor weather forecasts for fire warnings and evacuate if advised by local authorities.
2. If evacuation is necessary, take only essential items (e.g.,...
Action: Take immediate steps during a wildfire or fire emergency.

Steps:
1. Monitor weather forecasts for fire warnings and evacuate if advised by local authorities.
2. If evacuation is necessary, take only essential items (e.g., medications, documents, emergency supplies) and leave immediately.
3. Keep an emergency kit with food, water, flashlights, batteries, and first aid supplies in a safe place.
4. If you are caught in a wildfire, move to higher ground and avoid low-lying areas that can trap heat and smoke.
5. Stay away from fire lines and do not attempt to put out the fire yourself unless it is safe to do so.
6. After the fire has passed, be cautious of falling debris and potential hazards before returning home.

Details:
• Wildfires can spread quickly, so being prepared in advance is crucial.
• If you are in a high-risk area, consider purchasing fire insurance and creating defensible space around your home.
• Avoid using electrical appliances if there is smoke or heat damage to the building.
wildfire survival,fire emergency,response plan

Volcanic Eruption Impact (1) -

Understanding the Impact of Major Volcanic Eruptions on Global Climate
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Recognize historical impacts: Study major volcanic eruptions such as Krakatoa (1883) and Mt. Tambora (1815), which injected significant amounts of dust into the atmosphere, reducing sunlight by up to 20%.
2. **Monitor...
Procedure:

1. Recognize historical impacts: Study major volcanic eruptions such as Krakatoa (1883) and Mt. Tambora (1815), which injected significant amounts of dust into the atmosphere, reducing sunlight by up to 20%.
2. Monitor atmospheric changes: Be aware that large eruptions can lead to global temperature drops lasting one to three years.
3. Prepare for climate anomalies: Plan for potential agricultural disruptions and food shortages due to reduced sunlight and colder temperatures.
4. Stay informed about volcanic activity: Track volcanic monitoring reports, especially if you live near active volcanoes or in regions affected by distant eruptions.
5. Understand ash fall effects: Volcanic ash can cause heavy rainfall, mudflows (lahars), and toxic gases that may impact air quality and health.
6. Plan for long-term consequences: Consider the possibility of extended cold periods and their effect on food production, energy consumption, and overall survival.
volcano,eruption,climate change,ash fall,temperature drop

Volcanic Survival (1) -

Surviving Proximity to an Active Volcano
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Monitor volcanic activity: Keep track of seismic activity and signs of potential eruptions, such as increased tremors or gas emissions.
2. Evacuate immediately if necessary: If you live near an active volcano, be prepared...
Procedure:

1. Monitor volcanic activity: Keep track of seismic activity and signs of potential eruptions, such as increased tremors or gas emissions.
2. Evacuate immediately if necessary: If you live near an active volcano, be prepared to leave quickly in the event of an eruption warning.
3. Avoid lava flows: Stay away from molten lava, which can destroy everything it touches and is extremely hot (up to 1,000°C or more).
4. Protect against volcanic ash: Wear protective masks or respirators to avoid inhaling toxic gases and fine ash particles that can damage lungs.
5. Be aware of lahars: Prepare for heavy rains that may mix with volcanic ash to form fast-moving mudflows capable of destroying homes and infrastructure.
6. Secure your home: Reinforce windows, doors, and roofs to withstand potential debris or falling rocks from eruptions.
7. Have an emergency plan: Include a detailed evacuation route and meeting point for family members in case of sudden volcanic activity.
volcano,lahar,lava flow,ash protection,evacuation plan

Resource Depletion (1) -

Understanding the Depletion of Natural Resources and Its Historical Impact
High Priority Source: Life After Doomsday
Understanding the Depletion of Natural Resources and Its Historical Impact
